parse.why: HCGPE is used as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Reported intrinsic electrolyte metrics for HCGPE include: ionic conductivity of 4.8 mS cm^-1 at 30°C (EIS, Figures S13–S14); Li-ion transference number tLi+ = 0.56 (Figure S15); activation energy 0.16 eV (Arrhenius analysis, Figure S14); LSV shows an oxidation peak at ≈3.9 V and improved oxidation stability vs HCE (Figure 1h); exchange current density 0.518 mA cm^-2 for Li plating/stripping (Tafel, Figure S22). Performance metrics involving HCGPE-PE or HCGPE-PP pertain to composites with separators and are not attributed here to HCGPE alone.

HCE-specific evidence: (1) Coulombic efficiency: “both HCGPE-PE and HCE exhibit high CE values of ≈ 99.3%.” (2) Symmetric Li/Li lifetime: “the symmetric battery with HCE experiences a short circuit after ≈ 740 h.” Additional HCE metrics supporting application context: tLi+ = 0.48 (“the tLi+ of HCGPE (0.56) is slightly higher than the HCE (0.48)”); activation energy Ea = 0.18 eV (“the activation energy of the HCGPE (0.16 eV) is lower than that of the HCE (0.18 eV)”); exchange current density = 0.984 mA cm−2 (“HCGPE shows a significantly lower exchange current density (0.518 mA cm−2) compared to HCE (0.984 mA cm−2)”).

“Subsequently, the LATP and LAWTP powders were pressed into green pellets and then sintered to form solid electrolytes.” “The LAWTP sample has a room temperature ionic conductivity of 1.07 mS / cm, which is higher than that of the LATP electrolyte (0.38 mS / cm).”

PVDF-HFP-SN (PVDF-HFP + LITFSI with 20 wt% SN; 0 g LAWTP) is used as a solid electrolyte for Li-ion batteries. Measured properties for PVDF-HFP-SN: ionic conductivity 1.07e-5 S/cm at 30 °C and 3.52e-5 S/cm at 50 °C (Fig. 4b); electrochemical stability window 4.7 V (Fig. 4e); Li-ion transference number tLi+ = 0.296 at 50 °C with I0=42.19 μA, Iss=19.15 μA, R0=116.2 Ω, Rss=128.1 Ω (Table 2; Fig. 6a,b). Full-cell performance (LFP/PVDF-HFP-SN/Li quasi-solid-state): at 50 °C and 0.5 C, initial discharge capacity 144.12 mAh/g, 127.62 mAh/g after 200 cycles (88.55% retention) (Fig. 8a,c); at room temperature and 0.5 C, initial 128.35 mAh/g, 114.30 mAh/g after 500 cycles (89.05% retention) (Fig. 9a,c).

Sample defined: “The SCEs prepared using 0, 0.05, 0.1, and 0.2 g LATWP were denoted as PVDF-HFP-SN, PVDF-HFP-SN-0.05LAWTP, PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP and PVDF-HFP-SN-0.2LAWTP, respectively.” The study context and tests indicate these are solid composite electrolytes for lithium-ion batteries (e.g., assembly of Li/SCE/Li and LiFePO4/SCE/Li cells). However, numeric electrochemical metrics in the Results (ionic conductivity, stability window, tLi+, cycling) are explicitly reported for PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP (and/or PVDF-HFP-SN), not for PVDF-HFP-SN-0.05LAWTP, so no specific performance values can be extracted for this material.

PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP solid composite electrolyte shows the optimum ionic conductivities of 3.03×10^-5 S/cm at 30°C and 1×10^-4 S/cm at 50°C (Fig. 4a–b). Its electrochemical stability window (decomposition voltage) is 4.8 V (Fig. 4e). The Li-ion transference number tLi+ is 0.478 measured in Li/SCE/Li cells (Table 2). Li/PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP/Li symmetric cells exhibit lower and stable polarization and operate for 1000 cycles at 0.1 mA/cm^2 without short circuit (Fig. 7). In full cells (LFP/PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP/Li), at 0.5C and 50°C: initial 158.06 mAh/g, 133.82 mAh/g after 200 cycles (84.66% retention); at room temperature 0.5C: 142.69→136.41 mAh/g after 500 cycles (95.60% retention), CE >99% after the first cycle (Fig. 8–9). With 5 μL liquid electrolyte infiltration, ionic conductivity increases to 3.64×10^-4 S/cm (30°C) and 7×10^-4 S/cm (50°C), and stability window ~4.5 V (Fig. 5).

Identification: “The SCEs prepared using 0, 0.05, 0.1, and 0.2 g LATWP were denoted as PVDF-HFP-SN, PVDF-HFP-SN-0.05LAWTP, PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP and PVDF-HFP-SN-0.2LAWTP, respectively.” Performance context (trend only, no numeric for 0.2 g): “As displayed in Fig. 4b, the ionic conductivity initially increases and then decreases with increasing LAWTP content.” Detailed numeric values (ionic conductivity, electrochemical stability window, tLi+) are reported for the 0.1LAWTP sample, not for PVDF-HFP-SN-0.2LAWTP; hence metrics are left null for this material.

Neat [HMG][FSI] shows a very wide electrochemical window (~8 V) at 75 °C with a Pt working electrode: cathodic limit about −4 V and anodic limit about +4 V vs Pt quasi reference (Fig. 7a). No numeric ionic conductivity or Li+ transference number is reported for the neat [HMG][FSI]; such values are given only for mixtures with Li salts.

The paper frames PIL-based polymeric systems, including the solid binary PMMA–PIL blends such as (PMMA)0.91(IM14)0.09, as promising candidates for solid polymeric electrolytes in LIBs (“we focused on protic ionic liquids (PILs) as promising candidates for solid polymeric electrolytes in LIBs. The study examined two types of PIL-based polymeric systems: solid binary polymeric systems based on PMMA and PILs, and IM14-gel-like systems”). However, no ionic conductivity or electrochemical performance metrics are reported for (PMMA)0.91(IM14)0.09; only DSC/TGA/ssNMR data are provided. Diffusion and electrolyte-oriented measurements are presented for IM14 gel-like systems, not for this specific solid binary sample.

The solid samples, referred to as (PMMA)0.91-(IM14)0.09, (PMMA)0.91-(TFSI)0.09, and (PMMA)0.91-(TFO)0.09, were prepared... Thermal analysis and solid-state NMR (ssNMR) were employed to comprehensively characterize the solid samples. Conclusions: In this manuscript, we focused on protic ionic liquids (PILs) as promising candidates for solid polymeric electrolytes in LIBs... Solid-state NMR studies demonstrated the retention of PIL mobility within the polymeric matrix, indicating the potential for PIL-based polymeric electrolytes with enhanced ion mobility. No ionic conductivity or battery performance metrics were reported for (PMMA)0.91-(TFSI)0.09 specifically.

The paper includes (PMMA)0.91-(TFO)0.09 as one of the solid binary polymeric systems ("The solid samples, referred to as ... (PMMA)0.91(TFO)0.09, were prepared..."). For this specific sample, only DSC/TGA material properties are reported (e.g., Tg = 57 °C; degradation stages and an initial 220 °C event due to contaminants; Table 1), and no application performance metrics (e.g., ionic conductivity, electrochemical cycling, selectivity, capacity, breakthrough, or regeneration) are provided. Application-related diffusion and electrolyte discussions and data are focused on IM14 gel-like systems with/without Li+, not on the TFO solid sample.

The work evaluates IM14-gel-like systems as polymeric electrolytes for energy storage, explicitly measuring ion transport in (DBUH-IM14)0.60-(PMMA)0.40. Text: 'considering the potential application of gels as safer electrolytes for energy storage devices... The self-diffusion coefficients of the (DBUH-IM14)0.60-(PMMA)0.40 ... are presented in Fig. 7 and Table 2 at three different temperatures.' Table 2 reports PFG-NMR self-diffusion coefficients for DBUH+ and IM14− in (DBUH-IM14)0.60-(PMMA)0.40 at 40, 55, and 70 °C, evidencing reduced ion mobility due to PMMA confinement. The Conclusions frame these PIL–PMMA systems as promising solid polymeric electrolytes for LIBs.

The work positions IM14-gel-like systems as candidates for safer polymeric electrolytes in LIBs. The specific gel-like sample (DBUH-IM14)0.55-(Li)0.06-(PMMA)0.39 was prepared and studied for ion transport via PFG NMR. Table 2 reports self-diffusion coefficients at 40/55/70 °C for DBUH+ and IM14− in this sample: DDBUH+ = 2.01E-12, 4.90E-12, 1.16E-11 m2 s-1; DIM14− = 1.18E-12, 3.14E-12, 6.98E-12 m2 s-1. The Li+ self-diffusion coefficient in this sample could not be measured due to very short T2. The text explicitly motivates these gel-like systems as potential electrolytes for energy storage devices and LIBs.

No application/performance testing was reported for the monomer Pentafluorophenylacrylate (M1). The paper focuses on synthesis, characterization, and use of M1 as a precursor to polymers (PM1/PM2), with reactivity and kinetic data pertaining to the polymers, not M1. Relevant text: “Pentafluorophenylacrylate and -methacrylate, M1 and M2… They were soluble in many solvents… stable, not air sensitive and relatively robust against hydrolysis in air… Preparation in a large scale was possible and even after storage at 10 °C under nitrogen over several weeks no signs of decomposition could be detected.” The quantitative conversions, rate constants, and FT-IR kinetics are for PM1/PM3, not for M1.

No application/performance data are reported for the monomer M2 (pentafluorophenylmethacrylate). Reported conversions/kinetics pertain to polymers (e.g., PM1–PM4; PM2 = poly(pentafluorophenylmethacrylate). M2 is only described in synthesis and as stable/soluble; no catalysis, separation, or similar application metrics are provided.

Conclusions: "primary amines reacted with 100% conversion with PM1" under comparable conditions. FT-IR (Fig. 2 text): for PM1 + primary amines, the active-ester carbonyl peak vanished completely after 1 h at 50 °C (i.e., 100% conversion). Table 1 (NMR, 50 °C, 24 h, DMF) for PM1: hexylamine 99%; N-hexylmethylamine 80%; aniline 14%; 1-hexanol + triethylamine 30% (1 eq each) and 60% (7 eq each). Table 2 (kinetics in THF, 50 °C) for PM1: hexylamine k = 1.85×10^-3 s^-1, τ1/2 = 6 min; N-hexylmethylamine k = 4.67×10^-4 s^-1, τ1/2 = 25 min; 1-hexanol + triethylamine k = 2.5×10^-5 s^-1, τ1/2 = 462 min. These directly evidence PM1’s high reactivity toward primary amines in polymer-analogous functionalization.

Table 1 (Section 3.3.1) reports PM2 conversions after 24 h at 50 °C in DMF: with hexylamine (1 equivalent), PM2 = 65% conversion. Under the same conditions PM2 shows 65% with N-hexylmethylamine, 0% with aniline, and 0% with 1-hexanol + triethylamine (both at 1 eq and at 7 eq each).

PFPE_E10-DMC is studied as a battery electrolyte: “Keywords: Perfluoropolyether Electrolyte Battery” and “LiTFSI salt was dissolved in the PFPE polymers to form an electrolyte.” Table 2 explicitly reports for PFPE10-DMC the maximum [LiTFSI] as 31% (wt%), 2.70 (mol L-1), r = 0.16, with Td(5%) = 194 °C and Tg = −92 °C. These values directly correspond to the PFPE_E10-DMC sample (Fluorolink E10 with DMC endgroups).

The morphologies of two representative solid electrolytes, XL-PFPE_0.40 and XL-POSS0.13PFPE0.35 were determined by STEM. STEM image of XL-POSS0.12-PFPE_0.35 shows some heterogeneity... sulfur, which indicates the location of TFSI− anions, is seen in the dark PFPE domains, suggesting that the salt is primarily solvated by the fluorinated PFPE chains.

XL-PFPE_wLiTFSI electrolytes are UV-activated crosslinked solids with wLiTFSI between 0 and 0.5, so XL-PFPE_0.40 is a defined sample: “The UV activated crosslinked electrolytes are labelled XL-PFPE_wLiTFSI, with wLiTFSI ranging in between 0 and 0.5.” Conductivity at 90°C vs wLiTFSI is given in Fig. 7b; the text states: “In Fig. 7b, σ is reported as a function of wLiTFSI at 90°C… For the XL-PFPE electrolytes… [σ] reaches a plateau of 6.5 ± 0.2 × 10−5 S/cm for wLiTFSI ≥ 0.40.” Therefore, for XL-PFPE_0.40 the ionic conductivity at 90°C is 6.5×10−5 S/cm.

PFP (PETMP–TFEMA–PEGDA) is blended with PEO to form solid polymer electrolytes. Key results specifically for 80PEO20PFP: “the 80PEO20PFP polymer electrolyte exhibits a high lithium-ion transference number of 0.39 and ionic conductivity of 3.7 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C.” Additionally, the oxidative stability with PFP increases to 4.4 V vs. Li+/Li compared to PEO: “the oxidation potential of 80PEO20PFP electrolyte has been increased to 4.4 V (vs. Li+/Li).” These metrics demonstrate PFP’s application as a lithium battery solid polymer electrolyte component.

PEP (PETMP-EMA-PEGDA) was synthesized as a contrast sample: "As a contrast, the same synthetic procedure was adopted for PETMP-EMA-PEGDA (PEP) to further discuss the effect of fluorinated branched chain." Application as SPE is evidenced by the lithium-ion transference number measurement: "Additionally, the tLi+ of the 80PEO20PEP electrolyte was also tested and found to be approximately 0.18 (Fig. S5), higher than that of the PEO electrolyte..." This indicates PEP is used in a PEO-based solid polymer electrolyte for Li+ conduction.
Table 1 lists FR-PU with LOI of 27.6%; Fig. 2a describes FR-PU self-extinguishing within 10 s after ignition; Table 1 and Fig. 2b also show reduced PHRR and THR for FR-PU versus Pure-PU.

Pure PU is the non-flame-retardant control. Table 1 reports its flame-retardancy metrics: LOI 16.8%, Tp 402.4 °C, PHRR 55.4 W·g-1, THR 6.1 kJ·g-1, ID/IG 2.137. Fig. 2a describes that Pure-PU burns violently with molten dripping after 5 s of ignition and almost burns out after about 10 s. No electrochemical battery performance was evaluated for Pure PU; those tests were conducted on FR-PU-based electrolytes.

FR-PU-5% Li is explicitly prepared as a flame-retardant polyurethane solid electrolyte with 5 wt% LiTFSI (Section 2.3). Table 2 reports only its thermal/mechanical properties (e.g., Td,5% = 284.3 °C), but no ionic conductivity, electrochemical window, or battery performance values are provided for this composition. Quantitative ionic conductivities and electrochemical stability are given for FR-PU-20% Li (Table 3; Fig. 7c–d), and cell tests are performed with FR-PU-20% Li (Fig. 8), not with FR-PU-5% Li. Therefore, no numeric application performance specific to FR-PU-5% Li can be extracted.

FR-PU solid electrolytes with varying lithium salt contents, including 10 wt% LiTFSI, were prepared: "The mass ratio of FR-PU and LiTFSI are 95:5, 90:10, 85:15, 80:20 and 75:25... the flame-retardant polyurethane solid electrolytes with the mass fraction of lithium salt of 5%, 10%, 15%, 20% and 25% could be prepared." The ionic conduction function (application as electrolyte) was evaluated across these compositions: "The ionic conductivities of the FR-PU solid electrolytes are measured by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)... with different content of LiTFSI are shown in Fig. 7a." The specific sample "FR-PU-10% Li" is explicitly listed in Table 2 (thermal/mechanical data), confirming this composition as one of the prepared solid polymer electrolytes.

FR-PU-15% Li (FR-PU with 15 wt% LiTFSI) is one of the solid polymer electrolytes prepared (Section 2.3). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy compared ionic conductivities for different LiTFSI contents including 15% (Fig. 7a,b), but explicit numeric conductivity values and electrochemical window were only reported for the 20% Li sample (Table 3 and Fig. 7c,d). Battery performance data were also only provided for FR-PU-20% Li (Fig. 8). For FR-PU-15% Li, only thermal/mechanical properties are tabulated (Table 2), so no direct electrolyte performance metrics are extractable.

Direct property of the FR-PU-20% Li solid polymer electrolyte: “The ionic conductivity of FR-PU 20% Li solid electrolyte at 70 °C can up to 1.51 × 10^-4 S cm^-1” (Fig. 7b; Table 3 lists 1.51 × 10^-4 (70 °C) for FR-PU-20% Li). Additional electrochemical performance using FR-PU-20% Li in LiFePO4/FR-PU-20% Li/Li cells at 70 °C: “the electrochemical stability window … could reach around 4.81 V vs. Li+/Li” (Fig. 7c) and cycling performance “155.1 mAh g^-1 and coulombic efficiency of 98.3% after 120 cycles (0.2 C, 70 °C)” (Fig. 8c; Table 3).

The sample "FR-PU-25% Li" is explicitly prepared and characterized as a solid polymer electrolyte (Table 2 lists FR-PU-25% Li among FR-PU solid electrolytes with different LiTFSI contents). Ionic conductivity was measured for "different content of LiTFSI" (Fig. 7a,b), which includes 25% Li, but no explicit numeric conductivity values are given for the 25% sample in the text/tables. Electrochemical window and battery cycling data are reported for FR-PU-20% Li only (Fig. 7c–d, Fig. 8, Table 3). Therefore, for FR-PU-25% Li the application is a lithium battery solid electrolyte (Li+ conduction), but no directly reported application performance metrics (capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration) specific to this sample are provided.

Ta-ESM is used as an electrolyte membrane for Li-ion batteries. Reported metrics for Ta-ESM include: room-temperature ionic conductivity 4.858×10^-3 S cm^-1 (increasing to 6.026×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 70 °C); Li-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.61 (Table 1: I_in=0.03925 mA, I_ss=0.01916 mA, R_in=110 Ω, R_ss=130 Ω); electrochemical stability window beyond 5 V vs Li/Li+ (LSV); stable Li/Li symmetric cycling for 567 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 without short circuit; interfacial resistance stabilized in the range 45–62 Ω cm^-2 over 12 days. These results are specifically reported for Ta-ESM (LLZTO-incorporated PVdF-HFP/PBMA electrospun membrane).

LDFBDB is studied as a lithium salt for Li-ion battery electrolytes. Solubility in PC at 25°C is 0.518 mol dm−3 (Table 1). Ionic conductivities for 0.10 mol dm−3 LDFBDB at 25°C: PC 0.414 mS cm−1; PC+DME 2.167 mS cm−1; PC+EMC 1.124 mS cm−1; PC+EC+EMC 0.960 mS cm−1 (Fig. 3, Table 2). Saturated PC+DME solution shows 1.18 mS cm−1. Electrochemical oxidation potential in PC is ~3.7 V vs Li+/Li for 0.1 mol dm−3 at 20°C, 9 mV s−1 (Fig. 4). Thermal decomposition onset at 170°C in N2 (Fig. 2).

LBOB is evaluated as a Li-ion battery electrolyte salt. Table 2 reports ionic conductivity for 0.1 mol dm-3 solutions at 298.2 K: in PC, LBOB shows 5.56 mS cm-1, and in PC + DME 6.79 mS cm-1. Electrochemical oxidation stability is given as ~4.5 V vs Li+/Li (Fig. 4). Solubility in PC at 25 °C is 0.362 mol dm-3 (Table 1). Thermal decomposition onset for LBOB is 302 °C (Fig. 2). These data are specific to LBOB and directly relevant to its use as a lithium-ion battery electrolyte.

AOEM2 is only reported as a synthetic intermediate (Section 2.2.1: 'Synthesis of 3-(2-(2-methoxyethoxy)ethoxy)prop-1-ene (AOEM2)') and is not evaluated in any application; all performance data (electrolyte properties, cell tests, thermal stability) concern DFSM2 or MFSM2.

MFSM2 is proposed as an electrolyte solvent for LIBs and shows practical electrolyte-relevant metrics: with 1 M LiTFSI, ionic conductivity is 1.4 mS cm-1 at room temperature; oxidative decomposition potential is 4.5 V vs Li+/Li; reductive decomposition potential is 1.0 V vs Li+/Li (Table 1). The text states these fluorosilane compounds possess desirable properties making them potential electrolyte solvents for LIBs.

DFSM2 is applied as an electrolyte solvent/co-solvent for lithium-ion batteries: “Fluorosilanes synthesized as novel electrolytes for lithium-ion batteries.” DFSM2 shows higher oxidative stability and ionic conductivity: oxidative decomposition potential 5.0 V vs Li+/Li and ionic conductivity 1.8 mS cm-1 for 1 M LiTFSI in DFSM2 at room temperature (Table 1). It was chosen as electrolyte solvent for high-voltage LCO/graphite cells due to its higher conductivity and oxidative potential. As a co-solvent in E3 (1M LiPF6 EC/DFSM2/EMC (2/3/5 in vol.) + 5 wt% FEC), DFSM2-enabled electrolytes delivered improved high-voltage cycling: LCO/Li at 0.5C between 3.0–4.4 V showed only 6.3% capacity decay after 175 cycles; at 4.5 V decay was 16.7% after 175 cycles. LCO/graphite full cells at 4.4 V, 0.5C showed 92.5% capacity retention after 135 cycles with E3 vs 66.8% with commercial E0. Thermal stability with lithiated graphite and charged LixCoO2 was also enhanced for DFSM2-containing electrolyte (DSC). However, DFSM2 alone with 1M LiPF6 was not compatible with graphite without additives; compatibility improved with FEC.

E0 is defined as: "E0 1M LiPF6 EC/EMC (3/7 in vol.)." In graphite/Li half cells, the initial coulombic efficiency of E0 is reported as "88.8%" (vs. lower ICE for DFSM2-containing electrolytes). In LCO/graphite full cells (4.4 V upper cutoff, 0.5C), E0 shows: "The coulombic efficiency of the first cycle/135th cycle ... 81.4%/99.6% ..." and capacity retention: "66.8% for E0 after 135 cycles." Thermal stability (DSC): with lithiated graphite, E0 exhibits exothermic peaks at "around 138°C" and "260°C" and a strong peak at "about 300°C." With 4.4 V-charged LixCoO2 and E0, two exothermic peaks are observed at "215°C" and "283°C."

E1 is defined as 1M LiPF6 EC/DFSM2/EMC (2/3/5 in vol.). For graphite/Li half cell with E1, the first-cycle discharge and charge capacities are 332.4 and 455.7 mAh/g, respectively, with initial coulombic efficiency 72.9%. In cycling at 0.2C, the capacity increased from 287 to 304 mAh/g after 50 cycles. These data are explicitly reported for E1 in the Results (Fig. 2a,c discussion).

E2 is defined as: "1M LiPF6 EC/DFSM2/EMC (2/3/5 in vol.) + 5 wt% VC". In graphite/Li half cells, E2 gives an initial coulombic efficiency (ICE) of 78.3%. Its cycling is poor: capacity drops from ~330 mAh/g in the first three formation cycles at 0.1C to 200 mAh/g in the first cycle at 0.2C, and further to 75 mAh/g after 10 cycles at 0.2C. No LCO/Li or LCO/graphite high-voltage cycling or thermal stability data were reported specifically for E2; those tests focused on E3.

E3 is defined as: 1M LiPF6 EC/DFSM2/EMC (2/3/5 in vol.) + 5 wt% FEC. For LCO/graphite full cells at 0.5C with 4.4 V upper cutoff, E3 shows capacity retention of 92.5% after 135 cycles, compared to 66.8% for E0; the first/135th cycle coulombic efficiency is 72.9%/99.8%. Additional E3 data: LCO/Li half cells deliver 167 mAh g^-1 (4.4 V) and 182 mAh g^-1 (4.5 V) at 0.1C; capacity decay is 6.3% after 175 cycles at 0.5C (4.4 V) and 16.7% at 4.5 V. Graphite/Li half cell in E3 attains ~340 mAh g^-1 in the initial 50 cycles without noticeable capacity loss; initial coulombic efficiency is 79.0%. Thermal stability (DSC): with lithiated graphite, E3 suppresses the ~138°C exotherm and reduces the ~300°C SEI decomposition heat; with 4.4 V charged LixCoO2, E3 shifts the main exotherm to higher temperature (307°C vs. 283°C for E0).

Oxidative stability: “The oxidation potential of 1 M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE (3:7, by vol.) is observed at ∼5.08 V” (Fig. 2a, Li||NMC111 LSV, 0.1 mV/s). Li+ transference number: “The calculated tLi+ … were 0.426 for 1 M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE/EMC and 0.352 for 1 M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE.” (Eq. 10; Li||Li symmetric cell, 1 mV polarization). Table 2 also lists the experimental tLi+ = 0.352 for FEC/TTE.

For 1 M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE/EMC (3:5:2, vol.): (i) Oxidative stability measured by LSV on Li||NMC111 shows an oxidation potential extended to ~5.31 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 2a). (ii) Li+ transference number measured in Li||Li symmetric cell via Bruce–Vincent–Evans is 0.426 at room temperature (Fig. 7, Table 2). (iii) MD-derived transport: D(PF6−)=2.998×10^-6 cm2/s, D(Li+)=2.124×10^-6 cm2/s, giving theoretical tLi+=0.411 (Table 2). (iv) Adding EMC resolves phase separation and stabilizes the mixed fluorinated electrolyte (text; Table 1). All values/evidence pertain specifically to the ternary 3:5:2 formulation.

FBCP_20 is one of the synthesized triblock copolymers (Table 1) and is used to prepare solid polymer electrolyte films (FBCPEs) with LiTFSI at [EO]/[Li] = 20:1 (Section 2.4). The temperature-dependent ionic conductivities of the three FBCPEs (including FBCPE_20) were measured (Fig. 6a), and the electrolytes are evaluated in Li metal battery configurations (Sections 2.5, 3.5–3.6). However, explicit numeric electrochemical metrics (ionic conductivity, Li+ transference number, electrochemical stability window, cycling performance) are only reported for FBCPE_30, not for FBCPE_20; therefore no quantitative values are extracted for FBCP_20.

All battery/electrolyte performance data (ionic conductivity 2.68×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 70°C, t_Li+ = 0.41, ESW up to 4.9 V, symmetric-cell cycling, and LiFePO4/NCM cell results) are explicitly for FBCPE_30 (polymer + LiTFSI), not for FBCP_30. For FBCP_30 itself, only materials properties such as melting point (60.8°C) and tensile fracture strain (440%) are reported, without direct application metrics fitting the schema.

FBCP_40 was used to prepare an electrolyte membrane (FBCPE_40) with LiTFSI at [EO]/[Li] = 20:1. The three FBCPEs (including FBCPE_40) were evaluated for ionic conductivity, shown as Arrhenius plots (Fig. 6a), but no explicit numeric value for FBCPE_40 is provided: "FBCPE_30 shows a higher ionic conductivity (2.68 × 10^{-4} S cm-1) at 70°C than FBCPE_20 and FBCPE_40." Morphology for FBCPE_40 is directly shown (AFM/TEM: Fig. 5c, h, i). Cells and detailed electrochemical metrics (t_Li+, ESW, cycling) are reported for FBCPE_30, not FBCPE_40, so performance numbers for FBCP_40 are not extractable.

DPPA0 is defined as the GPE without TFOA: "GPEs without TFOA modifier was also prepared and named as DPPA0." In Li|Li symmetric cells, "the DPPA0 GPEs reached an initial overpotential of 87.4 mV and experienced a voltage dip short circuit at approximately 180 h" at 0.20 mA cm^-2 and 0.20 mAh cm^-2. For full cells, at 3C and 30 °C (3–4.3 V), "the cells utilizing DPPA0 GPEs exhibits a precipitous decline in capacity to approximately 105 mAh g^-1 after 300 cycles, representing 62.89% of their initial capacity." Additional electrolyte metrics specific to DPPA0 include Li+ transference number of 0.31 and an oxidation stability of 4.63 V (LSV), but these are not mapped to the schema fields.

Section 2.4: "...to form the polymer electrolyte named DPPA1." The study assembles Li metal batteries with these GPEs (DPPA-TFOA-TCGG-PAN). Quantitative performance values reported later are for DPPA3 (e.g., conductivity and cycling), not DPPA1. Fig. 2(a,b) discussion: "all samples containing TFOA demonstrated lower electrochemical impedance and higher ionic conductivity than that of the pure DPPA GPEs," which qualitatively includes DPPA1, but no specific numeric data for DPPA1 are provided.

DPPA2 is defined as a DPPA:TFOA=2:1 gel polymer electrolyte (Section 2.4). The study states that all samples containing TFOA exhibited lower electrochemical impedance and higher ionic conductivity than the pure DPPA GPEs, which applies to DPPA2. However, explicit numerical performance values reported in the text are given for DPPA3 (e.g., ionic conductivity 2.52×10^-3 S/cm at 30 °C; tLi+ = 0.61; >2000 h Li||Li stability), and no specific numeric data for DPPA2 are provided in the text; thus numeric fields are left null.

DPPA3 shows ionic conductivity of 2.52×10^-3 S/cm and t_Li+ = 0.61 at 30 °C (Highlights; Fig. 2b,c,d text). Li|DPPA3|Li symmetric cell cycles stably >2000 h at 0.2 mA/cm^2 (Highlights; Fig. 3a). Electrochemical stability window reaches 4.81 V (LSV, Fig. S4a). NCM811|DPPA3|Li retains 88.20% after 300 cycles at 3C and delivers 101.5 mAh/g at 10C (Results).

DPPA4 is defined as a DPPA:TFOA = 4:1 GPE sample (“DPPA2, DPPA3, DPPA4 and DPPA5 were prepared with ratios of 2:1, 3:1, 4:1 and 5:1 respectively.”). These GPEs are studied as electrolytes for lithium metal batteries, and it is stated that “all samples containing TFOA demonstrated lower electrochemical impedance and higher ionic conductivity than that of the pure DPPA GPEs.” However, specific numeric performance values are only reported for DPPA3 (e.g., conductivity and tLi+), and DPPA4 has no individual quantitative metrics given (figures provide no readouts). Therefore, performance fields are left null.

DPPA5 is explicitly defined: "DPPA2, DPPA3, DPPA4 and DPPA5 were prepared with ratios of 2:1, 3:1, 4:1 and 5:1 respectively." The application context and performance trend covering DPPA5: "Electrochemical impedance and ionic conductivity tests were carried out for different ratios of DPPA-TFOA-PAN GPEs. As presented in Fig. 2 (a) and (b), all samples containing TFOA demonstrated lower electrochemical impedance and higher ionic conductivity than that of the pure DPPA GPEs..." This establishes DPPA5 (a TFOA-containing GPE) as a lithium-ion conducting battery electrolyte, but no DPPA5-specific numeric values are reported.

At 30°C, the ion conductivity (σ) of FG-GPE-0, FG-GPE-3, FG-GPE-5, and FG-GPE-7 are 3.42 × 10^-4, 2.67 × 10^-3, 4.74 × 10^-3, and 2.24 × 10^-3 S cm^-1, respectively (Fig. 2e, S7, and Fig. S8). The ESW values for FG-GPE-0, FG-GPE-3, FG-GPE-5, and FG-GPE-7 are 4.54 V, 4.55 V, 4.64 V, and 4.71 V, respectively.

For NCM811|FG-GPE-3|Li: "NCM811|FG-GPE-3|Li battery demonstrates a capacity retention rate of 64.6% after undergoing for 400 cycles at 3C" (Fig. 6c). Supporting performance context for FG-GPE-3: ESW 4.55 V (Fig. 2b); σ at 30 °C: 2.67×10^-3 S cm^-1 (Fig. 2e); t_Li+ = 0.55 (Fig. S9); Li|FG-GPE-3|Li symmetric cell cycles stably for 4500 h at 0.05 mA cm^-2 with ~10 mV overpotential (Fig. 4a–d).

At 30 °C, the ion conductivity (σ) of FG-GPE-5 is 4.74 × 10^−3 S cm^−1 (Fig. 2e). The calculated t_Li+ for FG-GPE-5 was 0.68 (Fig. S9). In full cells, NCM811|FG-GPE-5|Li shows 80% capacity retention after 400 cycles at 3C (Fig. 6c; Highlights).

Direct data for FG-GPE-7: electrochemical stability window (ESW) = 4.71 V (Fig. 2d); ionic conductivity σ = 2.24 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 at 30 °C (Fig. 2e). Other cell-cycling metrics in the paper are reported for FG-GPE-3 and FG-GPE-5, not FG-GPE-7.

ILPE-EMImTFSI was used as the gel polymer electrolyte in Li/ILPE/LiFePO4 cells. Reported performance includes: “With an initially high discharge capacity of 143 mAh g−1 … the discharge capacity of a LiFePO4 cell in ILPE-EMImTFSI increases with stable cycle properties for up to 50 cycles.” and “At each current density, ILPE-EMImTFSI delivered reversible discharge capacities of 143 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C, 133 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C, and 109 mAh g−1 at 1 C.” Additional context for the same material: ionic conductivity 5.3×10−3 S cm−1 at 30 °C and anodic stability limit 5.5 V vs Li.

Used as a GPE separator in LIC. Evidence: “the MnO2@PVDF/TBAC LVNM had the smallest bulk resistance of 1.02 Ω and the largest ionic conductivity of 2.95*10^-3 S/cm” (Table 1; Fig. 6a). Electrochemical stability window Es = 3.9 V and interfacial resistance Rin = 124 Ω (Table 1; Fig. 6b–c). Electrical resistivity of MnO2@PVDF/TBAC was 5.9×10^10 Ω·cm, indicating it could be used as separator. LIC assembled with MnO2@PVDF/TBAC LVNM delivered specific capacitance 19.5 F/g at 0.5 C and capacity retention 67.20% after 10,000 cycles (Fig. 6d; Fig. 7a).

F-GPE was used as the electrolyte in Li||LCO and LCO||Gr lithium-ion cells. Key reported performances include: (i) Coin cell (LCO||F-GPE||Li): “The battery obtained impressive discharge capacities of 134.9, 133.3, 126.5, 119.6, and 105.2 mA h g^-1 at the rates of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1 and 2C, respectively” and “The capacity retentions of the LCO||F-GPE||Li cell at 0.1 and 1C for 100 cycles were 97.3% and 96.3% at room temperature.” Also at low temperature: “The discharge capacity of the LCO||F-GPE||Li battery was 89.4 mA h g^-1 when the operating temperature is reduced to −40 °C, which was 72.1% of capacity at room temperature.” (ii) Full pouch cell (LCO||F-GPE||Gr): “The capacity retention rates of the full battery were 95.3% after 200 cycles at 0.5C and 92.9% after 400 cycles at 1C… The first cycle discharge capacity of the full cell at 1C was 1.1 Ah… the capacity slowly decayed to 1.02 Ah at the 400th cycle… The discharge capacity of the full battery was 0.83 Ah at −40 °C… Additionally, the discharge capacity of the battery at 60 °C was up to 1.35 Ah.” Supporting electrolyte properties for F-GPE: ionic conductivity “were 1.2, 7.6, and 12 mS cm^-1 at −40, 30, and 60 °C, respectively”; Li+ transference number “tLi+ for F-GPE was 0.51”; oxidative stability “F-GPE had a high oxidation stability of 5.6 V.” Symmetric Li||F-GPE||Li: “remained stable … after 900 h cycling at 0.1 mA cm^-2,” and Li||Cu CEavg “94.36%.” These data directly tie the application and performance to F-GPE.

LE is defined as 1 M LiPF6 in EP/EMC/FEC (4:1:1 by weight). The average Coulombic efficiency (CEavg) for Li||LE||Cu is 80.62% (Fig. 3c inset: “compared to LE (80.62%)”). Additional LE-related performance details: electrochemical window 4.8 V (LSV: “the electrochemical windows of … LE were at 4.8 V”), t_Li+ = 0.44 (“the tLi+ for LE was only 0.44, as shown by Fig. S3 and Table S1”), Li||Li symmetric with LE shows highest overpotential ~200 mV and short-circuits after 420 h (“the highest overpotential … with LE electrolyte was 200 mV … battery short-circuited after 420 h”), desolvation activation energy Ea = 58.466 kJ mol−1 (“The Ea values of … LE … were … 58.466 kJ mol−1”), and TGA weight loss 54% at 100 °C (“The thermal stability of LE was the worst … with a weight loss of 54%”).

PVDF blended with 40 wt% [PMPyrr][TFSI] was assessed as a solid polymer electrolyte. Table 4 reports for PVDF: ionic conductivity σi = 5.3 × 10^-5 S cm-1 at 20 °C and 2.5 × 10^-4 S cm-1 at 60 °C; activation energy = 14 kJ/mol; Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.12. Fig. 4(c) shows a PVDF cyclic voltammogram with currents below 1e-8 A from 0–5 V, indicating electrochemical stability within the LFP operating range. However, Section 3.4 explicitly states that solid-state batteries were not assembled with the PVDF-based SPE due to its low Li+ transference number and high crystallinity, so no discharge capacity/cycling metrics are available for PVDF.

Solid-state half-cells (Li metal anode | LFP cathode) were assembled using the blend samples with 40 wt% [PMPyrr][TFSI], including P(VDF-HFP). At room temperature and C/10, the initial discharge capacity for the P(VDF-HFP)-based cell was 140 mAh/g (Fig. 5a). Electrolyte properties for P(VDF-HFP) from Table 4: ionic conductivity 6.2×10^-5 S cm^-1 (20 °C) and 2.4×10^-4 S cm^-1 (60 °C), Li+ transference number 0.35. EIS-derived overall resistance of the assembled cell before cycling was 1049 Ω and after cycling 778 Ω (Fig. 6).

The material P(VDF-TrFE) was used as the polymer matrix in a solid polymer electrolyte (40 wt% [PMPyrr][TFSI]) for solid-state Li/LFP half-cells. Cells were cycled at room temperature at C/10, and an initial discharge capacity of 146 mAh/g was achieved for the P(VDF-TrFE)-based sample (Fig. 5a: “An initial discharge capacity value of 146, 140 and 133 mAh/g was achieved for P(VDF-TrFE), P(VDF-HFP) and P(VDF-TrFE-CFE) based samples, respectively.”). Supporting electrolyte properties for P(VDF-TrFE) include σi(20 °C) = 4.6 × 10^-5 S cm^-1, σi(60 °C) = 2.1 × 10^-4 S cm^-1, activation energy = 13 kJ/mol, and Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.53 (Table 4). The assembled batteries excluded neat PVDF but included P(VDF-TrFE) (Section 3.4).

Cells using P(VDF-TrFE-CFE) with 40 wt% [PMPyr][TFSI] as SPE in Li|SPE|LFP half-cells show 122 mAh/g after 50 cycles at C/10 and room temperature (Fig. 5a,b). The P(VDF-TrFE-CFE) sample exhibits Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.71 and ionic conductivity 2.3×10^-5 S cm^-1 (20 °C), 3.3×10^-4 S cm^-1 (60 °C) (Table 4). At 1C, initial and final capacities are 91 and 68 mAh/g over 100 cycles with ~90% coulombic efficiency (Fig. 5c,d).

31VPIF/OZ is a solid polymer electrolyte used in Li metal batteries. Key performance directly reported for 31VPIF/OZ: (i) Ionic conductivity at room temperature: 3.58×10^-4 S/cm (Fig. 4b text: “To ensure normal operation of SPEs at room temperature (σ = 3.58×10^-4 S cm^-1), the copolymerization ratio of VEC to TFEMA was determined to be 3:1.”). (ii) Electrochemical window: 5.4 V at 40°C (Fig. 4d). (iii) Li+ transference number t_Li+: 0.52 at 40°C (Fig. 4e). (iv) Li|31VPIF/OZ|Li symmetric cell cycling: 1000 h without short circuit at 0.1 mA/cm^2 (performed at 40°C in the text around Fig. 5c) and stable cycling up to 600 h at room temperature (Fig. S21). (v) Full cell Li|31VPIF/OZ|LFP at 40°C, 1C: capacity 154.68 → 134.38 mAh/g after 300 cycles, capacity retention 86.88%, Coulombic efficiency ≈100% (Fig. 6c). (vi) Rate performance at 40°C: 0.1/0.2/0.3/0.5/1/1.5C discharge capacities = 160.0/159.4/158.9/156.8/146.4/107.4 mAh/g; recovery to 159.63 mAh/g at 0.1C (Fig. 6d). (vii) Room-temperature rate performance: 146.9/144.8/142.4/136.1/104 mAh/g at 0.1/0.2/0.3/0.5/1C; recovery to 147.1 mAh/g at 0.1C (Fig. 6e). (viii) Room-temperature cycling: 0.5C, 600 cycles, capacity 147.08 → 134.58 mAh/g, capacity retention 91.5%, Coulombic efficiency ≈100% (Fig. 6g; also emphasized in Conclusion).

TFF (TEP-FEMC-FEC) is used as a non-flammable high-voltage electrolyte for Li-ion/Li-metal batteries. Non-flammability: SET value 0 s g^-1 (LB-063: 22.77 s g^-1) (Fig. 1b). High-voltage stability: LSV shows ~0 μA until 5.6 V (Fig. 2a); leakage current ~0 μA for 15 h at 4.4, 4.5, 4.6 V (Fig. 2b). Transport properties: ionic conductivity 4.7 mS cm^-1; Li+ transference number 0.58; contact angle on Li 11.56° (Figs. S3, S4). LCO||Li performance: ~100 mAh g^-1 at 20 C; 141 mAh g^-1 at 0.5 C remains after 300 cycles with 76% retention (Fig. S9a); at 5 C, average capacity stability 74.9% after 400 cycles (Fig. 2h); generally higher capacities across rates (Fig. 2g). Interphase: uniform CEI ~23 nm on LCO (Fig. 3f). Li-metal compatibility: Li||Li symmetric lifespan >2200 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2, 1 mAh cm^-2 (Fig. 4f); in-situ optical shows dense deposition without dendritic growth over time (Fig. 4g). Graphite compatibility: Li||Gr shows reversible CV; LCO||Gr coin cells retain 84.37% after 200 cycles at 4.5 V (Fig. 5b); LCO||Gr pouch cell capacity 0.87 Ah with 95.5% retention after 150 cycles at 4.5 V (Fig. 5c). Safety: nail penetration—sparks without fire and lower peak temperature vs LB-063 (Fig. 5d,e); fast charge 2 A to 4.5 V—temperature rise only from 24.6 to 30.9 °C (LB-063 to 44.6 °C) (Fig. 5g).

The paper explicitly prepares and uses a TEP electrolyte (LiFSI-TEP) as a comparator in lithium battery tests: “a TEP electrolyte of 1.5 mmol LiFSI dissolving in 1 mL TEP was prepared for comparison” (Section 2.1). It is characterized alongside TFF in Raman: “Raman spectra of TFF and LiFSI-TEP electrolytes” (Fig. 1e). It is also used in full-cell tests: “cycling performance of LCO || Gr cells upon LiFSI-TEP and TFF” (Fig. 5b) and noted to cause graphite cracking (Fig. S21). However, the text provides no explicit numeric performance metrics for LiFSI-TEP (capacities/retentions are only quantified for TFF and LB-063 in the text), so quantitative fields are left null.

Composition/identity: "LB-063 electrolyte containing 1 mmol LiPF6 in 1 mL mixed solution of 30 vol% ethylene carbonate (EC) and 70 vol% dimethyl carbonate (DMC) was selected as representative of commercial electrolyte." Flammability: "the commercial carbonate electrolyte (abbreviated as LB-063) impregnated glass fibers continuously burn for 9.65 s after ignition and the SET value is 22.77 s g−1." Oxidative stability: "the LB-063 electrolyte exhibits obvious oxidative current early at 4 V." Ionic property: "the ionic conductivity of ... LB-063 (11.7 mS cm−1)." Li||Li lifespan: "the time-voltage profile of Li || Li cells upon TFF shows a longer lifespan over 2200 h at 0.5 mA cm−2, 1 mA h cm−2, which is only 900 h for LB-063." LCO||Gr performance (4.5 V): "TFF-based Gr anode could achieve high capacity retention of 84.37% after 200 cycles, which is much higher than that of commercial LB-063 electrolyte (18.92 mA h g−1 and 13.42% after 200 cycles)." Safety (nail penetration, fast charge): for LB-063 pouch cell, "sparks flashed with much smoke ... followed by great fire" and during 2 A fast charging to 4.5 V, temperature increased to "44.6 °C".

LLZTO 6 h is used as an active filler in composite ionogel electrolytes (e.g., 15% LLZTO 6 h). Reported performance for the LLZTO 6 h-containing IG includes: highest ionic conductivity among tested IGs; oxidation stability up to 5.27 V vs. Li+/Li; stable Li|IG|Li stripping/plating with ~100 mV overpotential for 600 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2; in Li|IG|LFP cells, specific capacity ~162 mA h g^-1 at 0.2C with 99.3% capacity retention after 200 cycles and average Coulombic efficiency ~99.93%. Conclusions also state >0.4 mS cm^-1 at 20 °C and >1 mS cm^-1 at 40 °C for 15% LLZTO 6 h.

In Li|IG|SS LSV tests (Fig. 5b), the insertion of the inorganic filler increased the oxidation potential from 4.65 V to 4.98 V for 15% LLZTO 15 h and 24 h, while 15% LLZTO 6 h was stable up to 5.27 V. No numeric ionic conductivity or cycling capacity values were reported specifically for 15% LLZTO 24 h; stripping/plating showed rising overpotential at 0.05 mA cm^-2 without a quantified value.

application.target_species: EMITFSI-based precursor solution (12 wt% C1-4TFSI + 0.1 wt% AIBN in EMITFSI-based electrolyte)

parse.why: The PDDATFSI porous membrane is used as the separator in the cells: “The LiFePO4/Li-HPILSE/Li cell was consisted of LiFePO4 as the cathode, Li foil as the anode and the PDDATFSI porous membrane as the separator… The above mentioned precursor … was injected into the PDDATFSI porous membrane and … polymerized.” Additionally, its application-relevant uptake capacity is reported: “Such high porosity and superior wettability provide the PDDATFSI porous membrane with a higher uptake ability for the precursor solution (251.2%, Fig. S5d).” Related properties supporting this role include porosity 57.5% and contact angle 5.3° with the precursor solution.

Directly stated for Li-HPILSE: “the prepared Li-HPILSE delivers a satisfied conductivity of 1.06×10^-3 Scm^-1 at 25 °C.” Additional Li-HPILSE application context: “the Li-HPILSE is relatively stable up to 4.4 V vs. Li/Li+,” “the Li-HPILSE exhibits a higher t_Li+ of 0.20,” and in LiFePO4/Li-HPILSE/Li cells it maintains 147.1 mAh g^-1 with 97.7% retention after 100 cycles at 0.1 C.

For Na-HPILSE: ionic conductivity is 1.15×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 25 °C (Fig. 3a). Electrochemical stability reaches 4.6 V vs. Na/Na+ (inset of Fig. 3b). Na+ transference number t_Na+ = 0.22 (Fig. 3d). Na/Na-HPILSE/Na symmetric cells show stable cycling for 300 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (Fig. S10). Full cells Na0.9[Cu0.22Fe0.30Mn0.48]O2/Na-HPILSE/Na deliver 101.0, 93.4, 82.5, 70.5, and 59.9 mAh/g at 0.05–1 C, and 85.6 mAh/g after 100 cycles at 0.1 C with 85.5% capacity retention and near-100% coulombic efficiency (Fig. 5).

G2 is the commercial baseline electrolyte evaluated in multiple lithium battery configurations. For Li||LFP: initial discharge specific capacity 154.8 mAh/g (CE 93.7%) at 0.1C; after 100 cycles at 0.5C, capacity retention 80.8% and average CE 98.2%; on rate test, upon returning from 5C to 0.2C, specific capacity restoration 92.5%. For Li||Cu at 0.5 mA·cm^-2 and 1.0 mAh·cm^-2: initial CE 86.7%; average CE 91.2% within 80 cycles; polarization 59 mV (10th) and 89 mV (50th). For Li||Li symmetric: at 0.5 mA·cm^-2, polarization rose sharply after ~350 h, reaching 280 mV after 430 h; at 1.0 mA·cm^-2, cycle life about 140 h with polarization 613 mV at 180 h then short-circuit. Oxidative stability (LSV) onset for G2 ~3.5 V vs Li/Li+.

F1 (TMS-only sulfone-based electrolyte) was tested as an electrolyte for lithium metal cells. In Li||Cu at 0.5 mA·cm^-2 and 1.0 mAh·cm^-2, the initial CE of the F1 electrolyte is 61.7% and the CE gradually decreases after 10 cycles; at 1.0 mA·cm^-2 the CE remains very low. The polarization voltage of F1 is much larger than G2 and F4 and increases sharply with cycling. In Li||Li symmetric cells at 0.5 mA·cm^-2 and 1.0 mAh·cm^-2, F1 shows an overpotential of 216 mV, and the polarization voltage increases to 1.30 V after 400 h with an interfacial impedance of 50.2 Ω.

F4 is a fluorine-free sulfone-based electrolyte (TMS+EMC). In Li||Li symmetric cells at 0.5 mA·cm^-2 and 1.0 mAh·cm^-2, F4 shows the shortest cycle life and reaches the cut-off voltage in <50 h, with interfacial impedances of 125 Ω (1st cycle) and 87.3 Ω (5th cycle) (Fig. 6a,e). When the current density increases to 1.0 mA·cm^-2, the polarization voltage of F4 increases to 1.4 V within 100 h (Fig. 6b,e). In Li||Cu half-cells at 0.5 mA·cm^-2 and 1.0 mAh·cm^-2, F4 exhibits an obvious CE 'diving' phenomenon due to unstable SEI (Section 3.3). Oxidative stability: although lower than F1, F4 can still be used up to 5.0 V for high-voltage lithium metal batteries (Section 3.2). SEI composition on Li||Cu after 10 cycles: F4 shows at.% Li 3.46, F 7.07, C 25.8, O 58.4, S 3.88, N 1.40 (Table 2), with LiF proportion ~77.2% from F 1s peak area (Fig. 8c).

F7 electrolyte in Li||LFP: “after the first activation cycle at 0.1C, F7 electrolyte delivers higher initial discharge specific capacity 155.7 mAh·g−1 (CE of 95.9%). … In contrast, the F7 electrolyte exhibited a higher capacity retention of 94.3% and an excellent average CE of 99.8% after 100 cycles” (Fig. 10). Rate test: “When the current density recovered from 5C to 0.2C… the specific capacity restoration for F7 electrolyte enhanced to 96.7%” (Fig. 12). Li||Cu: “F7 electrolyte … shows an excellent long-term cycling performance with 130 cycles (>500 h) and the average CE is 95.9% … F7 electrolyte can still be cycled stably for 100 cycles with the average CE of 93.5%” at 1.0 mA·cm−2. Overpotential: “33 mV at 10th and 43 mV at 50th cycles” (Fig. 4). Li||Li symmetric: “the cycle life of the F7 electrolyte is over 400 h” at 0.5 mA·cm−2; “voltage polarization … remained stable at ~60 mV … interfacial impedances of 7.17 Ω and 6.89 Ω” (200 h and 400 h). At 1.0 mA·cm−2, “F7 electrolyte can still maintain a relatively long cycle life of about 480 h.” Oxidative stability: sulfone-based electrolytes incl. F7 remain suitable “for 5 V high-voltage lithium metal batteries.”

The material is used as a cross-linked polymer solid electrolyte (FPCSPE) for lithium metal batteries. Reported values: “FPCSPE 3-40 exhibited the highest σLi+ of 5.02×10−5 Scm−1 at room temperature… The tLi+ value reached ~0.44… the oxidation potential increases and reaches the maximum of 5.7 V… At a current density of 0.1 mA cm−2, the overpotential was ~0.05 V and no visible short circuit occurred over 300 h… The assembled NCM811||FPCSPE 3-40||Li batteries showed a reversible specific capacity of 218 mAh g−1 at 0.1 C… capacity retention ~70% after 300 cycles with ~99.6% CE.” These data directly describe the application of crosslinked-P(VEC-co-TFEMA) as a Li+ conducting battery electrolyte.

DSLE2 is a dual-salt liquid electrolyte (LiTFSI:LiDFOB=10:2, 1.2 M in FEC) prepared alongside other DSLEs. The paper states that DSLEs have improved electrochemical windows (4.57–4.74 V) compared to LE and LiTFSI/FEC, and that DSLE ionic conductivities are lower than LiTFSI/FEC and decrease with increasing LiDFOB content (Fig. S1), but provides no DSLE2-specific numeric performance data or battery test results.

DSLE4 (LiTFSI:LiDFOB = 8:4 in FEC, total salt 1.2 M) was selected for further investigation as an electrolyte due to the best anodic stability (up to 4.74 V) among DSLEs and intact Al in Li|Al LSV tests. As electrolyte in Li|Cu cells, DSLE4 gave CE = 95.18% and ~95% over long-term cycling (fluctuated after 68 cycles). In symmetric Li|DSLE4|Li cells, short-circuit occurred after 938 h. In Li|DSLE4|LCO cells (3 mg cm^-2 cathode loading, 4.5 V, 1C), initial and average CEs were 91.97% and 99.45%, with 73% capacity retention after 300 cycles. DSLE4 also showed t_Li+ = 0.52. Evidence: LSV/EIS and CE data in Results 2.1–2.3 (e.g., 'DSLE4 had the best anodic stability (up to 4.74 V)... Al remained intact with DSLE4'; 'CE ... 95.18%'; 'Li|DSLE4|Li ... short-circuited after 938 h'; 'initial and average CEs of 91.97% and 99.45% ... 73% of capacity retention after 300 cycles').

DSLE6 is defined as a dual-salt liquid electrolyte with LiTFSI:LiDFOB = 6:6 at a total salt concentration of 1.2 M in FEC ("DSLEs with an LiTFSI:LiDFOB ratio of 10:2, 8:4, 6:6, 4:8, and 2:10 are denoted as DSLE2, DSLE4, DSLE6, DSLE8, and DSLE10, respectively"). LSV showed that DSLEs had significantly improved electrochemical windows (4.57–4.74 V) compared to LE (3.97 V) and LiTFSI/FEC (4.23 V). EIS indicated the ionic conductivities of DSLEs were lower than LiTFSI/FEC and decreased with increasing LiDFOB content. DSLE4 had the best anodic stability and was used for further investigation, and no DSLE6-specific numeric CE or full-cell data were reported in the provided text.

DSLE8 is defined as a dual-salt liquid electrolyte with LiTFSI:LiDFOB = 4:8 (total 1.2 M in FEC). However, the reported quantitative performance data (ionic conductivity, anodic stability specifics, cycling, CE, LMB performance) are given for DSLE4 and AFPEs, or for DSLEs as a group without DSLE8-specific values. Therefore no DSLE8-specific performance metrics can be extracted.

DSLE10 is defined as a dual-salt liquid electrolyte with LiTFSI:LiDFOB = 2:10: "The LiTFSI:LiDFOB molar ratio was set at 10:2, 8:4, 6:6, 4:8, and 2:10 for DSLE2, DSLE4, DSLE6, DSLE8, and DSLE10, respectively". General performance statements apply to all DSLEs: "DSLEs and LiDFOB/FEC had significantly improved electrochemical windows (4.57 - 4.74 V) compared to LE (3.97 V) and LiTFSI/FEC (4.23 V)" and "The ionic conductivities of DSLEs were lower than that of LiTFSI/FEC and decreased with increasing LiDFOB content". However, no DSLE10-specific numerical performance (e.g., exact anodic stability, conductivity, or cell cycling data) is reported in the provided text; thus metrics are left null.

AFPE5 is one of the in situ polymerized all-fluorinated polymer electrolytes (5 vol.% polymer). Electrochemical stability (LSV): "AFPE5, AFPE10, and AFPE20 were stable up to 4.76 V, 4.89 V, and 4.95 V, respectively." Ionic conductivity (EIS): "achieving 2.12 mS cm^-1, 1.88 mS cm^-1, and 1.31 mS cm^-1 for AFPE5, AFPE10, and AFPE20, respectively (Fig. 1e-f)." Subsequent battery tests were performed on AFPE10: "AFPE10, which balanced ionic conductivity and electrochemical stability, was used for further investigation." No full-cell or cycling performance data were reported for AFPE5.

AFPE10 is an all-fluorinated polymer electrolyte used in Li|AFPE10|LCO cells. Key reported metrics: electrochemical stability window up to 4.89 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 1d); Li|Cu half-cell CE = 98.88% (Fig. 2a); Li|AFPE10|LCO coin cell (3.0–4.5 V, 1C) retained 92% capacity after 500 cycles with average CE = 99.82% and showed rate capacities of 194, 189, 182, 161, and 142 mAh/g at 0.2, 0.5, 1, 3, and 5C, respectively; high-mass-loading LCO 14 mg/cm2 cell delivered 174 mAh/g at 0.5C with energy density 507 Wh/kg and 80% capacity retention after 200 cycles (avg CE = 99.83%); with 25 mg/cm2 LCO, energy density reached 546 Wh/kg with 82% capacity retention after 80 cycles; a ~50 mAh pouch cell showed initial 52.7 mAh and 94% capacity retention after 50 cycles; Li|AFPE10|Li symmetric cell cycle life >1600 h.

AFPE20 (20 vol.% polymer) is reported as an all-fluorinated polymer electrolyte. LSV shows an anodic stability up to 4.95 V vs Li/Li+ and EIS gives ionic conductivity of 1.31 mS cm^-1 (Fig. 1d-f). No Li-metal battery cycling or Coulombic efficiency data specific to AFPE20 are provided; subsequent device tests used AFPE10.

c-GPE (optimized as c-GPE-50) is used as an in situ crosslinked gel polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. It delivers ionic conductivity of 2.36 mS/cm at room temperature (Fig. 1b; Section 2.2). Additional performance with this electrolyte: Li||Li shows ~15.2 mV overpotential sustained for ~2800 h and CCD up to 3 mA/cm2 (Section 2.3); Li|c-GPE-50|LFP achieves 134 mAh/g and 78% capacity retention after 2000 cycles at 2 C (Fig. 2b,c); Li|c-GPE-50|NMC622 achieves 160 mAh/g at 0.5 C with 80% retention after 300 cycles (Fig. 5a); full cells under low N/P ratios retain 82% (LFP) and 79% (NMC622) after 300 cycles (Fig. 6a,b). The electrolyte exhibits Li+ transference number 0.6 and oxidation stability to 4.5 V (Section 2.2).

F-HV is used as a polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Electrochemical stability window: 4.9 V (LSV, Fig. 2b). Ionic conductivity: 1.97 mS cm^-1 at 30 °C, rising to 4.64 mS cm^-1 at 90 °C (EIS, Fig. 2c). Li+ transference number t_Li+: 0.33 (Bruce–Vincent, Fig. 2d). In Li||Li symmetric cells: ~128 mV overpotential and ultra-long cycle life of 1200 h; exchange current 0.37 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 3a,b). In Li||LFP at 1C and 30 °C: 144.0 mAh g^-1 (first), 124.4 mAh g^-1 after 3000 cycles (86.3% retention), average CE 99.9% (Fig. 4a); at 2C: 88.7% retention after 1500 cycles (Fig. 4b); rate: up to 132.8 mAh g^-1 at 5C (Fig. 4c); at 60 °C: 156.6 mAh g^-1 (first), 81.3% retention after 300 cycles (Fig. 4d). In Li||NCM811 at 4.3 V: 178.4 mAh g^-1 (first), 82.1% retention after 200 cycles, average CE 99.5% (Fig. 5a); rate: 99.6 mAh g^-1 at 3C (Fig. 5b); at 4.5 V: 186.3 mAh g^-1 (first), 82.3% retention after 150 cycles, average CE 99.1% (Fig. 5c); at 60 °C: 197.3 mAh g^-1 (first), 88.4% retention after 50 cycles (Fig. 5e). Safety/reliability: self-extinguishing time (SET) 0 s g^-1 and dimensional stability up to 200 °C (Fig. 2e,f).

PL is the PVH/LiTFSI solid electrolyte without LLZTO. Its room-temperature ionic conductivity at 25°C is 0.5 mScm^{-1} (Figure 2c). The electrochemical stable voltage window is 4.78 V (Figure 2e). The critical current density (Li||PL||Li) is 0.65 mAcm^{-2} (Figure 2g). In Li||Li symmetric cells at 0.10 mAcm^{-2}, PL exhibits unstable voltage and rapid failure (Figure 2h). In LFP||PL||Li full cells at 25°C, the average specific discharge capacity at 1C is 125.3 mAhg^{-1}, and at higher rates it gives 74.0 mAhg^{-1} (2C) and 48.1 mAhg^{-1} (3C) (Figure 3b). Long-term cycling at 1C shows a steady specific discharge capacity of 128.7 mAhg^{-1} with capacity retention of 81.5% (Figure 3e). The Li+ transference number of PL is 0.32 (Figure S4, Table S2).

PLL-0 slurry rapidly turns dark brown and gelatinize, indicating an interfacial reaction between LLZTO and PVH. It suffers from serious gelation and fails to derive a flat and smooth film. Therefore, further investigations about PLL-0 will not be conducted in the following analysis. (Figure 1c; Section 2.1)

PLL-15 is used as a composite solid electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Reported performance for PLL-15: room-temperature ionic conductivity 1.2 mS cm^-1 (25 °C) and electrochemically stable voltage 5.09 V (Figure 2c,e,f); critical current density 0.80 mA cm^-2 (Figure 2g); Li||Li symmetric cells show stable operation up to 800 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 at 25 °C (Figure 2h) and stability for as long as 600 h at higher current densities of 0.2 and 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Figure S3); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.54 (Figure S4, Table S2). In LFP||PLL-15||Li full cells at 25 °C, specific discharge capacities are 155.7, 148.2, 134.0, 109.0, and 81.9 mAh g^-1 at 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, and 3 C, respectively (Figure 3b,c). Long-term cycling at 1 C shows 95.4% capacity retention after 240 cycles (Figure 3e).

Ionic conductivity: "The ionic conductivities at 25°C for PL, PLL-15, and PLL-25 are 0.5, 1.2, and 0.6 mScm^-1, respectively." Stable voltage window: "The electrochemically stable voltages of the PL, PLL-15, and PLL-25 are 4.78, 5.09, and 4.96 V, respectively." CCD: "The CCD values of PL, PLL-15, and PLL-25 are 0.65, 0.80, and 0.45 mAcm^-2, respectively." Symmetric-cell stability: "Li symmetric cells with PL and PLL-25 exhibit unstable voltage over the cycling period and experience rapid failure." Full-cell rate performance with PLL-25: "the average specific discharge capacities for PL and PLL-25 at 1C are 125.3 and 116.9 mAhg^-1, respectively... at 2 and 3 C ... PL ... 74.0 and 48.1 mAhg^-1, while PLL-25 possesses higher values of 89.0 and 59.8 mAhg^-1, respectively." Long-term full-cell cycling: "The capacity retentions of the full cells with PL and PLL-25 degrade to 81.5% and 75.6%, respectively."

E-FPAESI films were used as the core of single-mode optical waveguides and evaluated at 1.55 μm: "Loss measurements were performed using a laser diode with the 1.55 μm wavelength." and "Propagation losses ... are less than 0.42 and 0.40 dB/cm for the TE and TM polarization, respectively." The authors conclude: "The optical loss was measured to be less than 0.40 dB/cm" and "This result indicated that the E-FPAESI is a good candidate as a core material for the optical waveguide devices."

No application performance data is reported for FPAESI. Waveguide fabrication and loss measurements were performed using E-FPAESI films: “To fabricate single-mode optical waveguides… E-FPAESI film was spin-coated…” and “The propagation losses… are less than 0.42 and 0.40 dB/cm… This result indicated that the E-FPAESI is a good candidate as a core material for the optical waveguide devices.” Refractive indices are also given only for E-FPAESI.

HBPS-(PTFEMA-b-PPEGMA)27 forms all-solid-state polymer electrolyte membranes with LiTFSI ("The polymer electrolyte membranes composed of the fluorine-containing star-branched polymer HBPS-(PTFEMA-b-PPEGMA)27 and LiTFSI are prepared..."). Ionic conductivity: "The highest room temperature ionic conductivity of 2.4×10^-5 S cm^-1 can be achieved when the molar ratio of [EO] to [Li] is 30." Also: "presents the maximum ionic conductivity of 4.1×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 80 °C" for EO/Li=30. Lithium-ion transference number: Table 2 gives tLi+ = 0.26 at 60 °C. Electrochemical window: "The oxidative decomposition potential could reach 4.9 V vs. Li/Li+ at ambient temperature (25 °C)." A Li/SPE/LiFePO4 cell using this SPE delivered 147 mAh g^-1 at 0.1C and 60 °C, evidencing its application as a lithium battery electrolyte.

FPE-6 is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. The LiFePO4/FPE-6/Li coin cell shows the highest discharge capacity of 154.2 mAh/g at 0.1C ("The LiFePO4/FPE-6/Li cell has the highest discharge capacity at 154.2 mAh g−1, and the coulombic efficiency in the first cycle is 91.92%"). Long-term performance with FPE-6 is also reported: at 0.5C, ~99.9% CE and 86.5% capacity retention after 424 cycles (~1350 h). Rate capability with FPE-6: 149.3→103.9 mAh/g from 0.2C to 3C, recovering to 148.0 mAh/g at 0.2C with 99.13% retention after 50 cycles. Symmetric Li/FPE-6/Li cells exhibit the lowest interface resistance (426.9 Ω), minimal polarization (~92 mV among FPE-x), and stable cycling over 1000 h. Electrolyte metrics specific to FPE-6 include LTN of 0.46, ESW of 5.0 V, and ionic conductivity 2.96×10−4 S/cm at room temperature.

PE-6 is the reference non-fluorinated polymer electrolyte used as the electrolyte in lithium batteries. Evidence: (1) “the calculated lithium ion transference number (LTN) of FPE-6 is 0.46… higher than 0.28 of PE-6 (Fig. S18).” (2) “the Li/FPE-6/Li cell can stably cycle over 1000 h… and it is much better than the reference Li/PE-6/Li cell, which exhibits unstable stripping/plating process and its polarization voltage increases much above 475 h.” (3) Full cells: “the cycling performance of LiFePO4/FPE-6/Li and LiFePO4/PE-6/Li cell (A)… the LiFePO4/FPE-6/Li has better battery cycling performance than LiFePO4/PE-6/Li.” These confirm PE-6’s role as a lithium battery polymer electrolyte (target species: Li+).

FPDE (fluorinated polymer deep eutectic electrolyte) is used as a lithium metal battery electrolyte. Its ionic conductivity at the optimized HFBA:DES volume ratio (1:3) is 0.97 mS cm^-1. FPDE shows a wide electrochemical window up to 4.8 V and a Li+ transference number of 0.58. In Li/FPDE/Li symmetric cells it maintains stable cycling for ~6000 h and achieves a critical current density of 1 mA cm^-2. In Li/FPDE/LFP full cells at 0.2C, the initial discharge capacity is 150.6 mAh g^-1 (96.7% initial CE) and 121.1 mAh g^-1 after 600 cycles (80.4% retention); rate capacities are 155.4, 151.8, and 146.3 mAh g^-1 at 0.1, 0.2, and 0.5C, recovering to 156.5 mAh g^-1 upon returning to 0.1C. Note: the 1.7 mS cm^-1 conductivity refers to the DES (not FPDE).

PVDF (Dense) membrane was used as a GPE matrix after immersion in 1 M LiClO4/PC and measured in SS/GPE/SS cells at 25 °C. Table I lists PVDF (Dense) with porosity 0% and electrolyte uptake 27%. The Results state: "The ion conductivities of the GPEs for membranes with dense, finger-like, and honeycomb-like morphology structures were calculated to be 0.1 mS cm−1, 1.7 mS cm−1 and 0.9 mS cm−1, respectively."

Relevant to PVDF (LIPS):
- Morphology and identification: “The PVDF membranes prepared via LIPS revealed finger-like morphology structure” and were used as GPE matrices by soaking in 1 M LiClO4/PC.
- Ionic conductivity (finger-like/LIPS): “The ion conductivities of the GPEs for membranes with dense, finger-like, and honeycomb-like morphology structures were calculated to be 0.1 mS cm−1, 1.7 mS cm−1 and 0.9 mS cm−1, respectively.”
- Electrochemical stability (finger-like/LIPS): “GPE with a honeycomb-like cellular structure exhibited anodic stability up to 5.0 V… while the GPE with finger-like morphology anodic stability was as high as 4.4 V.”
- Porosity and uptake (LIPS): Table I lists PVDF (LIPS) porosity 53% and electrolyte uptake 490%.
- Battery performance (LiMn2O4/GPE/Li): “The cell assembled with this GPE [VIPS] maintained… 75 mAh g−1 at 1C, higher than the GPE with a finger-like morphology structure (26 mAh g−1).”

PVDF (VIPS) refers to the PVDF membrane prepared via the vapor-induced phase inversion (VIPS) method and used as a gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) host. The paper reports: "The ion conductivities of the GPEs for membranes with dense, finger-like, and honeycomb-like morphology structures were calculated to be 0.1 mS cm^-1, 1.7 mS cm^-1 and 0.9 mS cm^-1, respectively." (honeycomb-like corresponds to VIPS). It further shows battery use: "the PVDF GPE with cellular morphology (VIPS) showed stable reversible capacities (158 mA hg^-1, 140 mA hg^-1, 120 mA hg^-1, and 75 mA hg^-1)." Electrochemical stability for VIPS PVDF is also given: "GPE with a honeycomb-like cellular structure exhibited anodic stability up to 5.0 V versus Li+/Li." Table I lists PVDF (VIPS) porosity 34% and electrolyte uptake 231%, confirming this specific sample’s morphology-performance linkage.

For PVDF/PVDF-g-PSSA (50/50): Table I lists Porosity = 37% and Electrolyte uptake = 371%. Figure 5A reports ionic conductivity values for blends 100:0, 70:30, and 50:50 as 0.9, 1.9, and 2.8 mS cm^-1, respectively. The text notes cracks for the 50:50 membrane and that 70:30 was selected for further electrochemical analyses; thus no battery discharge capacity, t_Li+, or stability window was reported for 50:50.

Electrolyte use for Li+ transport is directly discussed. Pure PFPE shows very low ionic conductivity: “...has 6 orders of magnitude difference in conductivity as compared to pure PFPE from 1.55 × 10^-11 to 2.26 × 10^-5 S/cm at 60 °C,” identifying 1.55×10^-11 S/cm at 60 °C for pure PFPE (Conclusions). Additionally, pure PFPE r=0.05 electrolyte did not support Li|Li cycling and quickly shorted after onset, indicating poor interfacial performance as a battery electrolyte (Fig. 6a description). The T1 for pure PFPE (~3 s) further indicates low Li+ mobility relative to blends (Fig. 4a), consistent with poor ionic conduction.

FPCE is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Key performance reported: Li-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.61 (Figure 3b). Additional FPCE metrics supporting its electrolyte application include ionic conductivity of 0.33 mS cm^-1 at −10 °C and 1.13 mS cm^-1 at 30 °C (Figures 3a, S17); Li|FPCE|Cu Coulombic efficiency 98.25% after 300 cycles at 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Figure 3e); Li|FPCE|Li symmetric operation >1100 h (Figure 3f); LFP|FPCE|Li: rate capacities 169.2, 158.5, 146.0, 129.7, 104.1 mAh g^-1 at 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5 C and 5000 cycles at 10 C with average decay 0.00448% per cycle (Figure 4a,d); NCM811|FPCE|Li capacity retention 98.62% after 100 cycles at 1 C within 2.8–4.5 V (Figure 4f); 1 Ah NCM811|FPCE|Gr pouch: 0.78 Ah initial at RT, 0.62 Ah after 600 cycles at 0.5 C (Figure 5b), retained 0.64 Ah after 400 cycles at 60 °C (Figure 5d); nail penetration showed negligible temperature rise for FPCE pouch vs liquid electrolyte (Figure 5h–i).

P(VEC-co-TFEMA) serves as the polymer matrix of the solid polymer electrolyte (FPCSPE). "FPCSPE 3-40 exhibited the highest σLi+ of 5.02×10^-5 S cm^-1 at room temperature" and "The tLi+ value of FPCSPE 3-40 ... reached ~0.44 at room temperature." It operates as an electrolyte in Li||FPCSPE||NCM811 batteries and Li||FPCSPE||Li symmetric cells, confirming its application as a battery electrolyte.

P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI is used as an all-solid-state sodium polymer electrolyte. Reported performance: ionic conductivity at room temperature = 2.23 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 (Fig. 3a); Na+ transference number t_Na+ = 0.7 (Fig. 3b); activation energy Ea = 0.279 eV (Fig. 3c); electrochemical stability window ≈ 5.55 V (Fig. 3f). In Na//Na symmetric cells it sustained stable cycling for 4690 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 with small polarization (~230 mV) and lower nucleation overpotential (75 mV) (Fig. 4a). In NVP/P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI/Na cells: 96.59% capacity retention (99.1 mAh/g) after 200 cycles at 0.1C and CE > 99.5% (Fig. 5a); rate capacities: 102.2, 98.1, 81.4, 39.4 mAh/g at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1C (Fig. 5b).

The material P-co-F (1:2)-SN-NaTFSI is explicitly prepared as a solid polymer electrolyte variant (“the molar ratios of PEGMEA to TFEMA (namely, 1:2 and 2:1) were adjusted, which are designated as P-co-F (1:2)-SN-NaTFSI and P-co-F (2:1)-SN-NaTFSI”). Its application is assessed via EIS among different ratios: “Specifically, when the ratio of PEGMEA to TFEMA was 1:2, the insulated TFEMA increased the impedance, inhibiting the movement of sodium ions.” No numeric conductivity or other quantitative electrochemical metrics are reported for the 1:2 sample (only comparative EIS behavior is discussed), so capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are left null.

The material P-co-F (2:1)-SN-NaTFSI is explicitly prepared as an all-solid-state polymer electrolyte variant: "the molar ratios of PEGMEA to TFEMA (namely, 1:2 and 2:1) were adjusted, which are designated as P-co-F (1:2)-SN-NaTFSI and P-co-F (2:1)-SN-NaTFSI, respectively." Electrochemical tests evaluate such films as ASSPE: "The ASSPE was sandwiched between two steel sheets... to evaluate its ionic conductivity" and Fig. 3a presents "EIS spectra of ASSPE with various PEGMEA and TFEMA molar ratios." However, no numerical performance values specific to the 2:1 sample are reported in text/captions; quantitative metrics (e.g., ionic conductivity, transference number, cycling) are given for the optimal 1:1 composition, so no numeric extraction is provided for the 2:1 sample.

Electrochemical stability window (ESW) reported for PEGMEA-NaTFSI: "The ESW of the P-co-F-NaTFSI electrolyte surpasses 5.62 V, in contrast to the 4.53 V observed for PEGMEA-NaTFSI (Figure S10)." Additional related performance context: "P-co-F-SN-NaTFSI displays a lower nucleation overpotential (75 mV) compared to PEGMEA-NaTFSI (110 mV) (Figure S11)." Also: "The sodium-ion transference number (t_Na+) for P-co-F-NaTFSI stands at 0.41, twofold higher than that of PEGMEA-NaTFSI (Figure S8b-c)."

PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI was used as the solid electrolyte in Na/PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI/Na symmetric cells and in NVP/PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI/Na full cells. Symmetric cell: “a short circuit occurred after 536 h of charging/discharging cycling for Na/PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI/Na battery” at 0.1 mA/cm2 (Fig. 4a). Full cell: “NVP/PEGMEA-SN-NaTFSI/Na delivers a high initial discharge capacity of 101.3 mAh/g … ultimately undergoes a direct short circuit at the 67th cycle” at 0.1C (Fig. 5a).

Performance for P-co-F-NaTFSI (without SN) is explicitly reported: '...representing an order of magnitude increase in comparison to P-co-F-NaTFSI (2.63 × 10^-5 S cm^-1, by Figure S8a).' Additionally: 'The sodium-ion transference number (t_Na+) for P-co-F-NaTFSI stands at 0.41...' and 'The ESW of the P-co-F-NaTFSI electrolyte surpasses 5.62 V (Figure S10)'.

PPP is used as a PFPE derivative additive in DOL-based electrolytes for lithium metal batteries. Reported performance with PPP: (1) Li | DOL-PPP | Li shows Li+ transference number t+ = 0.61 (vs 0.42 without PPP) (Fig. 3b,c). (2) Li | DOL-PPP | Li maintains stable cycling overpotential for 1800 h at 0.2 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 3g) and remains stable for 500 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 3h). (3) Electrochemical stability window extends to 4.2 V (Fig. S13). (4) LFP | DOL-PPP | Li after 150 cycles at 0.5C maintains specific capacity of 136.6 mAh g^-1 with 94.1% capacity retention (capacity decay 0.039% per cycle) (Fig. 6a). (5) Internal resistance of LFP | DOL-PPP | Li changes slightly from 47 Ω to 53 Ω (Fig. 6d,e). All these data are directly attributed to cells containing PPP additive.

Directly with DOL-PPP electrolyte: (1) LFP | DOL-PPP | Li maintained a specific capacity of 136.6 mAh g^-1 after 150 cycles at 0.5C with 94.1% capacity retention (Section 3.5: "After 150 cycles at 0.5C ... maintained a specific capacity of 136.6 mAhg^-1 ... capacity retention of 94.1%"). (2) Li | DOL-PPP | Li showed long-term stability: "the polarization voltage ... remained stable even after 1800 h of cycling" at 0.2 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 3g). (3) Electrolyte transport improved: "The t+ for the Li | DOL-PPP | Li cell reaches 0.61" (Fig. 3b,c). These results are the performance of cells employing the DOL-PPP electrolyte (3 wt% PPP in 1 M LiFSI/DOL).

FBCP_20 is one of the synthesized PFMA-b-PEO-b-PFMA triblock copolymers (Table 1) and its corresponding electrolyte is labeled FBCPE_20: “the PFMA-b-PEO-b-PFMA macromolecules are abbreviated as ‘FBCP_X’ … the corresponding triblock copolymer electrolytes are labeled as ‘FBCPE_X’.” The FBCPE films were prepared by doping LiTFSI at [EO]/[Li] = 20:1 and casting 30 μm films (Section 2.4). The study assembled LiFePO4/FBCPEs/Li batteries (Section 2.5) and measured ionic conductivities for the three FBCPEs (Fig. 6a). Lithium-ion transport (t_Li+) and electrochemical stability are discussed for FBCPEs; however, quantitative electrochemical performance (e.g., t_Li+, ESW, cycling) is only reported for FBCPE_30. No specific numeric performance metrics are provided for FBCPE_20, so values are left null.

FBCP_30 is used as a solid polymer electrolyte (FBCPE_30) after doping with LiTFSI ([EO]/[Li] = 20:1). Reported performance: ionic conductivity 2.68×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 70°C (Fig. 6a); lithium-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.41 (Fig. 6b). Additional context: ESW up to 4.9 V (Fig. 6d); Li/FBCPE_30/Li stable over 600 h with ~0.04 V overpotential at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and 70°C (Fig. 6e); LFP cell delivers 157.1 mAh g^-1 at 0.2C, 151.3 mAh g^-1 after 250 cycles (95.8% retention) at 70°C (Fig. 7a,c).

FBCP_40 is one of the fluorine-containing triblock copolymers used to form solid polymer electrolytes with LiTFSI: “The PFMA-b-PEO-b-PFMA macromolecules are abbreviated as ‘FBCP_X’… the corresponding triblock copolymer electrolytes are labeled as ‘FBCPE_X’.” The electrolyte films are prepared at [EO]/[Li] = 20:1: “The FBCPEs film was prepared… LiTFSI… [EO]/[Li] = 20:1… film (30 μm) was successfully produced.” FBCPE_40 is explicitly characterized as an electrolyte: “AFM images of (a) FBCPE_20, (b) FBCPE_30, and (c) FBCPE_40… Such nanocomposite morphology… is desirable for SPEs.” Ionic conductivity was measured for all three FBCPEs, but only a numeric value for FBCPE_30 is given: “The temperature-dependent ionic conductivities of the three FBCPEs… are plotted… FBCPE_30 shows a higher ionic conductivity (2.68 × 10^-4 S cm^-1) at 70 °C than FBCPE_20 and FBCPE_40.” No specific electrochemical performance values (conductivity, ESW, tLi+, or cell cycling) are reported for FBCPE_40.

Direct evidence for P-FEM@Li in batteries and specific energy:
- "The Li||LFP full cells were assembled to further evaluate the practical application potential of P-FEM@Li anode." 
- "Eventually, Li||NCM811 pouch cells with high energy density were assembled to further verify P-FEM@Li's practicality and effectiveness. The pouch cells achieved a specific energy of 428 Wh kg^{-1} and P-FEM@Li||NCM811 pouch cell maintained a capacity retention of 73% after 175 cycles at 0.2C charge/0.5C discharge condition..." 
Additional performance (context, not encoded in fields):
- "The symmetric cell using P-FEM@Li anode could stably cycle for more than 4400 h with a low polarization voltage at 1 mA cm^{-2} and 1 mAh cm^{-2}." 
- "The initial discharge capacity of P-FEM@Li||LFP is 146 mAh g^{-1}... After 600 cycles, the discharge capacity of P-FEM@Li||LFP was 127.7 mAh g^{-1} and retained 87.2%."

Li||Cu cells using P-FEM@Cu for Li deposition/stripping at 1 mA_cm^-2 and 1 mAh_cm^-2 showed almost no attenuation of CE within 270 cycles (Fig. 3d). The average CE of Li||P-FEM@Cu was 99.78% after 100 cycles (Fig. 3e).

FP-GPE is used as a gel polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries (LMBs). Key performance metrics directly reported for FP-GPE include: ionic conductivity of 4.45 mScm^-1 at 25 °C (Figure 1b/S7); oxidation stability with no remarkable oxidation up to 4.9 V vs Li/Li+ (Figure 1a); NCM622|FP-GPE|Li cells deliver 165.1 mAh g^-1 at 1 C and retain 80.1% after 1200 cycles (71.5% after 1500 cycles) (Figure 1f/Table 1); NCM811|FP-GPE|Li shows 181.8 mAh g^-1 with 86.0% retention after 1000 cycles (Figure 1g/Table 1); LCO|FP-GPE|Li shows 152.7 mAh g^-1 at 1 C with 79.7% retention after 1500 cycles (Figure S9/Table 1); LFP|FP-GPE|Li retains 93.6% after 4000 cycles at 2 C (Figure S10/Table 1). Li||Li symmetric cells with FP-GPE show CCD of 4.5 mA cm^-2 and stable overpotential for 700 h (Figure S13). High-voltage operation: LNCMO|FP-GPE|Li achieves 231.2 mAh g^-1 at 1 C with 84.5% retention after 100 cycles at 4.6 V (Figure 3a). Practical cells: full cell with 20.84 mg cm^-2 NCM622 attains 3.17 mAh cm^-2 with 85.2% retention after 100 cycles at 0.3 C (Figure 3b); FP-GPE–based 18650 delivers 1.0 Ah and 158.5 Wh kg^-1 with 95.0% retention after 30 cycles (Figure 3f). Low-temperature performance: 1.12 mScm^-1 at −25 °C and NCM622|FP-GPE|Li retains 78.4% after 200 cycles (Figure 4b/S27).

P-0.5 is only referenced as part of materials characterization and labeling, without per-sample application performance. Evidence: “e) FT-IR spectra of PVDF-HFP, P-0.5, P-0.6, P-0.7, P-0.8, and P-0.8-FEC…” (Figure 1e caption); “The solid electrolyte obtained was labeled as P-0.5, P-0.6, P-0.7, P-0.8, and P-0.8-FEC.” (Experimental Section). All reported performance metrics (ionic conductivity, cycling stability, etc.) are for P-0.8 or P-0.8-FEC; none are given for P-0.5.

P-0.6 appears only in the FT-IR comparison list: "e) FT-IR spectra of PVDF-HFP, P-0.5, P-0.6, P-0.7, P-0.8, and P-0.8-FEC..." indicating it is a DES-modified solid-state electrolyte. All reported electrochemical metrics (e.g., ionic conductivity 0.313 mS cm−1, activation energy, LSV up to 4.6 V, Li/Li symmetric cycling 500 h, Li+ transference number 0.435, cell cycling and rate performance) are given for P-0.8 or P-0.8-FEC, not for P-0.6. Thus only the application context (Li+ conduction in solid-state batteries) can be assigned; no P-0.6-specific performance values are provided.

P-0.7 is only mentioned as part of the FT-IR spectra set (Figure 1e) alongside P-0.5/0.6/0.7/0.8/0.8-FEC, with no specific application tests or performance values reported for P-0.7. Reported electrolyte/battery metrics and stability data are for P-0.8 or P-0.8-FEC (e.g., ionic conductivity 0.313 mS cm^-1 for P-0.8; cycling and stability for P-0.8-FEC). Any plots that may include P-0.7 do not provide numeric readouts; therefore, no application data are extracted for P-0.7.

P-0.8 is a DES-modified PVDF-HFP solid electrolyte used for Li+ transport in solid-state Li-metal batteries. Reported metrics specific to P-0.8 include: room-temperature ionic conductivity of 0.313 mS cm^-1 ("At room temperature, the ionic conductivity of P-0.8 reached 0.313 mScm^-1"), and a lower activation energy for Li+ diffusion of 0.117 eV compared with P-L 0.183 eV ("the activation energies of P-0.8 (0.117 eV)... are lower than that of P-L (0.183 eV)"). Post-cycling morphology indicates improved, thin and dense Li deposition with P-0.8 relative to P-L ("the addition of DESs improves the state of lithium deposition after cycling and enables the formation of a thin, dense layer of deposition on the surface"). Numeric long-cycle cell performance and transference number values provided in the paper pertain to P-0.8-FEC, not P-0.8, and are therefore omitted.

Direct performance of P-0.8-FEC in Li/P-0.8-FEC/LFP cells is reported: "By testing the P-0.8-FEC for 500 cycles at 25°C, the capacity after cycling was 139.9 mAh g^-1 with 97.8% capacity retention (Figure 5a,b)." Additional, related metrics for the same material include electrochemical window and stability: "P-0.8-FEC exhibits better electrochemical stability at 4.6 V (Figure 3d)"; symmetric Li/P-0.8-FEC/Li stability: "cycled stably for 500 h at a current density of 0.1 mA cm^-2 without significant short-circuiting (Figure 3e)"; Li+ transference number: "P-0.8-FEC exhibits a lithium-ion mobility number of 0.435"; safety: "Self-extinguishing DEEs are produced 0.3 s after ignition of P-0.8-FEC at room temperature over an open flame (Figure 2d)." Rate capacities with P-0.8-FEC are also given: "166.3, 156.6, 147.4, 131.7, 113.8, and 82.9 mAh g^-1 at 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 1, 2, and 5 C, respectively (Figure 5b)." Other schema fields (selectivity, breakthrough, regeneration) are not applicable or not reported for this material.

Material P-L is a PVDF-HFP/LiTFSI solid electrolyte control: "to obtain the solid electrolyte, named P-L." In Li/Li symmetric cells at 0.1 mA cm^-2, "the P-L short-circuited after only 30 h (Figure 3e)." P-L also shows higher activation energy than DES-modified samples: "activation energies ... P-L (0.183 eV)." Ionic conductivity at room temperature for P-0.8 is ~2x P-L (0.313 mS cm^-1), indicating lower conductivity for P-L, but no exact P-L value is reported, so it is not extracted.

PFMC-SPE is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Reported metrics: “The ionic conductivity of PFMC-SPE at 25 °C can be calculated to be 1.0 mS/cm” (EIS, SS/SPE/SS). “The tLi+ of PFMC-SPE is 0.57.” Electrochemical stability window shows polymerization current rises over ~5 V (LSV), and PFMC-SPE exhibits CCD of 0.8 mA/cm2 and stable Li/Li symmetric cycling (∼20 mV overpotential for up to 800 h at 0.1 mA/cm2), supporting its application as a battery electrolyte.

FMC-SPE is evaluated as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li-metal battery configurations. Reported metrics specific to FMC-SPE include: ionic conductivity 1.1 mS/cm at 25 °C (SS/SPE/SS, EIS); Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.21 (Li/SPE/Li); oxidation onset at 4.25 V (Li/SPE/SS, LSV); leakage current >100 μA at 4.3 V in floating test (Li/SPE/NMC622); critical current density (CCD) = 0.5 mA cm^-2; in Li symmetric cycling at 0.1 mA cm^-2 the overpotential rose from ~50 mV and increased to ~5 V after ~50 h.

NFMC-SPE-specific results reported: (1) Ionic conductivity: “The ionic conductivity of PFMC-SPE at 25 °C can be calculated to be 1.0 mS/cm, which is much higher than that of the NfMC-SPE (0.5 mS/cm at 25 °C).” (Section 3.3, Figs. 3a, S6). (2) Li-ion transference number: “The tLi+ of PFMC-SPE is 0.57 … higher than that of FMC-SPE (tLi+ = 0.21) and NFMC-SPE (tLi+ = 0.19) in Figs. S7 and S8.” (Section 3.3). Additional NFMC-SPE performance context (not encoded in fields): oxidation onset at 4.7 V from LSV (“oxidation occurred at 4.25 V (FMC-SPE) and 4.7 V (NFMC-SPE).” Fig. 3c), higher leakage current in floating test before 4.7 V (Fig. 3d), CCD = 0.35 mA/cm^2 (Fig. 4c), and Li||Li symmetric short-circuit after ~32 h at 0.1 mA/cm^2 (Fig. 4e).

FPASEI is used as an artificial SEI on Li metal anodes to improve Li+ transport and suppress dendrites. Key performance: electronic resistivity ~2.17×10^4 Ω·cm and ionic conductivity 0.87×10^−3 S·cm^−1 (Fig. 2d,e). Exchange current density I0 increases to 0.274 mA·cm^−2 (vs. bare-Li 0.127 mA·cm^−2) (Fig. 2f). Li+ transference number increases from 0.31 to 0.77 with FPASEI (Fig. 2h,i).
