Results: For FPEC80/50, the conductivity at 30 °C is 107.6 μS/cm (i.e., 1.076×10^{-4} S/cm), and it is selected for further cell tests. The electrolyte is used in Li/FPEC/LFP full cells, confirming its role as a solid polymer electrolyte.

Nafion 211 is used as the commercial PFSA membrane baseline in VRFB tests. The paper states: "Even though the ion-selective ultrathin PFSA ionomer layer... performed better in EE at 200 mA cm^-2, achieving approximately 76% EE compared to the commercial Nafion 211 (EE ~73%)." It also notes: "The red-dashed lines are efficiencies of the commercial PFSA membrane (N211) at each current density." These establish N211’s VRFB energy efficiency (~73%) at 200 mA cm^-2.

For PEO20-LiDFOB-40 wt% EMImTFSI, ionic conductivity is 1.85 × 10^-4 S/cm at 30 °C (Table 1; also noted in Conductivity studies). At 23 °C, conductivity is 9.44 × 10^-6 S/cm. As electrolyte in Li/PEO-LiDFOB-40 wt% IL/LiFePO4 cells (2.5–4.0 V vs Li+/Li, 0.1 C, 25 °C), the initial discharge capacity is 155 mAh/g and 134.2 mAh/g after 50 cycles.

PH denotes the electrospun PVDF-HFP fibrous membrane used as the separator/GPE matrix and was directly tested in Li|PH|Li, Li|PH|Cu, and LFP|PH|Li cells. Key PH-related results: ionic conductivity at room temperature = 3.83×10^-3 S_cm^-1 (Figure 2h); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.46 (from Figure S4, text); electrochemical window is lower than NVPH (Figure 2i/S5; no explicit PH value given). Symmetric Li|PH|Li cycling: at 0.1 mA_cm^-2, polarization fluctuates at ~510 h due to short circuit; at 0.5 mA_cm^-2, failure at 120 h (Figure 3a,b). Li|PH|Cu at 0.5 mA_cm^-2: nucleation and growth overpotentials 141.0 mV and 86.8 mV; initial CE 88.6% and average CE 89.0% over 100 cycles (Figure 3c,d). LFP|PH|Li cycling: at 0.2 C, capacity retention 80.4% after 220 cycles with average CE 96.6% (Figure 4d); EIS interfacial impedance 125 Ω (initial), 50 Ω (after 50 cycles), 58 Ω (after 100 cycles) (Figure 4f).

LFP|NVPH|Li cell performance: "After 250 cycles at 80 °C, the discharge specific capacity of the LFP|NVPH|Li cell at 1 C is 149.7 mA h g−1 with a capacity retention of 91.8%." Also, rate capacities: "the Li|NVPH|LFP cell delivers specific discharge capacities of 157.1, 155.8, 150.6, 143.2, and 130.1 mA h g−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 C, respectively."

Direct performance for PETEA-HFA-TCGG-PAN GPE (3:2): “GPE 3:2 at 30°C exhibits a satisfactory σ (4.06×10−3 S cm−1), higher than that of GPE 1:1 (3.59×10−3 S cm−1).” Additional related evidence for the same sample: “Owing to the immobility of the polymer skeleton, GPE 3:2 and GPE 1:1 both show excellent tLi+ of 0.87 and 0.73, respectively” and “the decomposition potentials … with values of 5.35 V and 4.8 V respectively.” Cell-level outcomes using this GPE: “Assembled cells using GPE 3:2 can stably operate … for 300 cycles at 2 and 3C with the capacity retention of 84% and 70%, respectively,” “LFP|GPE 3:2|Li can stably cycle for 1000 cycles at 3C with a capacity retention rate of 76%,” and symmetric Li|GPE 3:2|Li shows “overpotential … about 9 mV … before 1100 h.”

For GPE 1:1: (i) ionic conductivity: "GPE 3:2 at 30°C exhibits a satisfactory σ (4.06×10−3 S cm−1), higher than that of GPE 1:1 (3.59×10−3 S cm−1)." (Fig. 5a–c). (ii) Li+ transference number: "GPE 3:2 and GPE 1:1 both show excellent tLi+ of 0.87 and 0.73, respectively" (Fig. 5d,e). (iii) Electrochemical stability: "decomposition potentials of GPE 3:2 and GPE 1:1 at 30°C were ... 5.35 V and 4.8 V respectively" (Fig. 5f). Also reported for NCM811|GPE 1:1|Li: initial discharge specific capacity 204.67 mAh g−1, CE 89.19% (Fig. 6b).

CPE(PVAc) is used as a gel polymer electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Coin cells LiFePO4|CPE(PVAc)|Li show an initial discharge specific capacity of 135.4 mAh g^-1 at 2C and 25 °C with 97.6% capacity retention after 500 cycles (Fig. 5d,e; Conclusions). Additional performance: 157.5 mAh g^-1 at 1C and 55 °C with 80% retention after 400 cycles (Fig. 5f,g; Conclusions). Electrolyte properties supporting application include ionic conductivity ~1.47 mS cm^-1 at 30 °C (Fig. 3c,d; Conclusions), Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.75 (Fig. 4a,b), electrochemical stability window 4.9 V vs Li+/Li (Fig. 4c), and Li|CPE(PVAc)|Li symmetric-cell stability for 1500 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 with ~11 mV polarization (Fig. 4f; Conclusions).

SHSPE3 was used as the solid polymer electrolyte in LiFePO4/SHSPE3/Li coin cells at 60 °C. Reported discharge capacities: 163.9, 137.2, 121.6 and 104.7 mA h g−1 at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C and 1C, respectively (Fig. 4d). During long-term cycling at 0.2C and 60 °C, the discharge capacity increased to a maximum of 144.8 mA h g−1 with a coulombic efficiency >97%, and after 100 cycles remained 119.3 mA h g−1 (Fig. 4e; Conclusions). The optimized SHSPE3 used 20 wt% LiTFSI and showed the highest ionic conductivity among variants (text, Fig. 3a).

PVFH-PMC-PEGC is a gel polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. Its ionic conductivity reaches 2.70 mS cm^-1 (optimized 10 wt% PMC-PEGC), markedly higher than PVFH and other fillers (Fig. 3d). The Li+ transference number is 0.77 (Fig. 3b), and Li|PVFH-PMC-PEGC|Li shows stable cycling for 500 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 with ~13 mV overpotential (Section 3.4), supporting its electrolyte application. Exact quote: “At this optimized content, the ionic conductivity of PVFH-PMC-PEGC reaches 2.70 mScm^-1, which is 15 times higher than that of PVFH.”

PVFH is used as a gel polymer electrolyte baseline in Li metal batteries. Reported ionic conductivity at 25 °C is 0.19 mS cm^-1 (Fig. 3d: "PVFH has an ionic conductivity of 0.19 mScm^{-1} at 25 °C"). Additional PVFH-specific electrochemical outcomes include: Li|PVFH|Li symmetric cells are stable within 80 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 with ~80 mV overpotential and short-circuit at ~180 h (Sec. 3.4); in Li|PVFH|LFP, the 3C discharge capacity is 39.9 mAh g^-1 and after 200 cycles at 0.5C the capacity is 69.9 mAh g^-1 (53.2% of initial) (Fig. 5b,c).

CA/PVDF composite membrane used as gel polymer electrolyte (CA/PVDF-GPE) for lithium metal batteries: “The CA/PVDF-based GPE delivers the ionic conductivity of 1.82 mScm^-1 and Li+ transfer number of 0.506” and shows an electrochemical stability window ~4.8 V (Fig. 3a,d; Conclusion). Symmetric Li/CA-PVDF-GPE/Li cells exhibit stable stripping/plating over 300–350 h at 1 mA cm^-2 with 1 mAh cm^-2 (Fig. 4a; Conclusion). In LiFePO4/GPE/Li full cells, initial discharge 144.7 mAh g^-1 at 1C and 101.2 mAh g^-1 at 5C with 94.6% capacity retention after 400 cycles (Fig. 5a,d).

Electrochemical metrics for CA/PVDF-GPE as electrolyte: “The CA/PVDF-based GPE delivers the ionic conductivity of 1.82 mS cm−1 and Li+ transfer number of 0.506…”; LSV shows electrochemical stability window “about 4.8 V.” Symmetric Li/CA-PVDF-GPE/Li: stable stripping/plating “with no detectable failure in 350 h” at 1 mA cm−2, 1.0 mA h cm−2. Full cells (LiFePO4/GPE/Li) show initial discharge capacity 144.7 mA h g−1 at 1C, 150.8 mA h g−1 after 200 cycles; rate capacities 153.6→128.7 mA h g−1 from 0.2C to 5C; at 5C, 101.2 mA h g−1 with 94.6% capacity retention after 400 cycles. High-voltage cathodes: LFMP 153 mA h g−1 over 100 cycles; NCM811 178.6 mA h g−1 with 84.2% retention after 100 cycles.

ME91 is an ionogel polymer electrolyte sample. The paper states: "Comparing the ionic conductivity of all the electrolyte sample, it is found that σ(ME91) > σ(ME82) > σ(ME73)," indicating ME91 has the highest ionic conductivity among these samples, but no numeric value for ME91 is provided.

ME82 is used as an ionogel polymer electrolyte in LiFePO4/ME82/Li cells. Room-temperature ionic conductivity: "The room temperature ionic conductivity of ME82 is ... 1.19 mS cm−1. When the temperature rises to 85 °C, the conductivity rises to 2.85 mS cm−1." Electrochemical window and Li+ transport: "the electrochemical stability window (ESW) of ME82 is about 5 V"; "the tLi+ of ME82 still remains at 0.18". Interfacial stability: "after a 1000 h test period at room temperature and a current density of 0.1 mA cm−2 ... remained ... until the end of the test (1000 h)". Battery performance with ME82: at 25 °C and 0.5 C, "initial specific discharge capacity ... 151.0 mAh g−1 ... still as high as 148.5 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles"; at 55 °C and 0.5 C, "initial specific discharge capacity is 160.9 mAh g−1 with a capacity retention of 94.00% after 200 cycles".

ME73 is defined as one of the ionogel polymer electrolyte compositions: “According to the quality ratios of MPEGA/ETPTA, they were numbered as ME91, ME82, and ME73, respectively.” The only comparative result given is non-numeric: “Comparing the ionic conductivity of all the electrolyte sample, it is found that σ(ME91) > σ(ME82) > σ(ME73). Taking the mechanical strength and ionic conductivity of the electrolyte into comprehensive account, ME82 was selected as the further investigating object.” All detailed application/battery tests (e.g., LiFePO4/ME82/Li coin cells, ESW, tLi+, cycling) are reported only for ME82. No application performance metrics specific to ME73 are provided.

ME00 is explicitly described as the pure PVDF-HFP gel electrolyte ('the pure PVDF-HFP gel electrolyte ME00'), but all battery/cell performance measurements were conducted with ME82 (e.g., 'Measuring of the LiFePO4/ME82/Li Battery' and 'Battery Performance of LiFePO4/ME82/Li'). For ME00, only morphology, mechanical strength (47% elongation; 0.42 MPa), XRD and comparative thermal notes are given, with no cell-level application metrics reported.

Text states: "the calculated tLi+ of CPE-0, CPE-1, CPE-3, and CPE-5 are 0.39, 0.43, 0.48, and 0.45." Other specific performance values (e.g., ionic conductivity, electrochemical window, cycling) are reported for CPE-3 or CPE-0, not CPE-1, so they are not included.

Direct data for CPE-3: (i) Ionic conductivity: “CPE-3 had the highest ionic conductivity … up to 1.70 mS cm−1” (Fig. 2a); and specifically “the ionic conductivity of CPE-3 can reach about 1.88×10−3 S cm−1 at 30 °C and 3.99×10−3 S cm−1 at 80 °C” (Fig. 2c). (ii) Additional electrolyte performance linked to CPE-3: electrochemical window extended to 5 V (Fig. 2b); tLi+ = 0.48 (Fig. 5c); Li|CPE-3|Li symmetric cell stable for 1000 h at 0.1 mA cm−2 and 25 °C with ~70 mV polarization (Fig. 5g). (iii) Cell-level results using CPE-3: LiFePO4|CPE-3|Li at 25 °C shows initial 150.4 mAh g−1 at 1C and 88.7% retention after 500 cycles (Fig. 6g), rate capacities 165.6, 158.1, 149.9, 139.8, 129.8 mAh g−1 at 0.2C–3C (Fig. 6b); at 55 °C shows initial 160.3 mAh g−1 at 1C with 96.63% retention after 500 cycles and 80.2% after 1000 cycles (Fig. 7f). These support assigning CPE-3 as a Li+ battery electrolyte and using its ionic conductivity (1.88×10−3 S/cm at 30 °C) as the primary quantitative metric for the material.

CPE-5 (5 wt% LLZTO in PVDF-HFP) was tested in Li|CPE-5|Li symmetric cells for Li-ion transference number at room temperature using chronoamperometry/EIS with ΔV = 10 mV. The calculated t_Li+ values for CPE-0, CPE-1, CPE-3, and CPE-5 are 0.39, 0.43, 0.48, and 0.45, respectively (Fig. 5a–d). No specific battery discharge capacity or electrochemical stability window values were reported for CPE-5.

EVOH-graft-LiCPSI is synthesized as a single-ion polymer conductor (SIPE) and cast into a membrane (blended with PVDF-HFP) then swollen with EC/DMC to form a gel SIPE used as electrolyte/separator. Electrochemical data: ionic conductivity reported as 5.7×10^-5 S cm^-1 at 25°C (Conclusion) and shown vs. temperature (Fig. 6b); electrochemical stability window up to 4.8 V vs. Li/Li+ (Fig. 6c); Li+ transference number t+ = 0.88 from DC polarization/EIS (Fig. 6d); stable Li|SIPE|Li plating/stripping at 0.5 mA cm^-2 with ~19 mV polarization for 300 h and smooth Li surface post-cycling (Fig. 7a–c). In LiFePO4/Li half-cells using this gel SIPE, discharge capacities: 123 mA h g^-1 (0.2C), 115 (0.4C), 110 (0.6C), 106 (0.8C), 101 (1C), 96 (1.5C), 92 (2C), recover to 102 (1C), 84 (3C), 77 (4C), 72 (5C), with ~100% coulombic efficiency (Fig. 8a–b). Long-term cycling at 1C: ~100 mA h g^-1 initial, 95 mA h g^-1 after 500 cycles (95% retention) (Fig. 8c). These results demonstrate EVOH-graft-LiCPSI’s direct application as a lithium-ion battery electrolyte.

Li/PMS-PVDF-55 GPE/LFP rate performance: “The discharge specific capacities were: … 106.5 mAh g−1 (10 C).” (Fig. 4a). Supporting electrolyte properties for PMS-PVDF-55: “the PMS-PVDF membrane with a mass ratio of 5:5 exhibits the highest ionic conductivity of 5.62 mS cm−1.” and “The LSV curve exhibits no discernible oxidation peaks below 4.85 V (vs. Li+/Li).” Stable Li plating/stripping: “stable … during the 400-h cycling process of the symmetric cell.”

For TPP@PAN/PVDF-HFP GPE: ionic conductivity is 0.85 mS cm^-1 at room temperature (Figure S9); Li||Li symmetric cells exhibit stable cycling for 1000 h at 0.2 mA cm^-2 – 0.2 mAh cm^-2 (Figure 4b). Additional performance evidence directly tied to this GPE: Li+ transference number 0.877 (Figure 4a,S10); stable cycling 800 h at 1 mA cm^-2 with minimal polarization (Figure S11); charge-transfer resistance R_ct = 85 Ω after 1 cycle (Figure 4c); interfacial activation energy Ea = 26.6 kJ mol^-1 (Figure 4d); Li nucleation overpotential 66.0 mV (Figure 4e); exchange current density I0 = 0.650 mA cm^-2 (Figure 4f); Li||Cu plating/stripping Coulombic efficiency 99.1% at 0.5 mA cm^-2 (Figure 4h). In full cells: Li||LiFePO4 capacity retention 91.2% (remaining 158 mAh g^-1) after 200 cycles at 0.5 C; 82% after 200 cycles at 1 C (Figure 5d–g). High-rate cycling retention: 99.77% (2C), 97.75% (3C), 96.03% (5C) after 100 cycles (Figure S17, Table S2). Pouch cells with this GPE show flame resistance up to surface temperature ≈370 °C and self-healing after puncture (voltage recovers within ~100 s) (Figure 6a–d).

GTSSE (gradient trilayer solid-state electrolyte) is used as the electrolyte in Li-metal batteries. Reported performance directly for GTSSE includes: ionic conductivity 1.32×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 50 °C and 2.91×10^-5 S cm^-1 at 20 °C; electrochemical stability window up to 4.8 V vs Li+/Li; Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.38 at RT. In Li|GTSSE|Li symmetric cells: stable lithium plating/stripping for >2500 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (0.1 mAh cm^-2), with polarization increasing from 25 mV initially to 48 mV after 2500 h. Full-cell demonstrations: LFP|GTSSE|Li at 50 °C shows 142.5 mAh g^-1 initial at 0.5 C and 135.4 mAh g^-1 after 150 cycles (95% retention). NCM811|GTSSE|Li at 50 °C shows 83% capacity retention after 200 cycles at 0.5 C (137 mAh g^-1 from 164.7 mAh g^-1 initial) and 107.1 mAh g^-1 after 1000 cycles at 0.5 C; rate capacities of 188, 172, 165, 145, and 117 mAh g^-1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, and 1 C, respectively, recovering to 187 mAh g^-1 when returning to 0.1 C.

PVDF/PP SSPEs are used as solid-state polymer electrolytes in Li metal batteries. Key results directly reported: (i) Li+ conductivity: “PVDF/PP SSPEs achieve a high Li+ conductivity of 1.53×10^-4 S cm^-1 at room temperature.” (Conclusion; also Section 3). (ii) Electrochemical stability window: “PVDF/PP SSPEs can exhibit an apparent expanded electrochemical stability window up to 4.8 V.” (Fig. 3c text). (iii) Symmetric Li|PVDF/PP SSPEs|Li cycling: “the cells with PVDF/PP SSPEs can exhibit a long-term cycle stability up to 400 h nearly without voltage fluctuation” at 0.15 mA cm^-2 and 25 °C (Fig. 4a). (iv) Li+ transference number: t_Li+ = 0.24 (Fig. 3d text). (v) LFP full cell with PVDF/PP SSPEs at 25 °C: initial discharge capacity 134 mAh/g at 0.3C and 131 mAh/g after >180 cycles; capacity retention 97.8% after 180 cycles (Fig. 5a,c). Rate capability: 141, 136, 127, 112, 86, 41 mAh/g at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5C; reversible capacity 137 mAh/g after returning to 0.1C (Fig. 5b). (vi) High-voltage applicability: NCM523|PVDF/PP SSPEs|Li operated 2.8–4.3 V at 0.3C for 200 cycles steadily (Fig. S16).

The electrochemical performance of PCEE full cells was evaluated using LFP cathodes paired with lithium metal anodes... CNBAPCEE and HBA-PCEE showed initial discharge capacities of 109.60 mAhg^{-1} and 70.47 mAhg^{-1} at 0.5C, respectively, in the LFP full cells.

CNBA-PCEE is a polymer electrolyte used in Li metal battery full cells. Evidence: (1) Oxidative stability: "The oxidation potentials of CNBA-PCEE (4.9 V) and FBA-PCEE (5.1 V) surpass that of HBA-PCEE (4.6 V) (Fig. 3b)." (2) LFP full-cell capacity with CNBA-PCEE: "CNBA-PCEE and HBA-PCEE showed initial discharge capacities of 109.60 mAh g^-1 and 70.47 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C, respectively, in the LFP full cells." (3) Rate performance context including CNBA-PCEE: "Among different current densities, FBA-PCEE demonstrated superior performance, especially at 2C with 98.62 mAh g^-1 discharge capacity, significantly outperforming LFP||HBA-PCEE and LFP||CNBA-PCEE||Li cells (13.66 mAh g^-1 and 51.73 mAh g^-1, respectively) (Fig. 5c-e)." Test voltage range for LFP full cells: "The LFP cells containing polymer electrolyte were tested at various current densities within a voltage range of 2.7–4.0 V."

LFP||FBA-PCEE||Li showed an initial discharge specific capacity of 137.3 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C, with 85.38% retention after 200 cycles and 77.38% after 500 cycles (Fig. 5a). FBA-PCEE exhibited ionic conductivity 2.02 mS cm^-1 at 30 °C, t_Li+ = 0.75, and oxidation potential 5.1 V (Figs. S3–S6, Fig. 3b). In Li||Li symmetric cells at 0.1 mA cm^-2, polarization was ~35 mV at 200–210 h and ~45 mV at 1000–1010 h (Fig. 3c). With NCM-622, NCM-622||FBA-PCEE||Li delivered 151.61 mAh g^-1 at 0.1C with 91.84% retention after 50 cycles and 78.95% after 100 cycles (Fig. 5f).

PFIGE is explicitly used as an ionogel electrolyte and integrated anode coating in lithium metal batteries: “This PFIGE was directly cast onto Li-Cu composite foils to construct integrated anodes (donated as PFIGE@Li)” and evaluated in Li||Li and Li||NCM523 cells. Key electrolyte performance: “The PFIGE demonstrates an exceptional tLi+ value of 0.46,” and its ionic conductivity increases “from 0.17 mS m^-1 (0 °C) to 1.72 mS m^-1 (100 °C)” with low activation energy (Ea = 0.21 eV) and “ultralow electronic conductivity (1.55×10^-9 S cm^-1).” Interfacial stability: Li||Li cells with PFIGE “maintain ultrastable operation throughout 300 h cycling” whereas LE degrades after ~250 h. Full-cell performance (Li||NCM523): at 0.5C (2.8–4.3 V) PFIGE@Li retains “92.1% capacity over 300 cycles;” at 1C “88.5% after 100 cycles;” high-voltage “maintaining 78.9% capacity retention at 4.6 V;” and high-temperature “retaining 61.4% capacity after 100 cycles at 80 °C.” Electrochemical stability window is broadened to “4.8 V” for PFIGE. Safety and flame resistance relevant to electrolyte application: structural integrity “up to 300 °C,” cone calorimetry shows lower HRR (428 kW m^-2) and THR (42.43 MJ m^-2) than LE; pouch-cell ARC shows elevated onset/critical temperatures (T1 = 190.3 °C; T2 = 225.6 °C) and extended thermal runaway induction period (from 1200 to 3050 min), with non-ignition under direct flame/nail tests. These results demonstrate PFIGE’s role as a lithium-ion-conducting electrolyte/coating for Li metal batteries.

SIC_GPE-60 is a single-ion conducting gel polymer electrolyte used as both electrolyte and separator in Li metal coin cells and full cells. Key results directly reported for SIC_GPE-60 include: (i) Li-ion transference number LTN ≈ 0.85 ± 0.1 (Fig. 2b) with ionic conductivity 1.1×10^-5 S cm^-1 at 25 °C; (ii) stable critical current density up to 2.0 mA cm^-2 at 40 °C (Fig. 2d; Conclusions: “SIC_GPE-60 electrolyte stable up to a current density of 2 mA cm^-2”); (iii) homogeneous Li plating and resistance to dendritic growth in Li|SIC_GPE-60|Cu with 2 mAh cm^-2 deposition (Experimental 2.5; text and Fig. S9) and symmetric Li|SIC_GPE-60|Li plating/stripping from 50 to 25 °C with overvoltage ~160 mV (50 °C) to ~300 mV (25 °C) at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 3e); (iv) electrochemical stability showing main oxidation >5.0 V vs Li/Li+ with small current response around 4.0 V (Fig. 3b,c); (v) non-flammable behavior in flammability tests (Fig. 1d). Full-cell performance using SIC_GPE-60: LFP cell: ~140 mAh g^-1 at C/20 and ~120 mAh g^-1 at C/5 with 95% capacity retention after 50 cycles and 99.7% Coulombic efficiency at 40 °C (Fig. 4a,b). V2O5 cell: >200 mAh g^-1 at C/5 and >96% capacity retention after 50 cycles (Fig. 4c,d). NMC622 cell: 97% capacity retention and >99.0% Coulombic efficiency after 50 cycles (Fig. 4e,f). Highlights explicitly note: “SIC_GPE-60 resists dendritic lithium growth and shows homogeneous Li plating” and compatibility with LFP, V2O5, and NMC622.

SIC_GPE-80 is one of the SIC_GPE electrolyte formulations (Table 1) intended for lithium metal batteries. However, "while no plasticizer oozing is observed in the SIC_GPE-40 and SIC_GPE-60 samples, it is highly evident in the SIC_GPE-80 composition, as shown in Fig. S3. Therefore, this composition was excluded from further investigation." Consequently, no electrochemical performance data were reported for SIC_GPE-80.

Li/SPE/C-LiFePO4 half-cells were produced with [BMIM][SCN]/PVDF (40 wt% IL content). The cell with PVDF polymer exhibits the highest discharge capacity with about 148 mAh g−1 at C/8 and room temperature (Fig. 9a). Table 4 (This work) lists PVDF + [BMIM][SCN]: Conductivity 0.013 × 10−3 S/cm (25 °C) and Discharge capacity 148 (C/8). The composite shows no oxidation/reduction peaks between 0.0 and 5.0 V, indicating stability suitable for lithium-ion batteries (Fig. 8d).

Li/SPE/C-LiFePO4 half-cells using 40 wt% [BMIM][SCN]/PVDF-HFP showed a discharge capacity of 124 mAh/g at room temperature and C/8 (Fig. 9a; Table 4). Cycling at C/8 gave ~16% capacity fade over 50 cycles with Coulombic efficiency >98% (Fig. 9b). The composite SPE also exhibited room-temperature ionic conductivity of 0.15 mS/cm and an electrochemical stability window of 0–5 V (Fig. 8c,d; Conclusion).

PE 4 is a polymer electrolyte (24.7 wt% PVdF, 2.3 wt% LiODFB, 51.1 wt% EtMeImNTf2, 21.9 wt% TMS) used in Li|Li and LFP|PE|Li cells (Fig. 5 caption; Sections 2.2–3). Table 2 reports for PE 4: ionic conductivity 3.21 mS cm−1 at 25 °C and activation energy 19.58 kJ mol−1. In Li|PE 4|Li, after galvanostatic cycling, total impedance is as low as 120 Ω (≤320 Ω; Fig. 11b text), and without cycling stabilizes around ~370–450 Ω after six weeks (Fig. 11a text). The Li+ transference number for PE 4 is ~0.033 (determined from impedance; Section 3). In LFP|PE 4|Li, Table 3 shows discharge specific capacity after 50 cycles: 168 mAh g−1 at C/10, 119 mAh g−1 at C/2, and 115 mAh g−1 at 1C, with ~99–100% Coulombic efficiency (Table 3; Conclusion also reiterates these values).

Parent electrolyte is the [LiTFSA]/[SL] = 1/3 solution. Li-ion transference number t_Li+ for the parent electrolyte is explicitly stated as 0.56 at 30 °C: “the t_Li+ of the PDEGMA gel electrolyte (n = 1) (0.45) was lower than that of the parent electrolyte (0.56).” Figure 3(b) reports t_Li+ at 30 °C for the [LiTFA]/[SL] = 1/3 solution and gels.

No application performance metrics are reported for PPMA solution. The paper discusses PPMA only as a polymer solution for Raman analysis (e.g., “polymerized without a crosslinker to obtain a polymer solution… In the case of a solution of polymerized PMA (PPMA)… indicates that the carbonyl oxygen of PPMA was weakly coordinated to Li+”), while ionic conductivity, Li-ion transference number, electrochemical window, and battery discharge performance are provided for crosslinked PPMA gel electrolytes, not the PPMA solution.

In this paper, 'PTFPMA solution' (polymerized TFPMA without crosslinker in LiTFSA/SL) is only used for Raman analysis of solvation; no application performance metrics are reported for the solution. Quantitative application results (e.g., Li-ion transference number reaching 0.70 and battery rate capability) are for PTFPMA gel electrolytes with a crosslinked network, not for the solution. Evidence: '...polymerized without a crosslinker to obtain a polymer solution' and 'For the solution of polymerized TFPMA (PTFPMA), the TFSA peak position was independent of the polymer content... suggesting the polymer side chains did not coordinate or affect the solvation structure of Li+.' The ion transport and battery tests are explicitly for gel electrolytes [LiTFSA]/[SL]/[monomer unit] = 1/3/n.

Relevant to PDEGMA solution (polymerized DEGMA without crosslinker): "for the solution of polymerized DEGMA (PDEGMA), the TFSA peak clearly shifted toward a lower wavenumber... suggesting that this polymer enhanced the dissociation of CIPs and AGGs." and "the Li+ ion as a Lewis acid prefers to coordinate to the ether side chain... the fraction of free SL... was higher in the PDEGMA solution due to the complex formation of PDEGMA with Li+." No quantitative performance metrics (e.g., t_Li+) are reported for the PDEGMA solution; numeric t_Li+ is only provided for crosslinked gel electrolytes.

For PDEGMA gel electrolyte [LiTFSA]/[SL]/[DEGMA] = 1/3/1 (n=1), the Li-ion transference number t_Li+ is reported as 0.45 (Fig. 3b). Ionic conductivity trends are discussed but without explicit numeric values, and no full-cell performance for PDEGMA is provided. Therefore, only the application as a Li+ conducting gel electrolyte can be asserted; quantitative metrics required by the schema fields (capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration) are not applicable or not reported.

The paper reports: "the t_Li+ value increased with the polymer content and reached 0.70 for [LiTFSA]/[SL]/[TFPMA] = 1/3/2" (Fig. 3b, measured at 30 °C), for gel electrolytes intended for lithium-ion batteries.

Al-MCM41 is used as the mesoporous framework in an integrated composite quasi-solid-state electrolyte (CQSSE) formed by in-situ polymerization in its channels, termed Al-MCM41@HD. The resulting electrolyte shows an electrochemical stability window of ≈4.83 V (Figure 2i), ionic conductivity of 1.09×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 30 °C and Li+ transference number of 0.81 (Figure 3c–e), and enables Li|Al-MCM41@HD|Li symmetric cells to cycle stably for 4000 h at 0.2 mA cm^-2/0.2 mAh cm^-2 (Figure 4c). These data demonstrate the application of Al-MCM41 as a host in solid-state electrolyte for Li+ conduction in SSLMBs.

Direct performance for the PI/PV hybrid separator: Li+ conductivity 3.3 mS cm^-1 (Fig. 4d, “the PI/PV hybrid separator has high Li+ conductivity (3.3 mScm^-1)”), and Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.70 (Fig. 4e, “The Li+ transference number of the PI/PV hybrid separator is 0.70”). Related separator traits supporting the application include porosity 75% and electrolyte uptake 775% (Table 1), contact angle 0° (Fig. 4b), and electrochemical stability window 5.1 V (Fig. 5a). Cells using this separator show stable Li/Cu cycling CE ≈97% at 0.5 mA/cm^2, 2 mAh/cm^2 (Fig. 5d) and function after 140 °C storage for 1 h (10th-cycle discharge 1.4 mAh/cm^2, CE 94.5%; Fig. 6a).

Electrolyte role and transport species: The work evaluates LiBFSIE as a polymer gel electrolyte for Li metal/LFP batteries and Li symmetric cells; Li+ is the transported cation. Ionic conductivity: “A promising ionic conductivity of 2.0 × 10−4 S/cm is achieved at 35 °C.” Electrochemical stability: “No obvious oxidation peaks were witnessed, even at 4.8 V … indicating the excellent electrochemical stability of LiBFSIE.” Li+ transference number: “Using Bruce et al.'s equation, the tLi+ of LiBFSIE was calculated to be 0.93.” Symmetric Li cycling: “rectangle-like voltage profiles with an overpotential of 0.11 V … lasted for 300 cycles … at 0.1 mA/cm2.” Full-cell (LFP) performance enabled by LiBFSIE: “reversible capacity of 137.9 mAh/g at 0.1 C … 106.4 mAh/g at 1.5 C,” and “a promising average Coulombic efficiency of 99.95% … for 200 cycles.”

“the fabricated solid-state homogeneous F-PEO based electrolyte shows an impressive ionic conductivity of 3.32 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 (60 °C)” and “there is no decomposition below 4.3 V (vs. Li/Li+) for the F-PEO based electrolyte.” Also, application in cells: “The all-solid-state Li|Li symmetrical battery with the F-PEO/LiTFSI electrolyte showed an excellent cycling stability (2000 h) and a low average polarization potential (56 mV). The all-solid-state LFP|Li battery based on F-PEO/LiTFSI demonstrated a high capacity (151.0 mAh g^-1) and cycling stability after 500 cycles (capacity retention of 74.3%) at 0.5 C… at 1.0 C … 900 cycles.”

PEO/LiTFSI was used as the solid polymer electrolyte in LFP|PEO/LiTFSI|Li cells. Reported rate capacities: 148.9, 143.7, 136.8, and 121.4 mAh g^{-1} at 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, and 1.0 C, respectively. The Li|PEO/LiTFSI|Li symmetric cell showed ~130 h lifespan with ~128 mV average overpotential. LSV indicates decomposition at ~3.8 V vs Li/Li+. Ionic conductivity for PEO/LiTFSI is 2.06×10^{-4} S cm^{-1} at 60 °C.

F-PEO/LiTFSI is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in all-solid-state lithium metal batteries. Reported data tied to this material include: ionic conductivity 3.32×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C and stability window 4.3 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 2e,f). Symmetric Li|F-PEO/LiTFSI|Li cycled stably for 2000 h with an average polarization of 56 mV (Fig. 3a). In LFP|F-PEO/LiTFSI|Li cells: initial discharge capacity 151.0 mAh g^-1 and capacity retention 74.3% after 500 cycles at 0.5 C ("The LFP|F-PEO/LiTFSI|Li battery delivers an initial discharge capacity up to 151.0 mAh g^-1 ... remained at 112.2 mAh g^-1 after 500 cycles"). At 1.0 C: initial 141.5 mAh g^-1 and 99.8 mAh g^-1 after 900 cycles with CE >99.5%.

LS is a fluorinated polymer lithiophilic separator for lithium metal batteries. Key LS-specific results: Li||Li symmetric cell operated stably for >1000 h with a small overpotential of 34 mV at 1 mA cm^-2, 1 mAh cm^-2; moderate overpotential 180 mV at 5 mA cm^-2. NCM811|LS|Li: discharge specific capacities 202, 195, 184, 174, 160 mAh g^-1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2 C; at 0.5 C, initial 179 mAh g^-1 and 113 mAh g^-1 after 400 cycles (capacity retention 62%). LFP|LS|Li showed 92.9% capacity retention after 500 cycles at 1 C. LS shows Li+ conductivity 0.85 mS cm^-1 and transference number t_Li+ = 0.80; exchange current density j0 = 1.963 mA cm^-2; electrochemical stability up to 4.5 V; porosity 52% and electrolyte uptake 345%; contact angle ~0°.

Notably, the ionic conductivity of PZI-F is higher than that of PZI, and it still has an ionic conductivity of 1.39 × 10^−4 S cm^−1 even at −30 °C, which can meet the practical needs of applications. Linear scanning voltammetric (LSV) curves of Li||stainless steel batteries using PZI and PZI-F were shown in Fig. 3c, PZI has an electrochemical window of 4.7 V, while PZI-F has an electrochemical window of up to 5.3 V. The Li||Li symmetric cell of PZI-F has a very small polarisation voltage of 0.05 V and maintains a flat voltage plateau at this polarisation voltage for more than 1500 h. As shown in Fig. 6a, the Li||Li battery exhibits a relatively low overpotential of 78.4 mV after a short activation process and can operate stably up to 4,000 h at −10 °C. The t_Li+ values of PZI-F and PZI reached 0.83 and 0.73, respectively.

PZI is used as a solid-state electrolyte for Li-metal batteries: (i) At room temperature, PZI shows ionic conductivity of 7.11×10^-4 S_cm^-1 (Section 3.2). (ii) The Li+ transference number t_Li+ of PZI is 0.73 (Fig. 3b text). (iii) Linear sweep voltammetry gives an electrochemical stability window of 4.7 V for PZI (Fig. 3c text). (iv) In Li||Li symmetric cells with PZI at 0.2 mA_cm^-2 and 0.2 mAh_cm^-2, the polarization voltage is ~0.045 V, but the cell short-circuits after 547 h (Fig. 4a text). (v) In LFP||Li cells cycled with PZI, TEM shows a thick, inhomogeneous CEI (~12.3 nm), indicating interfacial instability (Fig. 5a text).

Room-temperature ionic conductivities: "the ionic conductivities of PZI, PZI-F, PZI-LiPF6 and PZI-LiTFSI are ... 7.11×10^-4 S cm^-1, 9.05×10^-4 S cm^-1, 5.24×10^-4 S cm^-1 and 5.59×10^-4 S cm^-1, respectively." The Li+ transference numbers: "significantly higher than those of PZI-LiPF6 (0.63), PZI-LiTFSI (0.62) (Fig. S9)."

PZI-LiPF6 is a single-salt solid polymer electrolyte prepared similarly to PZI-F but containing only LiPF6: "Preparation of PZI, PZI-LiTFSI, PZI-LiPF6: ... PZI-LiTFSI, PZI-LiPF6 contained only one corresponding lithium salt, respectively." Its ionic conductivity was explicitly reported: "At room temperature, the ionic conductivities of PZI, PZI-F, PZI-LiPF6 and PZI-LiTFSI are calculated to be 7.11×10^-4 S cm^-1, 9.05×10^-4 S cm^-1, 5.24×10^-4 S cm^-1 and 5.59×10^-4 S cm^-1, respectively." The Li+ transference number was also given for the single-salt sample: "... higher than those of PZI-LiPF6 (0.63), PZI-LiTFSI (0.62) (Fig. S9)." Temperature behavior specific to the single-salt system was noted: "After the temperature is raised to 100°C, the ionic conductivity of PZI-LiPF6 and PZI-LiTFSI of the single-salt system increase slowly below 100°C, and even a decrease in ionic conductivity is observed." No cell performance metrics (capacity/cycle life) were reported for PZI-LiPF6, hence capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are left null.

Specific to LFPP 1%-Li anode: Table 1 reports for LFPP 1%-Li at 0.5C: CE 100.0% and CR 77.7%. Additionally, symmetric LFPP-Li||LFPP-Li cell with LFPP 1%-Li exhibits the lowest and most stable over-potential at 0.038 V for over 1000 h at 0.5 mA cm^-2 with 1 mAh cm^-2 (Fig. 4 caption and text). Only 0.5C full-cell metrics (CE and capacity retention) and the symmetric-cell stability are explicitly tied to the 1% sample.

Table 1 reports that Li//LFP full cells using an LFPP 2%-Li anode have CE = 99.0% and capacity retention = 75.8% at 1C after rate performance. At 0.5C, the LFPP 2%-Li anode shows CE = 100.0% and capacity retention = 77.5%. The text also notes that cells with 5% and 2% precursor concentrations maintain 0.7–1.0 V overpotential for ~950 h in symmetric LFPP-Li||LFPP-Li cycling (0.5 mA cm^-2, 1 mAh cm^-2), but only the tabulated CE/retention values are recorded here for LFPP 2%-Li.

The work evaluates LFPP@PVDF as single-ion polymer electrolytes (SIPEs) for lithium batteries: “Ionic conductivity is a primary property for the practical application of polymer electrolytes in various batteries… a series of LFPP@PVDF SIPEs were prepared…” and conductivities were measured by EIS at room temperature. Specifically for this sample: “However, the ionic conductivity of LFPP-4/1.3/0.3@PVDF with 5.4 mol% of PET4A decreases obviously… poor mechanical strength… resulting in low ionic conductivity.” While the series generally shows high conductivities (“lithium-ion conductivities of all the LFPP@PVDF SIPEs… can reach as high as up to 10^-3 S cm^-1 at room temperature”), no explicit numeric value is reported for LFPP-4/1.3/0.3@PVDF.

LFPP-4/1.6/0.6@PVDF is reported to have the highest ionic conductivity among the series: 5.81 mS cm−1 at room temperature (Fig. 1a; Table 1 lists σ at RM = 5.81 mS cm−1 and ESW = 5.2 V for this sample). The temperature dependence section also specifies 5.81 mS cm−1 at 30 °C and 1.28×10−2 S cm−1 at 60 °C for LFPP-4/1.6/0.6@PVDF.

Direct evidence tied to LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF as SIPE in Li batteries: (i) Ionic conductivity for this specific sample: “the LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF SIPE is the best balanced choice … showing an ionic conductivity of up to 2.32 mS cm−1 at room temperature” (Section 2.4, Fig. 1a and 4d). (ii) Single-ion conduction behavior (Li+ as the charge carrier): “The t_Li+ of LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF SIPE … was calculated as 0.91 … suggesting … single-ion conducting behavior.” (Fig. 6a). (iii) Electrochemical stability relevant to battery electrolyte use: “Linear sweep voltammetry … SS|LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF|Li … the electrochemical stability window is up to 5.2 V.” (Fig. 6b). (iv) Battery performance with LiFePO4: “The initial discharge capacity of Li|LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF SIPE|LFP is 138 mA h g−1, and after 230 cycles the capacity … is still up to 140 mA h g−1” (Fig. 1b/Section 2.1 and 2.7). (v) Li|Li symmetric stability: “Li|LFPP-4/2/1@PVDF SIPE|Li cell exhibits a very stable galvanostatic cycling performance for up to 1200 h with a constant polarization voltage of 60 mV.” (Fig. 6c).

The specific sample “LFPP-4/3/2@PVDF” is listed in Table 2 (22.2 mol% PET4A). The paper states: “As depicted in Fig. 1a, lithium-ion conductivities of all the LFPP@PVDF SIPEs with various contents of PET4A can reach as high as up to 10^-3 S cm^-1 at room temperature.” It also notes: “When the molar ratio of PET4A is higher than 9.7%, ionic conductivities decrease with increasing crosslinking density,” which includes LFPP-4/3/2@PVDF. No explicit numeric conductivity or battery metrics were reported specifically for LFPP-4/3/2@PVDF.

Celgard 2400 was used as the commercial PP separator for comparison in LIBs: "A commercial polypropylene (PP) separator, namely, a Celgard 2400 membrane (Celgard, China) ... was used as a LIB separator for comparison." Table 1 reports ionic conductivity σ for PP as 0.66 mS cm-1. Additional PP (Celgard 2400) performance details in the text include porosity 40%, electrolyte uptake 60% (Table 1), contact angle 45°, electrolyte retention 52% after 5 days, capacity retention 86.1% after 300 cycles at 25°C, and 77.2% at 60°C (from 134.9 to 104.1 mAh g^-1).

Use case: electrospun FPEEK-0.25 used as a separator for lithium-ion batteries: "The electrospinning technique was used to fabricate porous PEEK and FPEEK separators for application in LIBs." Performance metric from Table 1: "σ (mS cm-1) ... FPEEK-0.25 3.12". Confirmed in text: "the ionic conductivity was calculated to be ... 3.12 mS cm−1 for ... FPEEK-0.25."

FPEEK-0.5 is used as an electrospun porous separator for lithium-ion batteries (cells: LiFePO4 | separator | Li). Table 1 reports its ionic conductivity as 2.86 mS cm-1 (PEEK 2.71, FPEEK-0.25 3.12, PP 0.66). Additional performance specific to FPEEK-0.5: porosity 85% and electrolyte uptake 534% (Table 1); contact angle 8° (Fig. 5B); electrolyte retention after 5 days 69% (Fig. 4); negligible thermal shrinkage at 150 °C for 1 h (Fig. 6A) and thermal degradation onset not until ~450 °C (Fig. 6B); bulk resistance 2.11 Ω (Fig. 7a); electrochemical stability with no measurable decomposition below 5 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 7b). In full-cell tests at 0.5 C and 25 °C, initial discharge capacity with FPEEK-0.5 separator is 146.9 mAh g-1 (Fig. 10), and the cells with electrospun separators show ~93% capacity retention after 300 cycles at room temperature (Fig. 9). At 60 °C and 0.5 C, the cell with FPEEK-0.5 shows 83.0% capacity retention after 300 cycles (Fig. 11).

Electrospun PEEK separator was used in Li-ion coin cells (LiFePO4|Li, 1 M LiPF6 in EMC/DMC/EC). Table 1 reports ionic conductivity σ = 2.71 mS cm−1 for PEEK at 25 °C. Additional PEEK-related performance in assembled cells: initial discharge capacity 143.9 mAh g−1 at 0.5 C and 25 °C; cells with electrospun separators (including PEEK) showed ~93% capacity retention after 300 cycles at 25 °C; PEEK cell retention 81.6% after 300 cycles at 60 °C. Separator properties for PEEK: porosity 92%, electrolyte uptake 520%, contact angle 9°, tensile strength 26.5 MPa.

F-cHBC (T-F-cHBC anode) used in Li-ion storage cells: “provided a reversible specific capacity of ≈160 mAh g−1 at a current density of 100 mA g−1” and “maintained a capacity of 100 mA h g−1 at a high current density of 7000 mA g−1… at the 42nd cycle” with “stable coulombic efficiency of 99.4% and specific capacity of 200 mA h g−1 … at the 400th cycle.” Also functional in Na-ion storage: “delivers ≈125 mA h g−1 … at 100 mA g−1” and “at a high current density of 4000 mA g−1, … delivered 65 mA h g−1.”

T-F-cHBC anode for Li-ion storage: "provided a reversible specific capacity of ≈160 mAh g−1 at a current density of 100 mA g−1" (Figure 3A; Section 2.2). Additional Li-cell performance context: maintained 100 mAh g−1 at 7000 mA g−1 and 200 mAh g−1 with 99.4% CE at 1000 mA g−1 after 400 cycles (Figure 3C,D). Cell voltage window: 0.02–3.00 V vs Li/Li+.

The material is the monolithium salt 1 used to form PEO-based solid polymer electrolytes (EO/Li = 30:1). Ionic conductivities were measured by EIS from 120 °C down to room temperature; Arrhenius plots for the monolithium salts (1–3) are shown (Fig. 7), and all new salts (including 1) display conductivities lower than LiTFSI at all temperatures. For salt 1, VTF parameters are reported: T0 = −55 °C, A = 0.1 (K1/2·S/cm), B = 563 K, Δμ = 10.0 ± 2.5 kJ/mol (Table 2). Thermal properties of the PEO/salt 1 SPE at EO/Li = 30:1 are also provided (Table 1). No single numeric conductivity values are tabulated; only curves are shown, so no conductivity value is extracted.

DF_{0.24}-20 is used as a solid polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. Key results directly for DF_{0.24}-20:
- Li||Li symmetric cells (J = 0.1 mA/cm2, Q = 0.1 mAh/cm2, 60 °C): “the cell applying DF_{0.24}-20 exhibits nearly 6000 h ultra-stable cycling, with a long-period stable overpotential (90–180 mV) for 300–5000 h”; also reported as “stable cycling for over 5000 h and an overpotential below 100 mV”.
- High-voltage stability from LSV (Li|SPE|C/Al, 5 μA/cm2): DF_{0.24}-20 belongs to the group with 5.0 V oxidation voltage (DF_{0.24}-20, TF_{0.24}-20 (5.0 V) > CA_{0.24}-20 (4.9 V) > AN_{0.24}-20, PM-20 (4.2 V)).
- Ionic conductivity: DF_{0.24}-20 = 0.11 mS/cm at 60 °C; 1.9×10^-5 S/cm at 28 °C.
- Full cells at 60 °C: Li|DF_{0.24}-20|LCO (charged to 4.45 V) shows 80% capacity retention after 300 cycles at 0.5 C. Li|DF_{0.24}-20|NCM811 (to 4.5 V) retains 67.8% after 50 cycles and shows initial CE 88.1% and average CE 99.05% in the subsequent 99 cycles. Li|DF_{0.24}-20|NCM523 (to 4.2 V) shows 71.2% capacity retention after 100 cycles. LFP cells: with high loading (14 mg/cm2), 75.3% capacity retention after 100 cycles; with 2.7 mg/cm2 and higher cutoff (4.2 V), 96% retention after 300 cycles; with 1.5 mg/cm2, 90.8% capacity retention (138.8 mAh/g) at 2 C.
- Membrane/application details: in situ polymerized ~10 μm membrane; tensile strength 94.0 MPa and 91.1% elongation with PE support; Li electrode potential indicates weakly solvating nature (E_Li = −3.056 V for DF_{0.24}-20 at 20 wt% LiTFSI).

PT-0 is the non-fluorinated copolymer electrolyte (PEGMEA:TFEMA = 1:0). It is tested as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li|PE|Li and Li|PE|NCM811 cells. Reported performance for PT-0 includes: (1) Oxidative stability: “the non-fluorinated sample PT-0 underwent anodic oxidation starting at approximately 4.5 V” (Fig. 3d); floating-test: “PT-0 showed a higher slope in the current profile at 4.0 V, with a sharp rise at 4.3 V” (Fig. 3e). (2) Li-ion transference number: “PT-0 (0.42)” measured in Li|PE|Li by chronoamperometry (Figs. 3f and S10). Coin cells with PT-0 are mentioned but without quantitative capacity data: “the Li||NCM811 cell containing non-fluorinated sample PT-0 showed rapid capacity fading and poor Coulombic efficiency” (Fig. S16).

PT-0.5 in Li|PT-0.5|NCM811 coin cells delivered specific discharge capacities of 181.5, 169.9, 145.3, and 120 mAh g^-1 at 0.1C, 0.2C, 0.5C, and 1C, respectively (Fig. 5a,b). Long-term cycling at 0.5C retained 118.06 mAh g^-1 with 81.47% retention and >99% Coulombic efficiency over 200 cycles (Fig. 5c). At 1C, it retained 76.89% of its initial capacity over 300 cycles with 99.8% average CE (Fig. 5d,e). A PT-0.5 pouch cell sustained 144.96 mAh g^-1 with 87.9% retention over 100 cycles at 0.2C (Fig. 5f,g). Supporting electrolyte metrics for PT-0.5 include ionic conductivity 0.329 mS cm^-1 at 30°C (Fig. 3a,b), oxidative stability up to 5.2 V vs. Li/Li+ (Fig. 3d), leakage current <10 µA up to 4.8 V (Fig. 3e), and tLi+ = 0.64 (Fig. 3f). In Li|PT-0.5|Li symmetric cells, overpotentials were 19.0, 38.6, 74.3, and 145.5 mV at 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, and 0.5 mA cm^-2, with stable cycling >1000 h at ~39 mV and CCD of 1.5 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 4c–g; Fig. S13).

PT-1 (PEGMEA:TFEMA = 1:1) is evaluated as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Reported metrics specific to PT-1 include: (1) activation energy Ea = 0.30 eV for ion conduction: "The calculated Ea values were 0.24, 0.25, 0.30, and 0.37 eV for PT-0, PT-0.5, PT-1, and PT-2 respectively." (2) Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.58: "The calculated tLi+ values for the fluorinated copolymers PT-0.5, PT-1, and PT2 (0.64, 0.58, and 0.51, respectively) are significantly higher than that of the non-fluorinated sample PT-0 (0.42)." Qualitative performance notes for PT-1: in Li|Li symmetric cells, "copolymers PT-1 and PT-2 displayed significantly elevated overpotentials" compared to PT-0.5; in full cells, "Li|PT-1|NCM811 and Li|PT-2|NCM811 cells exhibited comparatively lower rate performance." No explicit numerical oxidative stability limit, ionic conductivity value at 30 °C, or full-cell capacity data were provided for PT-1 in the text; thus capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration fields are left null.

PT-2 is the PEGMEA:TFEMA = 1:2 copolymer electrolyte used in Li|PE|Li and Li|PE|NCM811 cells. Reported PT-2 metrics include: ionic conductivity at 30°C = 0.118 mS/cm ("increases from 0.118 to 0.329 mS cm−1 as … changed from 1:2 (PT-2) to 1:0.5 (PT-0.5)"); activation energy Ea = 0.37 eV ("0.24, 0.25, 0.30, and 0.37 eV for PT-0, PT-0.5, PT-1, and PT-2 respectively"); oxidative stability up to 5.4 V vs. Li/Li+ ("PT-2 showed further improved oxidative stability extending up to 5.4 V"); lithium-ion transference number tLi+ = 0.51 ("PT-0.5, PT-1, and PT-2 (0.64, 0.58, and 0.51, respectively)"). In Li symmetric cells, PT-2 showed elevated overpotentials and micro short circuits relative to PT-0.5 ("copolymers PT-1 and PT-2 displayed significantly elevated overpotentials… Micro short circuits were also observed in the Li|PT-2|Li based cell"). No explicit values for capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration specific to PT-2 were provided, hence set to null.

F&C-PE is a polymer solid-state electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. Its Li+ conductivity reaches 1.06 × 10^−4 S cm^−1 at 30 °C (Fig. 2d). The Li/F&C-PE/Li symmetric cell cycles stably for 2000 h at 0.1 mA cm^−2 with ~50 mV polarization and for 900 h at 0.2 mA cm^−2 (Fig. 3a and S8). In full cells, Li/F&C-PE/NCM811 shows 82.2% capacity retention and 99.8% Coulombic efficiency after 300 cycles at 0.2C (Fig. 4b). The Li+ transference number is 0.53 (Fig. 2f), and the oxidation resistance voltage is 4.5 V (Conclusions).

FGPE1:1 is used as a fluorine-grafted gel polymer electrolyte enabling Li+ transport. Reported performance at 25°C: ionic conductivity σ = 1.21 × 10−3 S/cm (i.e., 1.21 mS/cm) and Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.68 (also summarized as σ = 1.21 mScm−1 and t_Li+ = 0.68 at 25°C). These values are explicitly given for FGPE1:1 in the Results/Summary.
No application/performance data for FGPE1:2 appears in the provided text; only FGPE1:1 (and LE) are discussed with quantitative results.

The provided text presents application data for FGPE1:1 (e.g., ionic conductivity, t_Li+, coulombic efficiency, and full-cell cycling performance) but does not report any application metrics or results for the specific material FGPE4:3.

The excerpt provides application/performance data only for FGPE1:1 as a lithium battery electrolyte (e.g., t_Li+ = 0.68, σ = 1.21 mS/cm, cycling data for LFP||FGPE1:1||Li). FGPE3:4 is not mentioned, nor are results stated to apply to all FGPE compositions. Therefore, no application metrics specific to FGPE3:4 can be extracted.

“the LLZTO nanoparticles also act as the ion-conducting ceramic filler to incorporate with PVHM, resulting in the PVHML composite SSEs.”; “We have successfully developed an in-situ grafting and compositing strategy and achieved superionic PVHML composite SSEs using LLZTO as a bifunctional mediator…”
Table 4 reports dielectric properties for each polymer. For 6FDA-PDA: ε′dry,1kHz = 2.81 ± 0.024 and ε′wet,1kHz = 3.222 ± 0.0284; n^2 at 632.8 nm = 2.506.

6FDA-FPDA was film-forming and used for dielectric measurements. Table 4 reports dielectric properties for 6FDA-FPDA: ε′dry,1kHz = 2.85 ± 0.041 and ε′wet,1kHz = 3.192 ± 0.0135; density 1.4835 ± 0.0056 g/cm3 and n^2 at 632.8 nm = 2.4825. Table 5 lists refractive index (TE = 1.5783 ± 0.0005; TM = 1.5756). The paper explicitly measures dielectric constant under dry and 40% RH conditions.
Table 4 (Density and Dielectric Properties) reports for 6FDA-TFPDA: ε′dry, 1 kHz = 2.68 ± 0.046 and ε′wet, 1 kHz = 2.905 ± 0.027, indicating measured dielectric performance of this material.
Table 4 lists for 6FDA-TFMPDA: ε′dry,1kHz = 2.72 ± 0.005 and ε′wet,1kHz = 3.054 ± 0.042 (also n^2@632.8 nm = 2.3975 ± 0.0019). These are dielectric constant measurements of the polymer films.

Table 4 shows for 6FDA-DAT: ε′dry, 1 kHz = 2.75 ± 0.023 and ε′wet, 1 kHz = 3.157. The paper states dielectric constant was measured at 1 kHz after equilibration at 40% RH (wet) and after exhaustive in‑situ drying (dry).
Table 4 reports for 6FDA-2TFMPDA: ε′dry,1kHz = 2.59 ± 0.03 and ε′wet,1kHz = 2.868 ± 0.080.

6FDA-2DAT was measured as a dielectric: Table 4 reports ε′dry,1kHz = 2.74 ± 0.039 and ε′wet,1kHz = 3.212 ± 0.075; n^2@632.8 nm = 2.42923 ± 0.0102; density ρ = 1.4250 g/cm3. Table 5 gives refractive indices: TE = 1.5633 ± 0.0023, TM = 1.5586 ± 0.00325, birefringence = 0.0047. Table 6 lists viscoelastic transitions: Tg (tan δ max) = 379 °C; β transition = 231 °C. Thermal stability (film forming = yes): in N2 Td(2%) = 500 °C, Td(5%) = 532 °C, Td(10%) = 551 °C (Table 7); in air Td(2%) = 463 °C, Td(5%) = 508 °C, Td(10%) = 538 °C (Table 8). These data indicate its evaluation and suitability as a polymer dielectric material.
Dielectric Properties section and Table 4 list 6FDA-OFB with ε′dry,1kHz = 2.55 ± 0.02 and ε′wet,1kHz = 2.729 ± 0.024, demonstrating low-k dielectric performance at 1 kHz.
Table 4 reports for 6FDA-22'PFMB: ε′dry,1kHz = 2.715 ± 0.035 and ε′wet,1kHz = 2.886 ± 0.0283.

Table 4 (Density and Dielectric Properties) includes a row for 6FDA-33'PFMB reporting ε′dry,1kHz = 2.568; the same table leaves ε′wet,1kHz blank for this polymer. Films were prepared specifically for dielectric constant measurements as described, and for 6FDA-33'PFMB durable films were achieved via the cyclic solid-state chain extension approach.

For PE-40% LCE@PEG at 60°C, the ionic conductivity is 1.21 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 ("showing 1.21 × 10^-4, 1.73 × 10^-4, and 2.11 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 for PE-x% LCE@PEG (x = 40, 50, and 60) at 60°C"). The Li+ transference number t_Li+ for x = 40 is 0.415 ("the t_Li+ values for PE-x% LCE@PEG (x = 40, 50, and 60) gel polymer electrolytes are 0.415, 0.425, and 0.5, respectively"). Additional relevant performance, not directly mappable to fields: critical current density for x = 40 is 1.1 mA cm^-2 and no obvious oxidation peak until 4.3 V vs. Li/Li+.

For PE-50% LCE@PEG in LiFePO4//Li cells: “the PE- 50% LCE@PEG based cell can stably cycle for 100 cycles at 0.5 C under 80 °C, exhibiting a reversible discharging specific capacity of 158.5 mA h g−1 at the 100th cycle with a capacity retention of 98.87%.” Also reported: “The PE- 50% LCE@PEG cell shows excellent cycling stability over 150 cycles with no capacity decay at 0.5 C under 60 °C.” Rate capability at 60 °C: “164.8, 165, 160, 157, 152, 142, 63.6 mA h g−1 at 0.1 C, 0.2 C, 0.5 C, 1 C, 2 C, 3 C, and 5 C, respectively.” A further note: “The cell exhibits a high initial reversible capacity of 160.3 mA h g−1 under a higher temperature of 80 °C at 0.5 C.”

PE-60% LCE@PEG is a gel polymer electrolyte for solid-state lithium batteries. Reported performance for x = 60% includes: ionic conductivity 2.11×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C ("The ionic conductivity ... showing 1.21×10^-4, 1.73×10^-4, and 2.11×10^-4 S cm^-1 for PE-x% LCE@PEG (x = 40, 50, and 60) at 60 °C, respectively."); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.5 ("the t_Li+ values for PE-x% LCE@PEG (x = 40, 50, and 60) gel polymer electrolytes are 0.415, 0.425, and 0.5, respectively"); electrochemical stability window up to 4.3 V vs. Li/Li+ without obvious oxidation peak for PE-x% LCE@PEG (Figure 3b); critical current density for Li/PE-60% LCE@PEG/Li = 0.9 mA cm^-2 ("The critical current densities of PE-x% LCE@PEG (x = 40, 50, and 60) are 1.1, 1.1, and 0.9 mA cm^-2, respectively"). Activation energy for ionic transport for x = 60% is 0.22 eV ("The activation energies ... are calculated to be 0.286, 0.281, and 0.22 eV, respectively").

GPE-0 (0 wt% NASICON filler) shows room-temperature ionic conductivity of 1.87×10^-3 S cm^-1. Electrochemical window for GPE-0 is 5.06 V. The Na+ transference number t_Na+ for GPE-0 is 0.33. In full cells (Na3V2(PO4)3/C|GPE-0|Na, 0.5C), the first discharge capacity is 88.8 mAh·g^-1, and after 100 cycles the capacity is 85.0 mAh·g^-1 (95.7% retention). EIS of the full cell with GPE-0 shows 422 Ω initially and 1030 Ω after 100 cycles. Symmetric Na|GPE-0|Na exhibits larger polarization than the filler-containing sample.

Cycling performance (0.5C) for NVPO/C|GPEs|Na: "The first discharge specific capacity was recorded to be 88.8 mAh·g−1, 92.1 mAh·g−1, 92.7 mAh·g−1, 90.9 mAh·g−1 and 90.6 mAh·g−1 for the GPE-0, GPE-2, GPE-4, GPE-6 and GPE-8 sample, respectively. After 100 cycles, the discharge capacity was recorded to be 85.0 mAh·g−1, 90.9 mAh·g−1, 92.0 mAh·g−1, 90.1 mAh·g−1 and 89.8 mAh·g−1 with capacity retention ratios of 95.7%, 98.7%, 99.2%, 99.1% and 99.1% respectively." Also measured property for GPE-2: "The ionic conductivity at room temperature of the GPE-0, GPE-2, GPE-4, GPE-6 and GPE-8 membranes was calculated to be 1.87×10−3 S·cm−1, 2.40×10−3 S·cm−1, 2.83×10−3 S·cm−1, 2.15×10−3 S·cm−1 and 1.89×10−3 S·cm−1 respectively."

GPE-4 is used as the electrolyte in Na3V2(PO4)3/C|GPE|Na sodium-ion batteries. Reported for GPE-4: ionic conductivity 2.83 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 and electrochemical window 5.16 V (Fig. 4); Na-ion transference number t_Na+ = 0.63 (Fig. 5); first discharge specific capacity 92.7 mAh·g^-1 at 0.5C and 92.0 mAh·g^-1 after 100 cycles with 99.2% retention (Fig. 6(a)); areal capacity 0.177 mAh/cm^2; rate performance: 99.8, 98.9, 98.6, 97.6, 95.5, and 83.9 mAh·g^-1 at 0.1C–5C for the GPE-4 cell (Fig. 6(e)).

In Na3V2(PO4)3/C | GPEs | Na cells at 0.5C, the GPE-6 sample showed first discharge specific capacity of 90.9 mAh·g^-1 and after 100 cycles 90.1 mAh·g^-1 with a capacity retention of 99.1% (Fig. 6(a) text). Its room-temperature ionic conductivity was 2.15 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 (Fig. 4(b) text). Cells were tested between 2.5–4.0 V vs Na/Na+ at RT.

GPE-8 is a PVDF-HFP/PMMA/TPU-based gel polymer electrolyte with 8 wt% Na3Zr2Si2PO12, tested in Na-ion batteries. Reported ionic conductivity at room temperature: “The ionic conductivity at room temperature of the GPE-0, GPE-2, GPE-4, GPE-6 and GPE-8 membranes was … and 1.89 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 respectively.” Battery performance for the assembled Na3V2(PO4)3/C|GPEs|Na at 0.5C includes GPE-8: first discharge specific capacity 90.6 mAh/g; after 100 cycles 89.8 mAh/g with capacity retention 99.1%.

PVDF (Solef 5130) is used as the polymer binder for C-LiFePO4 cathodes in Li-ion batteries. Reported discharge capacities for the PVDF-binder cathode include 146 mAh/g at C/10 and 125 mAh/g at C-rate (Fig. 5b–c). After 50 cycles at C-rate, the PVDF cathode shows 129 mAh/g with 101% capacity retention (Fig. 6d; Conclusions). Additional PVDF-related metrics: contact angle 22.2°, dc-electrical conductivity 0.57 S·cm-1, swelling 18.0% (Table 3); overall impedance ~437 Ω (Fig. 4b); CV redox peaks at 3.69/3.20 V with 0.49 V separation (Fig. 4a); initial/mean coulombic efficiency 93%/99% (Fig. 6c).

PVDF-HFP (Solef 21216) was used as the binder in C-LiFePO4 cathodes. Reported discharge capacities: “At C/10 rate (Fig. 5b) and C rate, the discharge capacity values are 146, 139 and 131 mAh·g−1 and 125, 97 and 81 mAh·g−1 for PVDF, PVDF-HFP and PVDF-TrFE binder, respectively.” Long-term cycling at C-rate: “Fig. 6d shows that … the discharge capacity value after 50 cycles for the cathodes with PVDF, PVDF-HFP and PVDF-TrFE binders are 129 mAh·g−1, 75.5 mAh·g−1 and 94.3 mAh·g−1 with capacity retention of 101%, 84%, and 89%, respectively.”

PVDF-TrFE was used as binder in C-LiFePO4 cathodes for Li-ion batteries. Reported discharge capacities for PVDF-TrFE are 130, 133, 124 and 80 mAh·g−1 at C/10, C/5, C/2 and C, respectively (Fig. 5a). Long-term cycling at C-rate shows 94.3 mAh·g−1 after 50 cycles with capacity retention of 89% (Fig. 6d). The coulombic efficiency for PVDF-TrFE is 98% (initial) and 99% (mean) (Fig. 6c).

NPH-GPE is used as the electrolyte in Li/NPH-GPE/Li symmetric cells and LFP/NPH-GPE/Li batteries. The NPH-130% shows a remarkable high ionic conductivity value of 1.21 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C; at 70 °C it is 3.16 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 (Fig. 2a,b). Symmetric Li/NPH-GPE/Li cells operate stably for 500 h at 25 °C with low overpotential, evidencing its role as a Li+ conducting battery electrolyte.

For NPH-130% (NPH-GPE with 130% electrolyte uptake): “The NPH-130% shows a conductivity of 3.16 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 70 °C and … 1.21 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C” (Section 3.2). In full cells, LFP/NPH-130%/Li delivered initial 155 mAh g^-1 at 70 °C; after 200 cycles at 0.5 C: 150.67 mAh g^-1; after 500 cycles: 70% capacity retention with ~99% coulombic efficiency (Fig. 4a,b). At 25 °C and 0.2 C, after 100 cycles: 150 mAh g^-1 with 98.65% retention (Fig. 4c). Rate performance at 70 °C: 155, 120, 95, 86 mAh g^-1 at 0.5, 2, 7, 10 C, restoring to 155 mAh g^-1 at 0.1 C (Fig. 5d,e).

Main text reports: "The PH-SPE electrolyte with salt concentration of 80 wt.% shows the highest conductivity, 4.786 × 10^{-5} S cm^{-1} at 25 °C (Fig. S7). Whereas, the conductivity of PH-SPE is still too low for real application." This value directly pertains to the PH-SPE sample.

PH-GPE is evaluated as a lithium battery electrolyte. Evidence: (1) At 25°C and 0.2 C, Fig. 4(c) comparison states: "In sharp contrast, the discharge capacities of LFP/PH-GPE/Li and LFP/NMP-LE/Li are only 134.8 and 59.45 mAh g^-1, separately" (context: after 100 cycles). (2) At 70°C (0.5 C), initial capacity for PH-GPE is reported: "The initial discharge capacities ... PH-GPE are 147 mAh g^-1" (Fig. 4a). (3) Symmetric Li//Li tests show PH-GPE gives higher overpotential (>0.2 V) and short circuits after ~300 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 3b), confirming its application as a Li-metal battery electrolyte.

PPZ-0% corresponds to PVDF-HFP/PMMA (PPZ-X%, X=0%); thus data reported for PVDF-HFP/PMMA apply to PPZ-0%. Electrolyte performance: ionic conductivity 1.31–2.85 mS/cm from 25–75 °C with Ea=0.172 eV (Table 2, row “PVDF-HFP/PMMA”). Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.35 (Figure 5c–e text). Electrochemical stability window 4.46 V (Figure 5f). Symmetric Li||Li cells: stable plating/stripping at 0.1 mA/cm2 over 400 h; short-circuit observed at 0.4 mA/cm2 and higher overpotential than PPZ-6% (Figure 6a,d). In LiFePO4|PPZ|Li cells at 0.5C (2.5–4.2 V, RT), initial discharge capacity with PVDF-HFP/PMMA electrolyte is 150.4 mAh/g (Figure 7a). These support the use of PPZ-0% as a Li-ion battery gel polymer electrolyte.

PPZ-2% is a PVDF-HFP/PMMA–ZrO2 gel polymer electrolyte tested for Li-ion batteries. Table 2 reports ionic conductivity for PPZ-2%: 25 °C = 1.11 mS cm-1 (35 °C = 1.28, 45 °C = 1.32, 55 °C = 1.84, 65 °C = 2.25, 75 °C = 2.42 mS cm-1) and Ea = 0.175 eV. Other detailed electrochemical metrics (e.g., stability window, cycling in LiFePO4 cells) are provided mainly for PPZ-6%, not PPZ-2%.

Table 2 reports ionic conductivity for PPZ-4% as 1.22 mS cm-1 at 25 °C (Ea = 0.165 eV). Other detailed electrochemical metrics (e.g., Li+ transference number, stability window, full-cell capacities) are only given explicitly for PPZ-6%, not PPZ-4%.

PPZ-6% is a PVDF-HFP/PMMA–ZrO2 (6 wt%) gel polymer electrolyte used in Li batteries. Ionic conductivity at 25 °C: “the PPZ-6% GPE membrane exhibits a higher ionic conductivity with the value of 1.46×10^-3 S cm^-1 at ambient temperature.” (Table 2: PPZ-6% = 1.46 mS cm^-1 at 25 °C). Electrochemical window: “PPZ-6% … 4.65 V” (Figure 5f). Li+ transference number: “0.50” (Figure 5c–e via Bruce–Vincent). Symmetric Li|PPZ-6%|Li shows stable plating/stripping and no short circuit up to 0.8 mA cm^-2 (Figure 6d). Full-cell with LFP cathode: at 0.5 C, “delivered … 153.0 mAh g^-1 and a high average Coulombic efficiency of 99% after 200 cycles” (Figure 7a,b); rate performance: 160.2, 158.8, 154.5, 149.7, 140.3, 127.9 mAh g^-1 at 0.1–5 C, recovering to 155.8 mAh g^-1 when back to 0.2 C (Figure 7c,d); at 1 C after 200 cycles: 150.2 mAh g^-1, retention 99.8% (Figure 7e).

Table 2 lists ionic conductivity for PPZ-8% GPE as 0.86 mS cm−1 at 25 °C (and 0.92, 1.29, 1.37, 1.54, 1.61 mS cm−1 at higher temperatures), indicating its role as a Li+ conducting gel polymer electrolyte. No t_Li+, electrochemical stability window, or quantified battery performance values are reported specifically for PPZ-8% in the text; plots lack readable numeric values, so remaining fields are set to null.

C-1 is used to prepare the composite LFP/C-1: "The obtained LiFePO4/C composites were denoted as LFP/C-1, LFP/C-2, LFP/C-3, which were prepared by using carbon supports C-1, C-2, and C-3, respectively." Performance for LFP/C-1: "Samples LFP/C-1 and LFP/C-2 deliver the initial discharge capacities of 68.1 and 106.5 mA h g−1..." and Table 2 lists for LFP/C-1: 0.1 C = 68.1 mA h g−1; 1 C = 50.3 mA h g−1; 10 C = 23.1 mA h g−1.

No application performance is reported for carbon support C-2 itself. Electrochemical tests and capacities are only given for LiFePO4/C composites (e.g., LFP/C-2), not for C-2: "The obtained LiFePO4/C composites were denoted as LFP/C-1, LFP/C-2, LFP/C-3, which were prepared by using carbon supports C-1, C-2, and C-3, respectively." and "The electrochemical properties of LiFePO4/C composites were tested in CR-2025 coin half-cells..." Table 2 lists rate capacities for LFP/C-2, not C-2.

“The molar ratio of SiO2 and resorcinol vary from 2:1, 3.5:1 to 5:1, the obtained carbon materials were accordingly denoted as C-1, C-2, and C-3. … The obtained LiFePO4/C composites were denoted as LFP/C-1, LFP/C-2, LFP/C-3, which were prepared by using carbon supports C-1, C-2, and C-3, respectively.” “Taking the carbon support C-3 as the example, it has a larger total pore volume (4.68 cm3 g−1) and higher BET surface area (977 m2 g−1). After infiltration, a significant reduced surface area (136 m2 g−1) and pore volume (0.33 cm3 g−1) are observed. This is an indication that LiFePO4 filled in most pore spaces of the carbon support C-3. The overall weight percentage of LiFePO4 in the LFP/C-3 composite is estimated to be 85.4 wt% by a TG analysis.”

Evidence for LFP/C-1 capacity: “Samples LFP/C-1 and LFP/C-2 deliver the initial discharge capacities of 68.1 and 106.5 mA h g−1 (based on the weight of composite, the same below except special claimed)” (Fig. 6a text). Table 2 (Rate capacity): row “LFP/C-1” lists 0.1 C = 68.1, 0.2 C = 59.9, 0.5 C = 53.8, 1 C = 50.3, 2 C = 43.9, 5 C = 33.5, 10 C = 23.1 mA h g−1. Fig. 6 caption clarifies: “The specific capacity is based on the entire weight of a LiFePO4/C composite.” No regeneration data provided.

Table 2 lists LFP/C-2 rate capacities (mA h g-1) based on the composite weight: 0.1C 106.5; 0.2C 105.8; 0.5C 102.2; 1C 102.3; 2C 95.9; 5C 78.4; 10C 54.8. In Fig. 6b caption/text: rate performance and cycling performance are shown, with nearly 100% capacity retention over 50 cycles for the LiFePO4/C composites.

For LFP/C-3 (the composite using carbon support C-3), Table 2 reports capacities based on the whole composite: 144.6 mA_h/g at 0.1C, 139.3 at 0.2C, 134.3 at 0.5C, 128.4 at 1C, 118.2 at 2C, 99.3 at 5C, 83.0 at 10C, and 60.4 at 20C. Long-term high-rate cycling shows 96.7% capacity retention over 1000 cycles at 10C with an average discharge capacity of 83 mA_h/g and specific power of 5114 W/kg.

Pure-LFP (pure LiFePO4) as a cathode in CR2025 half-cells (2.5–4.2 V) delivered 39.1 mA h g−1 at 1 C with capacity fading over 50 cycles (Fig. 6b). Table 2 (Pure-LFP) lists capacities: 0.1 C 70, 0.2 C 58.7, 0.5 C 46.8, 1 C 39.1, 2 C 31.2 mA h g−1.

MDPE is used as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Reported metrics include: highest ionic conductivity of 3×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C with 70 wt.% LiTFSI (Figure 2e; Table S1); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.76 (Figure 3g). Additional related performance: wide electrochemical window of 4.9 V vs Li+/Li (Figure 2d); Li/MDPE/Li symmetric cells cycle stably for 1000 h without short-circuit (Figure 4a); full cell using MDPE as electrolyte and binder reaches 170 mAh/g at 0.1 C with >80% retention and ~100% CE at 60 °C (Figure 5b).

UPE is the ultraviolet polymerized electrolyte membrane without UPy-CNTs. Ionic conductivity: “UPEC1,0 has an ionic conductivity of 0.226 mS cm−1 at 30 °C, while the UPE without the addition of UPy-CNTs is only 0.177 mS cm−1” (Fig. 4e,f). Li+ transference number: “the Li+ transference number of LE, UPE, and UPEC1,0 is 0.33, 0.36, and 0.79, respectively” (Fig. 4g–i). Critical current density: “The CCD for LE, UPE, UPEC0.5, UPEC1.5, and UPEC2.0 is 0.2 mA cm−2, 0.3 mA cm−2, 0.5 mA cm−2, 0.8 mA cm−2, 0.7 mA cm−2, respectively” (Fig. 4d). Li||Li symmetric cycling at 0.1 mA cm−2, 0.1 mAh cm−2: “UPE-based cell can achieve a cycle time of 1795 h… overpotential… 108 mV at 1795 h” (Fig. 5a). At 0.2 mA cm−2, 0.2 mAh cm−2: “UPE-based cell experiences a short circuit at 278 h, with an initial overpotential of 40 mV” (Fig. 5b). Full cell polarization: “Li||UPEC1.0||LFP… polarization voltage of 150 mV at 2C… Li||UPE||LFP… polarization voltage of 250 mV at 2C” (Fig. 7a and text).

Critical current density (CCD) for UPEC_0.5: "The CCD for LE, UPE, UPEC_0.5, UPEC_1.5, and UPEC_2.0 is 0.2 mAcm^-2, 0.3 mAcm^-2, 0.5 mAcm^-2, 0.8 mAcm^-2, 0.7 mAcm^-2, respectively (Fig. 4d and S13)." Long-term Li||Li symmetric cell cycling at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and 0.1 mAh cm^-2: "cells with UPEC_0.5, UPEC_1.5, and UPEC_2.0 cycle to 1159 h, 1917 h, and 1727 h, respectively"; at 0.2 mA cm^-2 and 0.2 mAh cm^-2: "UPEC_0.5 ... cycle for 88 h".

UPEC1.0 (i.e., x = 1 wt% UPy-CNTs) is used as a gel electrolyte for Li metal batteries. Full-cell data: "The specific capacity of Li||UPEC1.0||LFP cell can reach 146 mAh g^-1 even after 200 cycles at 0.5C" (Fig. 7d). Supporting electrolyte metrics for UPEC1.0 include: Li||Cu half-cell CE 97.82% at the 100th cycle (Fig. 4b); critical current density 0.9 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 4c–d); ionic conductivity 0.226 mS cm^-1 at 30°C and Ea = 0.183 eV (Fig. 4e–f); Li+ transference number 0.79 (Fig. 4i). Long-term Li||Li cycling: stable 2500 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2, 0.1 mAh cm^-2 (overpotential 22 mV initially, 54 mV at 2500 h) and >850 h at 0.2 mA cm^-2, 0.2 mAh cm^-2 (Fig. 5a–b). Rate performance: 167/160/150/141/120 mAh g^-1 at 0.1/0.2/0.5/1/2C; recovers to 166 mAh g^-1 at 0.1C (Fig. 7a–c). Orientation test confirms asymmetric design utility (Fig. 7f–g).

Explicit data for UPEC_1.5: “The critical current density of UPEC1.0 reaches 0.9 mA cm^-2. The CCD for LE, UPE, UPEC0.5, UPEC1.5, and UPEC2.0 is 0.2 mA cm^-2, 0.3 mA cm^-2, 0.5 mA cm^-2, 0.8 mA cm^-2, 0.7 mA cm^-2, respectively (Fig. 4d and S13).” Additional related performance (Li||Li symmetric cells, 0.1 mA cm^-2, 0.1 mAh cm^-2): “cells with UPEC0.5, UPEC1.5, and UPEC2.0 cycle to 1159 h, 1917 h, and 1727 h, respectively,” (Fig. S14). No ionic conductivity, transference number, or full-cell data were reported specifically for UPEC_1.5.

LIB anode testing conditions: coin cell (2016), Li metal counter, 1 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC (1:1 v/v), current density 0.2 mA/cm2, voltage 0.001–1.5 V vs Li/Li+. For SiO2@C specifically: “At the first cycle, the discharge capacity was 281 mAh g−1 … for SiO2@C … After second cycle, the capacity was rapidly dropped by 50–60% … Unexpectedly, further cycling gradually enhanced the discharge capacity from 95 mAh g−1 to 222 mAh g−1 for SiO2@C.” Only data for SiO2@C (without boric acid) were used; higher capacities (≈500 mAh g−1) refer to SiO2@C + B and were excluded.

Use case: Li-ion battery anode. Conditions: coin cell (type 2016), 1 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC (1:1), current density 0.2 mA cm^-2, 0.001–1.5 V vs Li/Li+. Material-specific outcomes: “the carbon-coated silica macroparticles processed with boric acid show highest charge capacity of 500 mAh g^-1 after 103 cycles.” Initial performance: “At the first cycle, the discharge capacity was … 382 mAh g^-1 for SiO2@C + B … the CE reaches above 97% for SiO2@C + B.” Improvement during cycling: “enhancement of the specific capacity from 195 mAh g^-1 to 503 mAh g^-1 was observed especially from SiO2@C + B.” Electrode loading: “The dried mass of anode containing … SiO2@C + B macroparticles were measured to be 1.12 mg.” Highlights confirm: “Carbon-coated silica formed with boric acid show improved capacity of 500 mAh g^-1.”

file_name: Adv Funct Materials - 2024 - You - High‐Voltage All‐Solid‐State Lithium Metal Batteries Enabled by Localized.md

parse.why: NCM811/F-PISE/Li cell exhibits an initial discharge capacity of 157.6 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C and 30 °C with ~98% capacity retention after 500 cycles (Figure 6a). F-PISE shows a wide electrochemical stability window up to 5.2 V vs Li+/Li at 30 °C (Figure 4a) and enables Li/Li symmetric cells to cycle >1500 h with polarization ~10–35 mV (Figure 5a).

DFNCA electrolyte (0.25 M LiNO3 + 0.9 M LiDFOB in DFNCA/FEC, v:v = 1:1) enabled Li||NCM811 full cells to deliver a specific capacity of 148.2 mAh/g after 100 cycles with 77% retention at 0.25 C (first two cycles at 0.1 C, 3–4.3 V) (Figure 4a–c). Additional performance evidence for the same DFNCA electrolyte includes: Li||Cu cells CE as high as 99% by Aurbach’s method; average CE ~97% for 400 cycles at 0.5 mA/cm2 and 0.5 mAh/cm2 (Figure 2a,b); Li||Li symmetric cells maintained ~50 mV overpotential for >1000 h at 0.5 mA/cm2 and 0.5 mAh/cm2 (Figure 2i); anode-free Cu||LiFePO4 pouch cells showed 64% capacity retention after 50 cycles and 212 Wh/kg energy density (Figure 4d). These results are explicitly reported for the DFNCA electrolyte.

For the Carbonate electrolyte (1 M LiPF6 in EC/DEC 1:1), Li/Cu cells evaluated by Aurbach's method showed CE of only 83% and the voltage hysteresis suddenly increased after ~95 h (Figure 2a). In Li||Li symmetric cells, the overpotential fluctuated from ~40 mV at ~200 h to ~313.4 mV at ~350 h (Figure 2i). In Li||NCM811 full cells at 0.25 C, capacity declined sharply after 25 cycles and was nearly zero after 100 cycles (Figure 4a,b). In anode-free Cu||LiFePO4 pouch cells, capacity dropped below 50 mAh with 23% retention after 40 cycles (Figure 4d). The Li+ transference number of the carbonate electrolyte was 0.30 (Figure 1f).

file_name: In situ cross-linked plastic crystal electrolytes toward superior lithium metal batteries.md

parse.why: CSPE is used as an in-situ polymerized quasi-solid gel electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Performance directly reported for CSPE includes: ionic conductivity 0.32 mS/cm at 30 °C (and 9.8×10^-5 S/cm at −20 °C); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.58 at RT; oxidative decomposition onset at 5.3 V vs Li+/Li. Li/CSPE/Li symmetric cells cycle stably for 700 h at 0.5 mA/cm^2; at 0.1 mA/cm^2 show ~100 mV polarization after 1000 h. In Li/CSPE/Cu cells at 0.1 mA/cm^2, Coulombic efficiency is ~90% for 150 cycles with only slight capacity loss after 500 cycles. LCO/CSPE/Li cells: at 4.3 V, capacity retention 80% after 400 cycles at 0.5C with ~99.5% Coulombic efficiency; rate performance ~136.9 mAh/g at 1C and ~102.7 mAh/g at 5C. At 4.4 V, discharge capacity ~130.5 mAh/g after 200 cycles. At 4.5 V, ~146.7 mAh/g after 100 cycles with ~99.3% Coulombic efficiency. LFP/CSPE/Li cells: capacity retention 88.6% after 850 cycles at 0.5C with ~99.9% Coulombic efficiency; ~102 mAh/g at 5C. Safety: CSPE polymer shows slow ignition (~5 s) and self-extinguishing behavior; LCO/CSPE/Li pouch cell can power LEDs after bending and cutting.

file_name: In situ cross-linked plastic crystal electrolytes toward superior lithium metal batteries.md

parse.why: Direct evidence for SN-LiDFOB electrolyte: “the ionic conductivity of CSPE and SN-LiDFOB electrolytes at 30°C are 0.32 mS/cm and 0.34 mS/cm.” Additional related metrics (not encoded as capacity here): “the CSPE shows an oxidative decomposition at 5.3 V (vs. Li+/Li), which is much higher than that of the SN-LiDFOB electrolyte (5.0 V vs. Li+/Li).” and “the (tLi+) of CSPE and SN-LiDFOB electrolytes are 0.58 and 0.30, respectively.” Also, “the Li/SN-LiDFOB/Li cell fails in about 6 h” at 0.5 mA/cm2.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: ABSTRACT: "These new hyperbranched ionomers and copolymers are strong candidates for future use in anhydrous proton exchange membranes." Introduction: "the polymer can be used as the matrix for a proton exchange membrane." Results: "As the planned future use for these membranes in this laboratory is proton exchange membranes... Imidazole has shown to use a different proton transporting mechanism that does not require water... We are planning to use imidazole to develop a dry fuel cell membrane."

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: LAE1 was synthesized as a linear perfluorinated aryl ether polymer and used to form membranes and as a toughening component in blends: "As a control, LAE1 was cast from DMAc without any HBFP, which resulted in a transparent film. The AFM micrograph (Fig. 5) showed a surface free of pores, but with cavities of >5 μm." In blends, "Crosslinking the HBFP into LAE1 produced several free standing, clear, orange films..." and mechanical properties of 30% HBFP/70% LAE1 blends were reported for ethylene glycol (Table 1) and TMP (Table 2) crosslinking. Non-crosslinked HBFP/LAE1 blends were brittle and turbid, while crosslinking improved film formation and mechanics.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: LAE2 is synthesized and used as a linear polyaryl ether sulfone toughening agent in blends with HBFP ("Synthesis of Linear Polyaryl Ether Sulfone (LAE2)", Tg 175.3°C; used in 30% HBFP/70% LAE2 blends). The study reports only physical/mechanical properties of LAE2-containing membranes (DMA tables for TMP-crosslinked FPSA50/LAE2 and FPIM50/LAE2 blends; DSC results), and AFM/clarity observations. The paper states future intent for proton exchange membranes ("As the planned future use for these membranes ... is proton exchange membranes"), but no proton transport, adsorption/separation, catalytic, or other application performance metrics specific to LAE2 (or its blends) are provided. Therefore, no extractable application performance data exist for LAE2.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: FPSA50 (50% sulfonated HBFP) was specifically blended with LAE2 and crosslinked for membrane formation: "As a starting point, the FPSA50 (50% sulfonated HBFP) was used for FPSA/HBFP blends... Each film was characterized by DMA in triplicates (Table 3)." The work positions these materials for proton exchange membrane use: "These new hyperbranched ionomers and copolymers are strong candidates for future use in anhydrous proton exchange membranes." However, for FPSA50/LAE2 blends, only mechanical properties were reported (Table 3), and the authors note: "the material was not resilient enough to produce uniform membranes on a larger scale... when dehydrated... the films were completely brittle. No further membrane preparation was completed." No proton conductivity or related performance metrics were provided for FPSA50.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: FPSA85 is defined in the text: "Polymers with three different sulfonate contents were prepared (50, 85, and 100%, designated as FPSA50, FPSA85, and FPSA100, respectively)." For FPSA85 blended with LAE1 (30/70, no crosslinker): "membranes were too brittle for mechanical analysis and phase separated on the macroscale. No sulfonated HBFP/LAE1 blend was further characterized." For FPSA85 blended with LAE2 and TMP crosslinking: "When similar conditions were used for FPSA85 and FPSA100 blends with LAE2, all films were similar in appearance, although the mechanical properties were much worse. The blends could not be cut for mechanical testing at any crosslinking concentration (0, 10, 20, or 30% TMP)." No quantitative proton transport or other performance metrics were reported for FPSA85.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: Polymers with three different sulfonate contents were prepared (50, 85, and 100%, designated as FPSA50, FPSA85, and FPSA100, respectively). When similar conditions were used for FPSA85 and FPSA100 blends with LAE2, all films were similar in appearance, although the mechanical properties were much worse. The blends could not be cut for mechanical testing at any crosslinking concentration (0, 10, 20, or 30% TMP). These new hyperbranched ionomers and copolymers are strong candidates for future use in anhydrous proton exchange membranes.

file_name: Journal of Polymer Science Part A  Polymer Chemistry - 2019 - Quast - Membrane preparation from hyperbranched.md

parse.why: FPIM50 is a 50% imidazole-substituted HBFP used to form membranes blended with LAE2 and TMP-crosslinked; mechanical properties for 30FPIM50/70LAE2 are reported (Table 4) and membranes are described as clearer and more ductile. The paper states these hyperbranched ionomers are strong candidates for future use in anhydrous proton exchange membranes and that imidazole is intended to enable a dry fuel cell membrane. No proton transport metrics were measured.

file_name: Long-chain fluorocarbon-driven hybrid solid polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries.md

parse.why: LFSPE is a long-chain fluorocarbon-driven solid polymer electrolyte used in SLMBs; the ionic conductivity of the LFSPE membrane was 6.78×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C and 1.37×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 80 °C; LFSPE shows a wide electrochemical stability window up to 4.713 V and a Li+ transference number of 0.47; PDDA-TFSI@Li|LFSPE|PDDA-TFSI@Li symmetric cells maintained low overpotential for 2000 h at 0.2 mAh cm^-2; full cells with LFSPE delivered high capacities and long-term cycling stability (e.g., LFP: 138.8 mAh g^-1 at 1C and 98.8% retention after 500 cycles).

SPEs were prepared by mixing PC-1–3 with 30 wt% LiTFSI. The ionic conductivity at 25 °C for PC-1-based SPE is reported as 4.86 × 10^-6 S.cm^-1 ("At 25 °C, an ionic conductivity of 3.75 × 10^-5 S.cm^-1 was measured for PC-3 which is 7.8 times higher than that obtained for PC-1 (4.86 × 10^-6 S.cm^-1)").

PC-2 is evaluated as a solid polymer electrolyte (SPE) with LiTFSI: "We then prepared SPEs by mixing the different polymers (PC-1-3) with 30 wt% LiTFSI in acetone, followed by solvent removing. The ionic conductivity of the three SPEs were evaluated at a temperature ranging from 100 °C to 25 °C." PC-2-specific property reported for the SPE: "The Tg of SPEs decreased with the molar mass of PEO, from −37 °C for PC-1, to −41 °C for PC-2 and to −44 °C for PC-3." No explicit ionic conductivity value for PC-2 is provided in the text; only PC-1 and PC-3 are quantified.

PC-3 was evaluated as a solid polymer electrolyte (SPE) with 30 wt% LiTFSI: "We then prepared SPEs by mixing the different polymers (PC-1-3) with 30 wt% LiTFSI...". For PC-3, "At 25 °C, an ionic conductivity of 3.75 × 10^{-5} S.cm^{-1} was measured for PC-3...
