The ionic conductivity of PC-3 (3.75 × 10^{-5} S.cm^{-1} at room temperature and 1.86 × 10^{-4} S.cm^{-1} at 60 °C) is comparable...". Note: the PC-3 based SPE was "a sticky material that could not form a self-standing membrane."

PC-4 (cross-linkable poly(oxo-carbonate)) was used to form a semi-interpenetrated network (SIN) solid polymer electrolyte together with PC-3 and diethylene glycol diacrylate: “All solid cross-linked SPE. 60 mg of PC-3, 20 mg of cross-linkable polycarbonate (PC-4), 20 mg of diethylene glycol diacrylate, 42.85 mg of LiTFSI and 3 mg of 2-hydroxy-2-methylpropiophenone… Finally, the films were passed 3 times under a xenon arc lamp…”; The resulting membrane (SIN-PC-3) is a self-standing SPE: “A self-standing transparent membrane was collected after a few seconds of irradiation.”; Electrolyte performance metrics of the SIN that contains PC-4: ionic conductivity at rt 1.11×10^−5 S·cm^−1; Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.28 at 70 °C; electrochemical stability window up to 5 V vs Li+/Li; In Li/SIN-PC-3/LFP cells at 60 °C: 161 mAh g^−1 at 0.1C; after 400 cycles at 1C, 83 mAh g^−1 retained (~86% of initial); With 10 wt% TEG added to the same SIN formulation: rt conductivity 4.7×10^−5 S·cm^−1; at 25 °C, 165 mAh g^−1 at C/10 and 82 mAh g^−1 at 1C; 86 mAh g^−1 at 1C after 400 cycles at rt. These data are reported specifically for the SIN electrolyte that incorporates PC-4 as the crosslinkable component.

F2DEM (1.75 M LiFSI / F2DEM) is used as a single-solvent electrolyte for lithium metal batteries. Quantified performance directly reported: average CE in Li||Cu half cells = 99.53 ± 0.03% (modified Aurbach method). Additional R&D evidence specific to F2DEM: ionic conductivity peaks at ~2 mol LiFSI/L solvent, corresponding to 1.75 M LiFSI/F2DEM; Li||Li symmetric cells show ~30 mV overpotential at 1 mA cm^-2 (vs ~55 mV for F5DEE), with stability issues only beyond 6 mA cm^-2; LSV on Pt shows leakage current onset ~4 V (higher than DEM ~3 V), and Li||Al voltage-hold at 4.4 V shows no significant leakage current; Pouch cells (Cu||LFP, 2.5–3.65 V) exhibit higher capacity utilization and CE than references across rates (e.g., C/2–2C and C/2–C/5); Coin cells (Li||LFP, 20 μm Li, 3.5 mAh cm^-2 LFP, 2.5–3.8 V) retain 80% capacity over 350 cycles at 0.4 mA cm^-2 charge / 2 mA cm^-2 discharge, 200 cycles at 1 / 2 mA cm^-2, and 125 cycles at 1.5 / 3 mA cm^-2, indicating stable cycling under high-rate conditions.

DEM (diethoxymethane) was evaluated as 1.7 M LiFSI/DEM electrolyte. In Li||Cu half cells, the average coulombic efficiency was reported as 99.19 ± 0.07% (four cells): "The average CE of F2DEM was ... 99.19 ± 0.07% (4 cells) with DEM." DEM also showed lower oxidative stability in Li||Pt LSV (onset ~3 V) compared to F2DEM: "the leakage current was observed at an onset voltage of around 4 V for F2DEM, which is higher than the 3 V observed with DEM"; and DEM performed poorly in higher-rate Li||LFP tests, being excluded from some comparisons due to poor performance.

For 1.75 M LiFSI / F2DEM: (i) Li||Cu half cells (modified Aurbach protocol) report "The average CE of F2DEM was measured as 99.53 ± 0.03% for four cells" (Figure 3a text). (ii) Li||LFP coin cells: "Under a relatively fast discharge rate of 2 mA cm−2, F2DEM can retain 80% of its first cycle capacity over 350 cycles with 0.4 mA cm−2 charge rate" (Figure 5e-f text). Additional corroborating performance relevant to this electrolyte (not encoded in fields): Li||Li symmetric cells show ~30 mV overpotential at 1 mA cm−2 and lower than F5DEE across currents (Figure 2d); Li||LFP coin cells retain 80% over 200 cycles at 1 mA cm−2 charge/2 mA cm−2 discharge and over 125 cycles at 1.5/3 mA cm−2 (Figure 5c-h text).

PU-SN-LiTFSI is used as a solid polymer electrolyte for Li batteries. Reported room-temperature ionic conductivity: "Conductivity reaches its maximum (2.865×10^{-4} S cm^{-1}) at room temperature when the SN content is 41.1 wt%." Electrochemical window: "there is no obvious current peak below 4.068 V". Li+ transference number: "calculated to be 0.511 when the SN content was 41.1 wt%". Symmetric Li//Li cell stability: "cycles for 600 h with a stable overpotential at 0.1 mA cm^{-2}". Solid-state Li/LFP battery performance at room temperature: at 0.5C "still delivers a specific capacity of 100 mAh g^{-1} even after testing for 700 cycles, exhibiting a retention rate of ~92%"; rate capability: 150, 143, 134, 121, 88, 56, 33, and 17 mAh g^{-1} at 0.1C to 5C.

LLZO-F0.2 fiber-based CSE displays the highest ionic conductivity of 5.37 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at room temperature (Figure 4a). The lithium-ion transference number of LLZO-F0.2 fiber-based CSE reaches 0.61 (Figure 4e,f). The oxidation potential of the LLZO-F0.2 fiber-based CSE reaches 5.18 V (Figure 4d). Li|LLZO-F0.2 CSE|Li symmetric cells are stable for >800 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 (Figure 5c).

LiBC-1 is used as a solid polyelectrolyte and binder in Li/LiBC-1/LiFePO4 cells operated at 70 °C: “the LiBC-1 block copolymer was used as both the solid-state polyelectrolyte and the binder… Lab-scale LiFePO4 / LiBC-1 / lithium metal battery prototypes were then assembled… The electrochemical behavior of the assembled cells was evaluated at 70 °C.” LiBC-1 shows ionic conductivity 2.3 × 10^-6 S cm^-1 at 25 °C (Table 1) and reaches ~10^-5 S cm^-1 at 55 °C: “The highest value of conductivity was measured for LiBC-1 being 2.3 × 10^-6 S cm^-1 at 25 °C… increased up to one order of magnitude and reached the targeted 10^-5 S cm^-1 level at 55 °C.” It has an electrochemical stability window up to 4.5 V vs Li+/Li and Li+ transference number 0.83: “the anodic limit shifts up to 4.5 versus Li+/Li… The obtained transport number value of 0.83…” In full cells it enabled up to 130 mAh g^-1 at C/15 and reversible cycling up to C/5 at 70 °C: “capable of delivering large capacities up to 130 mAh g^-1… reversibly charge/discharge at least up to 100 cycles… (>99% at C/5).

Table 1 reports for LiBC-2 (poly(PEGM)-b-poly(LiMTFSI)) an ionic conductivity σ at 25 °C of 1.5 × 10^-6 S cm^-1. The materials are presented as single-ion conducting block copolymer electrolytes for lithium batteries. The text also notes: 'the conductivity values of the block copolymers increased up to one order of magnitude and reached the targeted 10^-5 S cm^-1 level at 55 °C.'

Table 1 lists LiBC-3 with ionic conductivity σ at 25 °C = 1.2 × 10^-6 S cm^-1 (with [EO]/[Li] = 18 and Tg = −41 °C). The work describes these materials as single-ion conducting block copolymer electrolytes for lithium systems.
Table 1 (row LiBC-4) reports: "σ at 25 °C (S cm^-1) = 1.6 × 10^-7"; also summarized in the text: "σ^LiBC-4 (1.6 × 10^-7 S cm^-1) ..."

Only CPE-related application data extracted:
- Ionic conductivity (SS/CPE/SS, 25°C): 1.64×10^-5 mScm^-1 (vs GCPE 1.19×10^-4 mScm^-1) — Figure 3 text.
- Ionic conductivity (SS/CPE/SS, 0°C): 1.7×10^-6 mScm^-1 (vs GCPE 2.51×10^-5 mScm^-1) — Figure 3 text.
- Electrochemical window (LSV, vs Li/Li+): CPE 4.5 V (GCPE 4.6 V) — Figure 3c text.
- Li/CPE/Li symmetric cell stability: soft short circuit after 30 h at 0.2 mA/cm^2; polarization reached 255 mV in the first hour; at 0.5 mA/cm^2, short circuit after 3 h — Figure 4a text.
- LFP/CPE/Li full cell: specific discharge capacity at room temperature described as very poor; coulombic efficiency fluctuates greatly; at 0°C the cell cannot work — Figure 6a,d text.
- Interfacial impedance (LFP/CPE/Li, before cycling): R_ct = 2264 Ω; R_sei = 685 Ω — Figure S7a / standstill EIS text.
No regeneration procedure or t50-type metric reported for CPE; no quantifiable discharge capacity values for CPE were given, hence capacity/selectivity/breakthrough left null.

GCPE was used as the electrolyte in LFP/GCPE/Li coin cells. At room temperature, LFP/GCPE/Li cells have a specific discharge capacity of 164.7 mAh/g at 0.1C, with >99% capacity retention and >99% Coulombic efficiency after 100 cycles (Figure 6a,b). Additional GCPE-specific performance includes ionic conductivity of 1.19×10^-4 mS/cm at 25 °C (significantly higher than CPE) and an oxidation stability of 4.6 V vs. Li/Li+ (Figure 3c). Li/GCPE/Li symmetric cells showed no short circuit for 700 h at 0.2 mA/cm^2 with low polarization (overpotential 76 mV at 2 h, 131 mV at 300 h, 187 mV at 700 h), evidencing stable Li+ plating/stripping with GCPE (Figure 4a and text).

The optimized PVA9-P(AMPSLi-IL)1 electrolyte delivered an initial discharge capacity of 145.7 mAh·g−1 at 0.5 C in LFP|Li half-cells with a capacity retention of 79.0% after 200 cycles and average Coulombic efficiency >99.9% (Fig. 10(d) and Conclusion). It also exhibited a lithium transference number of 0.85 (Section 2.6; Fig. 8(a)), ionic conductivity of 2.87×10−3 S·cm−1 at room temperature (Section 2.5; Fig. 7(a); Conclusion), oxidation potential of 4.34 V vs Li/Li+ (Section 2.7; Fig. 8(d); Conclusion), and enabled symmetric Li|PVA9-P(AMPSLi-IL)1|Li cycling for over 1800 h with stable overpotential (Section 2.8; Fig. 9(a)).

Table 1 reports ionic conductivity at 25 °C for PVA8-P(AMPSLi-IL)2 (PVA:P(AMPSLi-IL)=8:2) as 2.56×10^-3 S·cm^-1. Original: “8:2 … σ (S·cm-1) (25 °C) 2.56×10^-3”. This sample is evaluated as a polymer electrolyte for Li-ion batteries.

Table 1 reports porosity, electrolyte uptake and ionic conductivity at 25 °C for the series. For PVA:P(AMPSLi-IL)=7:3 (i.e., PVA7-P(AMPSLi-IL)3), the values are: porosity 78.4%, uptake 518%, and σ = 2.12×10^-3 S·cm^-1 (25 °C).

Sample naming: “... respectively denoted as PVA10, PVA9-P(AMPSLi-IL)1, PVA8-P(AMPSLi-IL)2, PVA7-P(AMPSLi-IL)3 and PVA6-P(AMPSLi-IL)4.” (Section 1.2.4). Application context: “The nanofiber membranes were in situ gelated ... which was used as a polymeric solid electrolyte.” (Abstract/Section 3). Performance (Table 1): For weight ratio 6:4 (i.e., PVA6-P(AMPSLi-IL)4), σ (S·cm-1, 25 °C) = 1.83×10^-3.

Ion conduction in Li-ion battery GPE: “For GPE with a P(HFBMA - co - PEGMA) / PVDF mass ratio of 1.7 / 10, the ionic conductivity reached 3.19 mS cm−1.” (Section 3.6). Battery performance with this GPE (E4): initial discharge capacity 120.0 mAh g−1 and 103.0 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles (86% retention) (Section 3.8). These data correspond to the blend membrane with PVDF/P(HFBMA-co-PEGMA) = 100/17 (i.e., copolymer/PVDF = 1.7/10; M4/E4).

Poly(HFIPA) (Mn = 6491, Mw/Mn = 1.20) underwent DBU-catalyzed transesterification with benzyl alcohol to near-quantitative conversion: 98% conversion in 5 h at 25 °C (THF, 2 equiv BnOH, 0.5 equiv DBU) and 99% conversion in 2 h at 50 °C (same conditions). Source: Table 1, section 'Aminolysis and transesterification of poly(HFIPA)'. The text reiterates: 'a very efficient transesterification of poly(HFIPA) with BnOH was obtained under DBU catalyzed conditions ... 98% conversion within 5 h at 25 °C ... 99% conversion in 2 h at 50 °C.'
Table 2 (Aminolysis and transesterification of poly(HFIPMA)) shows: benzyl amine (2 equiv) with DBU (2 equiv) in DMF at 75 °C achieved 98% conversion in 12 h for poly(HFIPMA).

No application/performance data are provided for material (1) bis(4-methy benzene sulphonyl)imide. It is reported only as a synthetic intermediate (yield, NMR, MS) en route to monomer (2) and subsequent polymers/membranes. All application results (ionic conductivity, transference number, ESW, Li plating/stripping, battery cycling) pertain to the lithiated polymer blend SIPE membranes, not to (1).

No direct application/performance data are reported for potassium(4-carboxyl benzene sulphonyl)imide monomer (2) itself. The paper applies and evaluates polymer membranes derived after polycondensation of (2) and subsequent lithiation. Evidence: “Polycondensation of 4,4′(hexafluoroisopropylidene)dianiline and potassium(4-carboxyl benzene sulphonyl)imide (2) was performed… The lithiated polymer (5)…”. Application measurements (ionic conductivity, ESW, cycling) are for the swollen SIPE membranes based on the lithiated polymer (5): “Four different membrane compositions were made… The swollen membranes exhibited sufficient mechanical stability… ionic conductivities…”, “The electrochemical stability window of the swollen SIPE membrane (1:3)…”, and “Cycle stability of the NMC111/lithium metal cell with the polymer blend electrolyte membrane…”.

The paper states the polymer was prepared for application as a single-ion conducting polymer electrolyte in lithium-ion or lithium metal batteries. Quantitative performance (e.g., ionic conductivity 0.5 mS cm-1 at 20 °C with t_Li+ = 0.9; ESW up to 4.6 V vs Li/Li+; long-term Li/Li plating-stripping stability; NMC111/Li cycling capacities) was measured on swollen blend membranes that use the lithiated form of the polymer (5) with PVdF-HFP (e.g., 1:3), not directly on polymer (3) itself.

No application/performance data are reported for material (4) itself. All electrochemical and membrane results (ionic conductivity, ESW ~4.6 V vs Li/Li+, Li/Li symmetric cycling, NMC111 cell capacities) are for the lithiated polymer (5) blended with PVdF-HFP, e.g., 'swollen SIPE membrane (PVdF-HFP to lithiated polymer ...)', 'Electrochemical stability window of the blend SIPE membrane', and 'Li metal/SIPE membrane/Li metal'. DSC/TGA were also performed on the lithiated polymer powder.

The lithiated polymer (5) was used as the single-ion conducting component of gel-type polymer electrolyte membranes (PVdF-HFP:lithiated polymer = 1:3, swollen in EC:PC (1:1)). At 20 °C, ionic conductivity increased from 0.10 mS/cm (1:1) to 0.52 mS/cm for the 1:3 membrane; at 60 °C, 1.08 mS/cm was achieved (EIS). The lithium-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.9 was determined for the 1:3 membrane at 40 °C by potentiostatic polarization with impedance. 7Li self-diffusion for 1:3 was 4.62×10^-11 m^2/s at 20 °C (PFG-NMR). The swollen 1:3 membrane showed an oxidative stability onset at ~4.6 V vs. Li/Li+ (LSV, 20 °C). In symmetric Li/Li cells at 60 °C, stable stripping/plating at 0.1 mA/cm^2 was sustained for ~1100 h. NMC111/Li cells using this membrane delivered 163 mAh/g (0.05C) and 161 mAh/g (0.1C) at 60 °C, and 142 mAh/g (0.05C) and 116 mAh/g (0.1C) at 20 °C, with post-formation coulombic efficiencies ~97.5% (60 °C) and ~99.2% (20 °C).

PEL is a composite solid electrolyte used in Li-ion batteries. Results state: "when the LLZTO content is 10 wt.% , the composite solid electrolytes exhibit the highest ionic conductivity of 5.1×10^-4 S/cm, a wide electrochemical window of 4.6 V, and a lithium-ion transference number of 0.41." The LFP/PEL/Li cell shows: initial discharge capacity 132 mAh g^-1 at 0.1 C and 108 mAh g^-1 after 100 cycles (81% retention). Table 1 lists ionic conductivity peaking at 5.1×10^-4 S/cm for 10 wt.% LLZTO.

PVDF-HFP-PEO-SiO2 GPE exhibited an ionic conductivity of 1.12 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 (Fig. 4b: “The ionic conductivity of ... PVDF-HFP-PEO-SiO2 GPEs can be calculated to be 1.12 × 10^-3 S cm^-1”). Additional performance for this material includes tLi+ = 0.49 (Fig. 5), anodic limiting potential 4.7 V (Fig. 4a), initial discharge capacity 152.5 mAh g^-1 and ~100% retention with 146.3 mAh g^-1 after 200 cycles at 0.1C (Fig. 6b,c), 115.5 mAh g^-1 at 2C (Fig. 6d), and stable Li/Li plating-stripping for 1000 h at 0.1 mA cm^-2 and 600 h at 0.2 mA cm^-2 (Fig. 7a,b).

Use-case: PVDF-HFP membrane as gel polymer electrolyte (GPE)/separator for Li-ion batteries. Key performance for pure PVDF-HFP GPE: ionic conductivity 2.46×10^-4 S cm^-1 (Fig. 4b: “the ionic conductivity of … pure PVDF-HFP … can be calculated to be 2.46 × 10^-4 S cm^-1”), Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.39 (Fig. 5: “the pure PVDF-HFP GPE (t_Li+ = 0.39)”), porosity 41% and electrolyte uptake 181% (Fig. 3b: “the pure PVDF-HFP (41%) … corresponding liquid electrolyte uptake … 181%”). Thermal safety as separator: shrinkage ~4% at 150 °C and no significant change at 175 °C (Fig. 2a,b: “the dimensional changes of the pure PVDF-HFP … were only 4% at 150 °C … did not exhibit significant dimensional changes at … 175 °C”); decomposition ~415 °C (Fig. 2c: “PVDF-HFP membrane started to decompose at around 415 °C”). Battery performance with LiFePO4 cathode using pure PVDF-HFP GPE: “exhibited a serious capacity fading after 30 cycles, and only retained the capacity of ∼50 mA h g^-1 after 100 cycles” at 0.1C (Fig. 6c). Symmetric Li/Li stability: overpotential increased sharply after ~150 h indicating dendrite growth/short circuit (Fig. 7a). Capacity field reported as 50 mAh/g corresponds to LiFePO4/PVDF-HFP GPE/Li cell after 100 cycles at 0.1C.

TF+HCE (fluorinated gel polymer electrolyte) shows high ionic conductivity of 4.02 mS cm^-1 at 30 °C and Li+ transference number of 0.63, yielding Li+ conductivity 2.53 mS cm^-1. It enables Li||Cu CE of 99.17% for 300 cycles at 1 mA cm^-2, 2 mAh cm^-2, and 98.73% for 1000 cycles at 1 mA cm^-2, 1 mAh cm^-2. Li||Li symmetric cells cycle stably for 2000 h at 1 mA cm^-2, 2 mAh cm^-2; CCD reaches 4.4 mA cm^-2. In Li||NCM811 full cells with lean Li (45 μm) and high loading (2.09 mAh cm^-2), TF+HCE retains 80% capacity for 180 cycles at 2.8–4.4 V and maintains ≥80% retention over >500 cycles at 2–3 C (e.g., 85% over 547 cycles at 2 C; 80% over 665 cycles at 3 C). A Li||NCM811 pouch cell achieves 465.63 Wh kg^-1, and a 2.5 Ah Gr||NCM811 pouch retains ~95% capacity over 200 cycles.

In Li|FGPE|NCM811 cells, the discharge specific capacity reached 159.1 mAh/g at 5C; capacities at 0.1–2C were 224.1, 218.3, 205.6, 195.5, and 183.3 mAh/g (Figure 5a,b). At 1C, the initial specific capacity was 201.7 mAh/g with 78.6% retention after 200 cycles (Figure 5c,d). Additional FGPE performance supporting its battery-electrolyte application: ionic conductivity 4.53×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 25 °C (Figure 2b), electrochemical window up to 5.0 V (Figure 2c), Li+ transference number 0.43 (Figure S7), Li|FGPE||Li symmetric cell stable >2000 h at 1 mA cm^-2 with ~55 mV overpotential (Figure 4c), and Li|FGPE||Cu average Coulombic efficiency 98.8% with nucleation overpotential 67 mV (Figure 3c).
The provided excerpt only includes associated content, author information, and acknowledgments; no application or performance data for LTOC-0%F is present.

The provided excerpts only include supporting information notice, author information, and acknowledgments, without any R&D results, tables, figure captions, or experimental details containing application/performance data for LTOC-5%F.

No application/performance data for LTOC-10%F in the provided text; only supporting-information notice, author information, notes, and acknowledgments are present, with no Results/Discussion, tables, figures, or experimental details.
The provided excerpt includes only Supporting Information notice, author information, notes, and acknowledgments, with no application or performance data for LTOC-15%F.
The provided excerpt only contains Supporting Information notice, author list, and acknowledgments, with no application or performance data related to LTOC-20%F.

Supporting Information lists 'preparation of PVT electrolyte', 'leakage current curve of PVT electrolyte', and 'lithium ion transference numbers ... in orthogonal experiments', indicating PVT is used as a Li+ conducting electrolyte.

The provided excerpts discuss a PVT electrolyte and general literature but do not mention the material PVE or provide any performance data tied to it; therefore no application information for PVE can be extracted.

Direct performance for P(HFBA1:1-co-MEA)/LiTFSI: “The ionic conductivity of P(HFBA1:1 - co - MEA) / LiTFSI reached 4.83×10^-5 S cm^-1.” Additional corroborating application metrics for this electrolyte include: “the t_Li+ of P(HFBA1:1 - co - MEA) is 0.36,” “The electrochemical window of the P(HFBA1:1 - co - MEA) / LiTFSI electrolyte exceeded 4.9 V,” symmetric Li|P(HFBA1:1 - co - MEA)/LiTFSI|Li “exhibited an extremely stable voltage over 1000 h” at 0.05 mA cm^-2, NMC cell rate capacities “135.5, 121.3, and 93.6 mAh g^-1 at 0.1, 0.2, and 0.5C,” and LFP cell performance “initial discharge capacity of 159.4 mAh g^-1 and a Coulombic efficiency of 99% over 200 cycles.” These are all explicitly tied to the P(HFBA1:1-co-MEA)/LiTFSI electrolyte.
Paper title states: "PVDF-HFP-based polymer electrolyte producing LiF-rich interface for solid-state lithium metal batteries"

Title states: "Fluorinate localized solvation PVDF-HFP-based polymer electrolyte producing LiF-rich interface for solid-state lithium metal batteries". No performance metrics or conditions are provided in the supplied text.

In Li|LiFePO4 cells using freestanding electrolyte films, the 75 vol% LiFSI/EMI-FSI sample delivered 46 mAh g^-1 at 2C (Figure 2 sentence: “...2C (46 mAhg^-1) for the battery sample using a freestanding electrolyte film with 75 vol% LiFSI/EMI-FSI.”). Tests were performed after charging at 0.1C under 35 °C (Experimental and Figure 2 caption). It was also noted that for 75 vol% the discharging capacity could not reach 150 mAh g^-1 even at 0.1C (Results, Figure 2 discussion).

The paper tests Li|LiFePO4 cells using freestanding electrolyte films at 75, 77.5, 80, 82.5, and 85 vol% LiFSI/EMI-FSI (Fig. 2). However, explicit capacity/retention values are only reported for 85 vol% (e.g., 133 mAh g^-1 at 2C) and for the group "over 80 vol%" at 1C; the phrase "over 80 vol%" excludes the 80 vol% sample. For 80 vol% specifically, no numeric capacity or cycle data are provided in text/figures captions. Table I lists transport properties for 80 vol% (e.g., D_Li = 2.6×10^-11 m^2 s^-1, t_Li = 0.27 at 35°C), but the schema has no field for these, and no battery performance numbers (mAh g^-1, retention) are given for 80 vol%.

For the freestanding electrolyte film with 85 vol% LiFSI/EMI-FSI, the single-layer Li|LiFePO4 cell showed: (i) high capacity (>150 mAh g^-1) at 1C (0.6 mA cm^-2) based on 0.1C; (ii) best performance: 84% of capacity, 133 mAh g^-1 at 2C (1.2 mA cm^-2) based on 0.1C; (iii) discharge even at 100C with 22 mAh g^-1; (iv) stable cycling at 1C with ~150 mAh g^-1 and coulombic efficiency >99.95% up to 200 cycles. These data are explicitly reported for the 85 vol% LiFSI/EMI-FSI electrolyte.

For HBPS-(PMMA-b-PPEGMA)30/BMImPF6/LiTFSI used as solid electrolyte in Li/LiFePO4 cells: “after 100 cycles, discharge capacity can still reach 115.8 mAh g−1 with a retention ratio of about 96% (under the current density of 0.1 C) at 60°C” (Fig. 8). Supporting electrolyte metrics for this material include ionic conductivity 4.1×10^−5 S cm−1 at room temperature and 3.0×10^−4 S cm−1 at 60°C (40 wt% BMImPF6), oxidation potential 4.3 V vs Li+/Li (30 wt% BMImPF6), and tLi+ = 0.03 at 60°C (Table 1, 30 wt% BMImPF6).

HBPS-(PMMA-b-PPEGMA)30/BMImPF6/LiTFSI electrolyte shows ionic conductivity of 3.0×10^-4 S cm^-1 at 60 °C. For the BMImPF6 system with 40 wt% ionic liquid, the room-temperature conductivity is 4.1×10^-5 S cm^-1. The oxidation potential of the HBPS-(PMMA-b-PPEGMA)30/BMImPF6/LiTFSI electrolyte (30 wt% BMImPF6) reaches 4.3 V vs Li+/Li. The lithium-ion transference number tLi+ for this BMImPF6-based electrolyte (30 wt% BMImPF6) is 0.03 at 60 °C. In Li/LiFePO4 cells using this electrolyte, after 100 cycles at 0.1 C and 60 °C, the discharge capacity is 115.8 mAh g^-1 with ~96% retention.

For Li/40PDFP-POPM GPE/LiFePO4, the discharge capacity reached 145 mAh g^-1 after 100 cycles with coulombic efficiency ~96.3% (Fig. 8b; Conclusion: "The discharge capacity of Li/40PDFP-POPM GPE/LiFePO4 reached 145 mAh g^-1 after the 100 cycles and the columbic efficiency was maintained over 95% during the 100 cycles"). The electrolyte stability window for all samples (including 40PDFP-POPM GPE) was over 4.7 V vs. Li/Li+ (Fig. 4). The Li+ transference number for 40PDFP-POPM-based GPE was 0.692 (Table 3).

Membrane F4 (PE(F4)) is used as a polymer electrolyte in a Li/PE(F4)/LiCoO2 cell. Table 4 and Fig. 11 report that F4 exhibited an ionic conductivity of 1.00 × 10^-4 S cm^-1 at 23 °C: “membrane F4 exhibited an ionic conductivity of 1.00×10^-4 S cm^-1 at ambient temperature.” The material is explicitly evaluated as the electrolyte in Li/PE(F4)/LiCoO2 (Electrochemical analysis section).

UiO-66-(F)4 is used as MOF filler in a composite solid polymer electrolyte (CSPE15-12, 12 wt.% UiO-66-(F)4). The electrolyte with MOF fillers exhibits an ionic conductivity of 3.01×10^-5 S·cm^-1 at 30 °C (“The highest ionic conductivity (3.01×10^-5 S·cm^-1 at 30 °C) is obtained when 12 wt.% UiO-66-(F)4 is added. Therefore, the CSPE15-12 membranes are chosen to conduct the following tests.”). The filler also increases the Li+ transference number to 0.54 and broadens the electrochemical window to 4.3 V, supporting its role in Li+ conduction (Li/SPE15-0/Li t_Li+ = 0.23 → Li/CSPE15-12/Li t_Li+ = 0.54; LSV shows 4.0 V → 4.3 V after adding UiO-66-(F)4).

SPE15-0 is the polymer electrolyte without MOF fillers (0 wt.% UiO-66-(F)4). Performance for SPE15-0: ionic conductivity 7.53×10^-6 S·cm^-1 at 30 °C (“By contrast, the ionic conductivity of SPE15-0 was only 7.53×10−6 S·cm−1 at 30 °C.”). Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.23 (“the t_Li+ value of the obtained electrolyte is improved from 0.23 to 0.54 after composition”). Electrochemical stability window ≈4.0 V (“after the addition of the MOF fillers, the electrochemical window of the electrolyte becomes broaden from 4.0 to 4.3 V”). Li/SPE15-0/Li symmetric cell overpotential ~80 mV and shorted after 396 h (“Both … show a lower overpotential of around 80 mV … the Li/SPE15-0/Li cell encountered short-cut after cycled for only 396 h”). LFP/SPE15-0/Li cell failed after 26 cycles at 0.2 C, 60 °C (“the cell based on SPE15-0 fails after only 26 cycles”).

CSPE15-9 is one of the prepared composite solid polymer electrolyte membranes (9 wt.% UiO-66-(F)4): "Free-standing electrolyte membranes SPE15-0, CSPE15-9, CSPE15-12, and CSPE15-15 (containing 0 wt.%, 9 wt.%, 12 wt.%, and 15 wt.% UiO-66-(F), respectively) were obtained..." It is used as a Li-ion conducting solid electrolyte (electrolyte membranes measured by EIS; Li/electrolyte/Li and LFP/electrolyte/Li cell configurations are discussed). However, explicit performance values in the text (ionic conductivity, t_Li+, electrochemical window, cycling capacities) are reported for CSPE15-12 (and SPE15-0) and not for CSPE15-9; the authors state "Therefore, the CSPE15-12 membranes are chosen to conduct the following tests." Hence no numeric performance specific to CSPE15-9 can be extracted.

CSPE15-12 is a composite solid polymer electrolyte evaluated in Li metal batteries. Evidence: (1) All-solid-state LFP/CSPE15-12/Li coin cell: “The LFP/CSPE15-12/Li cell remained a high capacity of 141.4 mAh·g−1 after 200 cycles” at 0.2 C and 60 °C (Fig. 9a and text). (2) Li/CSPE15-12/Li symmetric cell: “the Li/CSPE15-12/Li cell stably cycled for more than 1,000 h” at 0.1 mA·cm−2 and 0.1 mAh·cm−2, 60 °C (Fig. 8c). (3) Supporting electrolyte metrics for CSPE15-12: ionic conductivity 3.01×10−5 S·cm−1 at 30 °C; tLi+ = 0.54; electrochemical window broadened to 4.3 V (vs. Li+/Li). These results are explicitly reported for CSPE15-12 and underpin its application as a solid-state battery electrolyte.

CSPE15-15 is a composite solid polymer electrolyte membrane containing 15 wt.% UiO-66-(F)4: "Free-standing electrolyte membranes ... CSPE15-15 (containing 15 wt.% UiO-66-(F), respectively)" (Section 2.1.2). Its role is as an electrolyte for all-solid-state lithium metal batteries (Section 2.2.2 and throughout Results). Performance trends include Fig. 5(a), where "the ionic conductivities of the electrolyte membranes first increase with the increase of content of the UiO-66-(F)4 and then decrease. A lower ionic conductivity of CSPE15-15 may be caused by the aggregation of excessive nanofillers." However, numerical performance values and battery cycling data were only provided for CSPE15-12: "Therefore, the CSPE15-12 membranes are chosen to conduct the following tests." Hence, no quantitative metrics specific to CSPE15-15 are extractable.

Performance for the sample marked as pristine (0 wt.% Li-X) is explicitly reported. At 0.5 C, the full cell with pure PVDF-HFP electrolyte delivered a 113.8 mAh/g capacity: "when the rate increases to 0.5 C, the battery with zeolite composite electrolytes could deliver an average capacity of 153.8 mAh/g, which is much more than that of pure PVDF-HFP electrolyte with a 113.8 mAh/g capacity." Electrochemical window for pristine shows earlier oxidation onset: "the current signal of pristine dramatically arises above a low voltage of 3.8 V vs. Li|Li+ resulting from DMF decomposition." Additionally, at 0.1 mA/cm^2 in Li|CPEs|Li symmetric cells, the pristine electrolyte short-circuits, while Li-X-3% remains stable, indicating inferior cycling stability for pristine.

Li-X-1% (1 wt.% Li-X in PVDF-HFP) is explicitly prepared as a composite polymer electrolyte sample (Section 2.3) and evaluated among Li-X@PVDF-HFP electrolytes. Electrochemical window measurements (Fig. 4a) indicate that all zeolite-introduced electrolytes, which include Li-X-1%, are stable up to ~4.7 V vs. Li|Li+. Ionic conductivity and t_Li+ results are presented across contents with a numeric value only reported for Li-X-3% (1.98e-4 S/cm at 80 °C) (Fig. 4b), not specifically for Li-X-1%, so no quantitative metric specific to Li-X-1% can be extracted.

Li-X-2% is defined as a Li-X@PVDF-HFP composite electrolyte with 2 wt.% Li-X ("the composite electrolytes with different content of Li-X ... were marked as ... Li-X-2%"; Section 2.3). These Li-X@PVDF-HFP electrolytes are evaluated as solid-state lithium battery electrolytes (Sections 2.5.4 and 3.3). After introducing zeolite, the LSV curves (for all zeolite-containing samples, which include Li-X-2%) are stable up to 4.7 V vs. Li/Li+ ("after introducing zeolite, the LSV curves are stable and smooth up to 4.7 V vs. Li|Li+"; Fig. 4a, Section 3.3). However, explicit numeric ionic conductivity and t_Li+ are only reported for Li-X-3% (e.g., 1.98×10^-4 S/cm at 80 °C; tLi+ = 0.55), and other samples’ values (including Li-X-2%) appear only in plots without tabulated readings (Fig. 4b,c), so no numeric performance is extracted for Li-X-2%.

For Li-X-3% (3 wt.% Li-X in PVDF-HFP), the paper states that this electrolyte exhibits the highest ionic conductivity of 1.98×10^-4 S/cm at 80 °C (Fig. 4b). Additional context supporting its role as a solid-state battery electrolyte includes: electrochemical window stable up to 4.7 V vs Li/Li+ after introducing zeolite (Fig. 4a); lowest activation energy Ea = 0.21 eV and highest t_Li+ = 0.55 among the compositions; Li|Li-X-3%|Li symmetric cell operated smoothly for over 700 h at 0.1 mA/cm^2 and for 130 h at 0.2 mA/cm^2; Li|Li-X-3%|LiFePO4 full cell showed stable cycling over 500 cycles at 0.1 C with ~99.6% Coulombic efficiency and delivered ~153.8 mAh/g at 0.5 C.

The sample set includes Li-X-4%: “the composite electrolytes with different content of Li-X (0 wt.%–4 wt.%) in PVDF-HFP were marked as pristine, Li-X-1%, Li-X-2%, Li-X-3%, and Li-X-4%.” Electrochemical window: “after introducing zeolite, the LSV curves are stable and smooth up to 4.7 V vs. Li|Li+.” Performance note specific to high loading: “such as 4% could impede the migration of lithium ions and increase their migration energy barrier, due to limited DMF and more grain boundaries.” No numeric ionic conductivity, transference number, cycling, or other metrics were reported specifically for Li-X-4% in the text (values given focus on Li-X-3% or figures without tabulated numbers), so fields remain null.

As shown in Fig. 3(c), the addition of SN significantly increases the liquid electrolyte absorptivity of the composite polymer membranes... Specifically, the liquid electrolyte absorptivity of the PPEM, PSPEM-1, PSPEM-2, and PSPEM-3 are 158%, 180%, 209%, and 222%, respectively.

Electrolyte uptake directly reported for the membranes: "the liquid electrolyte absorptivity of the PPEM, PSPEM-1, PSPEM-2, and PSPEM-3 are 158%, 180%, 209%, and 222%, respectively." Electrochemical performance (ionic conductivity, tLi+, cycling) was measured on PSGPE-2 (gel after soaking), not on PSPEM-2, so only uptake is extracted for PSPEM-2.

PSPEM-3 is the PVDF-HFP:SN = 7:3 polymer electrolyte membrane. The paper reports: "Specifically, the liquid electrolyte absorptivity of the PPEM, PSPEM-1, PSPEM-2, and PSPEM-3 are 158%, 180%, 209%, and 222%, respectively." The membranes are used as hosts for gel electrolytes obtained by soaking PEMs in liquid electrolyte for 1 h.

PPEM is the pure PVDF-HFP polymer electrolyte membrane. Figure 3(c) reports the electrolyte absorptivity (uptake) of PPEM as 158%, with uptake defined by Eq. (1). Other electrochemical metrics (oxidation voltage, ionic conductivity, Li+ transference number, cell performance) are for GPEs (PGPE/PSGPE) after soaking and thus not directly for PPEM.

PSGPE-1 is a PVDF-HFP–SN gel polymer electrolyte evaluated in LiFePO4//graphite LIB coin cells. Its ionic conductivity at 30 °C is ~1.0 mS·cm−1 (Fig. 4c: 'At 30 °C, the ionic conductivities for PGPE, PSGPE-1, PSGPE-2, and PSGPE-3 are ~0.81, 1.0, 1.07, and 1.35 mS·cm−1, respectively.'). Additional context: oxidation stability for PSGPE-1/2 is ~5.17 V (Fig. 4a), and the full-cell discharge capacity at 1 C with PSGPE-1 is 98.3 mAh·g−1 (Fig. 5c).

Direct data for PSGPE-2: (i) ionic conductivity at 30 °C = 1.07 mS·cm−1 ("At 30 °C, the ionic conductivities for ... PSGPE-2 ... are ... 1.07 mS·cm−1"); (ii) lithium-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.69 ("The PSGPE-2 reaches a high t_Li+ of 0.69"); (iii) oxidation stability window ~5.17 V ("The oxidation voltage slightly decreases to 5.17 V (PSGPE-1 and PSGPE-2)"); (iv) full-cell performance using PSGPE-2: 106.2 mAh·g−1 at 1C and 119.2 mAh·g−1 at the 350th cycle with 93.1% capacity retention at 0.5C.

PSGPE-3 is a PVDF-HFP/SN (7:3) gel polymer electrolyte used in LiFePO4//graphite coin cells. At 30 °C, its ionic conductivity is reported as 1.35 mS/cm among PGPE, PSGPE-1, PSGPE-2, and PSGPE-3 (Fig. 4(c)). LSV shows PSGPE-3 has an anodic limiting potential of 4.92 V at 25 °C (Fig. 4(a)). In full cells, PSGPE-3 delivers 109.5 mAh/g at 1 C and 127.3 mAh/g after activation at 0.1 C when cycled at 0.5 C, but retains 56.4% after 350 cycles (Fig. 5).

PGPE is a PVDF-HFP-based gel polymer electrolyte tested in Li cells. Reported for PGPE: (i) ionic conductivity at 30 °C is ~0.81 mS·cm−1: "At 30 °C, the ionic conductivities for PGPE, PSGPE-1, PSGPE-2, and PSGPE-3 are ∼ 0.81, 1.0, 1.07, and 1.35 mS·cm−1, respectively." (ii) Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.58 for PGPE: "higher than that of PGPE (tLi+ = 0.58)". (iii) Oxidation stability: "the PGPE exhibits the highest oxidation voltage (5.34 V)". These confirm its role and performance as an electrolyte; the capacity field records its ionic conductivity.

The sample 35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB is explicitly abbreviated as SGPE. For SGPE, the paper reports: “SGPE exhibits high ionic conductivity of 1.03 mS cm−1 at 30 °C.” Additional SGPE-specific performance includes: Li|SGPE|Li symmetric cell stable for >1000 h at 0.1 mA cm−2; oxidation onset ~5.1 V vs Li/Li+; Li+ transference number tLi+ = 0.41 at 30 °C; LFP|SGPE|Li retains ~65.8% over 500 cycles with 110.4 mAh g−1 at 0.5C; LCO|SGPE|Li delivers 106.1 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles (76.1% retention). These confirm the application of SGPE as a lithium battery electrolyte.

No application performance is reported for 35TFMA-65TEP. Section 2.2.2 states these were samples prepared for FTIR/Raman verification of the anchoring effect: "The samples for FTIR characterization were prepared by mixing TEP with TFMA... the finally obtained samples were named 35TFMA-65TEP, 40TFMA-60TEP, 60TFMA-40TEP and 80TFMA-20TEP..." Performance results (ionic conductivity, tLi+, cycling, etc.) are given for SGPE (35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB) rather than 35TFMA-65TEP.

The sample "40TFMA-60TEP" appears only as an FTIR characterization specimen: "the finally obtained samples were named 35TFMA-65TEP, 40TFMA-60TEP, 60TFMA-40TEP and 80TFMA-20TEP according to gradient volumetric fraction" (Section 2.2.2). Subsequent discussion (e.g., Fig. 7b,c) analyzes -P=O peak shifts and anchored TEP fractions across TFMA contents but does not report electrochemical or battery performance metrics for 40TFMA-60TEP. Performance data are provided for SGPE (35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB) and other electrolytes, not for 40TFMA-60TEP.

The sample 60TFMA-40TEP is only described in Section 2.2.2 as an FTIR characterization sample (prepared by mixing TFMA and TEP and polymerizing with AIBN) to verify the anchoring effect; no electrochemical/battery application tests or performance metrics are reported for this specific composition.

The only mention of 80TFMA-20TEP is as an FTIR sample: "The samples for FTIR characterization were prepared by mixing TEP with TFMA in volumetric ratios of 35:65, 40:60, 60:40 and 80:20... the finally obtained samples were named 35TFMA-65TEP, 40TFMA-60TEP, 60TFMA-40TEP and 80TFMA-20TEP". No electrochemical/battery performance data were reported for 80TFMA-20TEP.

The sample 35HEMA-65TEP is only described as an FTIR verification sample: “Samples of 35HEMA-65TEP, 40HEMA-60TEP, 60HEMA-40TEP and 80HEMA-20TEP can be obtained by replacing TFMA with HEMA under the same synthetizing process.” (Section 2.2.2). No electrochemical or application performance data (battery tests, conductivity, cycling, etc.) are reported for 35HEMA-65TEP specifically.

The paper only prepares 40HEMA-60TEP for spectroscopy verification, not for electrochemical application testing. Section 2.2.2: "The samples for FTIR characterization were prepared by mixing TEP with TFMA in volumetric ratios of 35:65, 40:60, 60:40 and 80:20... Samples of 35HEMA-65TEP, 40HEMA-60TEP, 60HEMA-40TEP and 80HEMA-20TEP can be obtained by replacing TFMA with HEMA under the same synthetizing process." No battery or performance data are reported for 40HEMA-60TEP specifically.

The material '60HEMA-40TEP' is only listed as an FTIR verification sample (Section 2.2.2: “Samples of 35HEMA-65TEP, 40HEMA-60TEP, 60HEMA-40TEP and 80HEMA-20TEP...”). It is not employed in electrochemical/battery tests, and no application performance metrics are reported for this specific composition.

80HEMA-20TEP appears only in the FTIR verification set (Section 2.2.2: “Samples of … 80HEMA-20TEP can be obtained by replacing TFMA with HEMA under the same synthetizing process”) and no electrochemical/battery performance was reported for this specific composition. All performance data (ionic conductivity, t_Li+, cycling, etc.) are for SGPE (35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB) or CGPE (35HEMA-0.5LF-0.5LB), not 80HEMA-20TEP.

The material 1 M-SGPE is introduced only for spectroscopic verification of coordination structure, not for device performance. Evidence: “The final samples can be obtained after heating at 60 °C for 12 h and were labeled as 1 M-SGPE and 1 M-CGPE, respectively.” (Section 2.2.2). Raman discussion: “As demonstrated in the peak deconvolution results, TFSI ions interact with more Li ions in 1 M-SGPE compared with the 1 M-CGPE…” (Fig. 7g text). No electrochemical performance (conductivity, tLi+, cell cycling) is reported specifically for 1 M-SGPE; such metrics are given for SGPE (35TFMA-0.5LF-0.5LB) elsewhere.

The paper explicitly prepares and labels a sample "1 M-CGPE" only for Raman characterization: "The final samples can be obtained after heating at 60 °C for 12 h and were labeled as 1 M-SGPE and 1 M-CGPE, respectively." It is later discussed qualitatively: "As demonstrated in the peak deconvolution results, TFSI ions interact with more Li ions in 1 M-SGPE compared with the 1 M-CGPE..." No quantitative electrochemical performance or battery application metrics are reported for 1 M-CGPE; thus application fields are left null.

Use as electrolyte in Li metal batteries is explicit: symmetric cells Li/F-QSCE@30/Li and full cells with LFP or NCM622. Reported performance includes: “F-QSCE@30 has an ionic conductivity of 1.21 mScm−1 at 25 °C” and a “wide electrochemical stability window of 5.20 V.” Also, “the Li/F-QSCE@30/Li symmetric cell demonstrates a low overpotential of 24.2 mV and remains stable cycling for over 4000 h.” The ion-transport target is Li+: e.g., higher t_Li+ (0.41 at 60 °C) and exchange current density at Li interface.

SiO2@IL was prepared by confining PYR13TFSI in SiO2 (molar ratio 23:1) and used as fillers to prepare QSCEs; the optimized electrolyte F-QSCE@30 contains 5 wt% SiO2@IL. All performance metrics reported (ionic conductivity, electrochemical window, cycling, etc.) are for the composite electrolytes (F-QSCE@30 or QSCE@30), not for SiO2@IL alone. (Sections 2.2, 3.1)

application.target_species: TFEMA (2,2,2-trifluoroethyl methacrylate); monomers with double bonds

parse.why: “Tailor-made 21-β-CD-Br with multiple active sites was synthesized by an esterification reaction and acted as a highly efficient multifunctional macroinitiator for initiating the ATRP of monomers with double bonds.” “All hydroxyl groups of β-CD were substituted with bromine groups, which were subsequently utilized as ATRP initiating sites for polymerizing the functional fluorene-containing methacrylate-type monomers.” “Based on this, a series of 21-β-CD-g-PTFEMA with high molecular weights were synthesized by ATRP using 21-β-CD-Br as a multifunctional macroinitiator and CuCl/bpy as the cocatalyst.” Quantitative initiator performance metrics (e.g., efficiency/yield values) are not reported in the main text.

In FMC-ASPEs containing 21-β-CD-g-PTFEMA, the ionic conductivity reached 6.43×10^-4 Scm^-1 at 80 °C for FMC-ASPE-Li (Fig. 3a,b; text). The calculated t_Li+ of FMC-ASPE-Li was 0.88 (Fig. 3c,d; text). The improvements are explicitly attributed to adding 21-β-CD-g-PTFEMA to PEO-based ASPEs.

PEO-ASPE-Li is used as a polymer electrolyte in all-solid-state Li metal batteries. Reported properties for PEO-ASPE-Li include: (i) electrochemical window: "The oxidation potential of PEO-ASPEs has been verified to be ~3.8 V vs. Li+/Li" (used as reference in LSV, Fig. 2a); (ii) mechanical properties: "A tensile strength (s) of 1.6 MPa and elongation at break (εb) of 649% were obtained for PEO-ASPE-Li" (Fig. 2c); (iii) thermal behavior: "once the temperature increased above 70 °C, the PEO-ASPE-Li membrane incurred sudden and severe dimensional shrinkage and melted completely at 120 °C" (Fig. 2d); (iv) ion transport: the lithium transference number for PEO-ASPE-Li is tLi+ = 0.21 (Fig. 5a, Supplementary Table 11); (v) interfacial behavior: after ten plating/stripping cycles (0.1 mA cm^-2, 0.1 mAh cm^-2), a mossy Li layer thickness of ~20 μm was observed at the PEO-ASPE-Li|Li interface (Fig. 5l) indicating heterogeneous nucleation and dendrite growth.

PEO-ASPE-Na is the PEO-based all-solid-state polymer electrolyte for sodium metal batteries used as the baseline against FMC-ASPE-Na. The paper reports: "The determined t_Na+ value of FMC-ASPE-Na (0.71) was 3.5 times larger than that for PEO-ASPE-Na (t_Na+ = 0.2, see Fig. 5a, Supplementary Fig. 54, and Supplementary Table 14)." Methods specify t_Na+ was measured in Na|ASPE-Na|Na symmetric cells at 80 °C. Additionally, the oxidation potential of PEO-ASPEs is given as ~3.5 V vs. Na+/Na, but no other quantitative Na metrics for PEO-ASPE-Na are provided in the main text.

FMC-ASPE-Li is an all-solid-state polymer electrolyte for lithium metal batteries (AS-LMBs). Key performance evidence for FMC-ASPE-Li: (1) Electrochemical stability: oxidation potential increased to ~4.7 V vs. Li+/Li per LSV (Fig. 2a); (2) Ionic conductivity: 6.43×10^-4 S/cm at 80 °C (Fig. 3a); (3) Cation transference number: t_Li+ = 0.88 (Fig. 3c,d; Supplementary Table 11); (4) Symmetric cell cycling: Li|FMC-ASPE-Li|Li cycled >3800 h at 0.1 mA/cm^2 and 70 °C with ~0.1 V polarization, no short circuit (Fig. 5n); (5) Thermal stability: minimal dimensional change up to 120 °C vs. PEO-ASPE-Li; decomposition onset ~400 °C from TG-FTIR/TGA (Fig. 2d,e; Supplementary Figs. 47, 48); (6) Mechanical properties (membrane): tensile strength 2.0 MPa, elongation at break 2198%, toughness 3.36×10^4 kJ m^-4 (Fig. 2c; Supplementary Table 5); (7) Full-cell performance (CR2032, 70 °C): LMFP|FMC-ASPE-Li|Li: 91% discharge capacity retention after 200 cycles at 70 mA/g, 2.5–4.2 V (Fig. 6a–c); SC-NMC811|FMC-ASPE-Li|Li: 87% after 100 cycles at 80 mA/g, 2.7–4.2 V (Fig. 6d–f); LFP|FMC-ASPE-Li|Li: 98% after 400 cycles at 70 mA/g, 2.5–3.8 V (Fig. 6g–i). Optimal composition from orthogonal design for FMC-ASPE-Li: 21-β-CD-g-PTFEMA Mn ≈ 900 kDa, PEO:21-β-CD-g-PTFEMA mass ratio 2:1, LiTFSI:(PEO+polymer) mass ratio 20% (Table 1).

For FMC-ASPE-Na (fluorine-rich macromolecule-containing all-solid-state polymer electrolyte for Na): (1) Ionic conductivity: "the ionic conductivity reached 8.43×10^-4 Scm^-1 at 80 °C for FMC-ASPE-Na" (Fig. 3b). (2) Electrochemical stability window: "The oxidation potentials of the FMC-ASPEs increased to ... 5.0 V in the AS-SMBs" (LSV, Fig. 2b). (3) Cation transference number: "the t_Na+ of FMC-ASPE-Na was ~0.71" (Fig. 3e,f; text). (4) Full-cell performance using this electrolyte: "NVP|FMC-ASPE-Na|Na full cells showed cycling stability of 89% after 400 charge and discharge cycles (60 mA g^-1, 2.5–3.8 V, 80 °C)" (Fig. 7g–i). Optimal Na-electrolyte formulation used subsequently: 21-β-CD-g-PTFEMA Mn ≈ 900 kDa, PEO:21-β-CD-g-PTFEMA mass ratio 4:1, NaTFSI-to-polymer mass ratio 15% (Table 2).

P(IL-OFHDODA-VEC) is used as a solid polymer electrolyte enabling Li+ conduction. Its ionic conductivity reached 1.37 mS cm^-1 at 25 °C with optimized monomer ratio ("...further increased from 0.49 to 1.37 mS cm^-1 by incorporating VEC... at 25 °C (Fig. 2b)"). Additional application-relevant metrics for this electrolyte include t_Li+ ~0.40 at 25 °C (Fig. 2c), ESW ~5.08 V vs. Li+/Li (Fig. 3a), stable Li|Li cycling with ~43 mV polarization up to 2500 h (Fig. 4a), and full-cell performance Li|P(IL-OFHDODA-VEC)|NCM523: initial discharge 164.19 mAh g^-1 at 0.5C and 146.96 mAh g^-1 after 200 cycles at 30 °C (Fig. 6a,b).

For P(IL-OFHDODA) at 25°C, with [LiTFSI]:[monomers]=1:2 and [IL]:[OFHDODA] tuned from 10.5:2 to 2.5:2, the maximum ionic conductivity reached 0.16 mS/cm at 8.5:2 (Fig. 2a). The ESW of P(IL-OFHDODA) was 5.31 V vs Li+/Li (Fig. 3b). Its Li+ transport activation energy from 25–75°C was 0.43 eV (Fig. 2e).

Electrochemical stability window (ESW) for P(IL-VEC) is reported as 4.39 V (vs. Li+/Li) (Fig. 3b: "ESW of ... P(IL-VEC) ... 4.39 V (vs. Li+/Li)"). Lithium symmetric cell with the SPE without OFHDODA (i.e., P(IL-VEC)) short-circuited after 388 h at a charge density of 0.1 mAh/cm2 (Fig. 4a: "the SPE without OFHDODA-based cell short-circuited after 388 h").
Figure 3b: "the extracted ESW of PIL, POFHDODA, PVEC, P(IL-VEC), P(IL-OFHDODA) and P(OFHDODA-VEC) is 5.51, 5.13, 4.35, 4.39, 5.31, and 5.12 V (vs. Li+/Li), respectively." (LSV of Li|SPE|C).

Figure 3b shows the electrochemical stability window (ESW) of PIL is 5.51 V (vs. Li+/Li) from LSV measurements on Li|SPE|carbon, evidencing PIL’s application as a solid polymer electrolyte in Li-based batteries.

POFHDODA (homopolymer of OFHDODA) was evaluated for electrochemical stability: Figure 3b reports an ESW of 5.13 V (vs. Li+/Li). LSV methodology for ESW determination is described for Li|SPE|carbon cells at 25 °C with the oxidation current threshold of 1.5 μA. No ionic conductivity, cycling, or other performance metrics were reported specifically for POFHDODA alone; therefore other fields are left null.

Figure 3b states: "the extracted ESW of PIL, POFHDODA, PVEC, P(IL-VEC), P(IL-OFHDODA) and P(OFHDODA-VEC) is 5.51, 5.13, 4.35, 4.39, 5.31, and 5.12 V (vs. Li+/Li), respectively." Thus PVEC has ESW = 4.35 V vs Li+/Li.

“To demonstrate the potential of PVCA as a polymer electrolyte component of high-energy-density LMBs, we selected PVFH… as the polymer matrix.”
“The PVFH-PVCA electrolyte developed in this study can generate a stable SEI on the Li-metal anode… Simultaneously, PVCA participates in the formation of the cathode-electrolyte interphase (CEI).”
“PVCA activates the dissociation of Li salts, leading to a greater number of mobile Li+.”
“As shown in Fig. 2e, PVCA has the lowest LUMO energy level and a relatively high HOMO energy level, indicating its ability to preferentially participate in the formation of SEI and CEI.”
“In the Li 1s spectrum of PVFH-PVCA, … two peaks … assigned to N-containing species, LixN and Li-N-C, originating from PVCA.”

PVFH used as the polymer matrix/electrolyte host appears as the PVFH-only gel control (PVLE). The PVFH-PVCA gel showed higher ionic conductivity at 25 °C (2.04×10^-3 S cm^-1) than PVLE (0.94×10^-3 S cm^-1; Fig. 3b). The Li+ transference number of PVLE was 0.31 (Fig. 3c and Supplementary Fig. S15). LSV showed PVLE oxidation onset ~4.7 V vs Li+/Li, similar to CLE, whereas PVFH-PVCA was ~5.3 V (Fig. 3a). Mechanical properties for the PVFH-only gel (PVLE): fracture toughness 3.24 MPa and elastic modulus 0.43 MPa (Fig. 2c); AFM Young's modulus 26.5 MPa (Fig. 2d and Supplementary Fig. S14). In Li|Cu, PVLE led to loose porous Li deposits with thickness 49.7 μm vs theoretical 19.4 μm (Fig. 4a,d). In Li||Li at 8 mA cm^-2, PVLE showed sustained polarization increase, while PVFH-PVCA cycled >4500 h (Fig. 5a). These data directly pertain to the PVFH-based electrolyte (PVLE) and justify listing PVFH as a Li+ conducting battery electrolyte with ionic conductivity 0.94×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 25 °C.

PVFH-PVCA is explicitly presented as a quasi-solid polymer electrolyte for Li-metal batteries: “Design of PVFH-PVCA polymer electrolytes for high-performance Li-metal batteries.” The electrolyte coordinates and transports Li+: “the C-O-C and C=O groups coordinate with Li+, … the polar -CN functional groups in the polymer chain can also interact with Li+ providing a fast Li+ diffusion channel.” Performance highlights (context for electrolyte application): ionic conductivity 2.04×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 25 °C; Li+ transference number 0.61; higher oxidation potential (~5.3 V vs Li+/Li) than controls; Li||Li symmetric cells stably cycled >4500 h at 8 mA cm^-2 with ~32 mV overpotential at 10 mA cm^-2; full cells LMNCO|PVFH-PVCA|Li capacity retention 84.8% after 400 cycles (2.1–4.6 V, 1C) and 78.8% after 340 cycles (2.1–4.8 V, 1C); LCO|PVFH-PVCA|Li retention 89.2% after 1500 cycles (1C, 3.0–4.5 V); LFP|PVFH-PVCA|Li 86.4% after 1940 cycles (0.5C, 2.6–3.8 V); NC95|PVFH-PVCA|Li 78.7% after 200 cycles (1C, 2.7–4.3 V). These statements justify the scenario as a battery electrolyte and the target species as Li+; other requested fields (capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration) are not applicable in the reported context.

PVLE is defined as PVFH soaked in the liquid electrolyte and used as a control sample. Electrochemical/mechanical performance for PVLE reported in the text includes: (1) Oxidative stability by LSV: the oxidation potential of PVLE is ~4.7 V vs Li+/Li (text: “the oxidation potentials of PVLE and CLE are similar at −4.7 V vs. Li+/Li”). (2) Ionic conductivity at 25 °C: 0.94 × 10^−3 S/cm (Fig. 3b: “PVFH-PVCA … 2.04×10^−3 S cm^−1 … PVLE (0.94×10^−3 S cm^−1)”). (3) Li+ transference number: 0.31 (Fig. 3c: “PVFH-PVCA … 0.61 … CLE (0.34) and PVLE (0.31)”). (4) Li plating morphology in Li|PVLE|Cu: loose/porous Li deposit; thickness 49.7 μm vs theoretical 19.4 μm (Fig. 4a,d: “highly loose and porous deposition … thickness of 49.7 μm, far exceeding the theoretical … 19.4 μm”). (5) Symmetric Li||Li at 8 mA cm^−2, 4 mAh cm^−2: PVLE shows sustained increase in polarization (Fig. 5a: “the voltage curve of the cell with the PVLE showed a sustained increase in polarization”). (6) Raman speciation in PVLE: FA 40.9%, LIP 36.3%, AC 22.8% (Supplementary Fig. S12: “the FA, LIP, and AC contents of PVLE are 40.9, 36.3, and 22.8%”). (7) LMNCO|PVLE|Li cell cycling: capacity retention 33.9% after 400 cycles at 2.1–4.6 V and 1C; damaged after 100 cycles at 2.1–4.8 V (Fig. 6a,c: “LMNCO|PVLE|Li … 33.9% after 400 cycles …; … damaged after only … 100 cycles”). (8) Morphology/mechanics of PVLE membrane: porous surface, thickness 47 μm (Supp. Fig. S7–S8); elastic modulus 0.43 MPa; fracture toughness 3.24 MPa; AFM Young’s modulus 26.5 MPa (Fig. 2c,d: “PVFH-PVCA … 4.72 MPa than PVLE (0.43 MPa); … PVLE (3.24 MPa) … AFM … PVLE (26.5 MPa)”). These data confirm PVLE’s role and measured performance as a Li-metal battery gel electrolyte control.

WSGPE is used as the electrolyte in Li metal batteries. Reported properties directly tied to WSGPE include: electrochemical window up to 5.05 V, Li+ transference number 0.83, and ionic conductivity 4.40e-4 S/cm at RT. Coin cells: Li|WSGPE|NCM811 (mass loading 1.6 mg/cm2) deliver 154.8 mAh/g with 92.5% retention after 300 cycles (188 mA/g, 3–4.3 V); at 376 mA/g, 130.4 mAh/g after 200 cycles with 90.8% retention; at 4.5 V cutoff, 120.8 mAh/g at 188 mA/g after 300 cycles. Wide-temperature operation: -30 to 80 °C; capacities 160.1, 147.9, 121.4 mAh/g at -10, -20, -30 °C; at -20 °C, 131.2 mAh/g with 97.1% retention after 200 cycles (37.6 mA/g); at 80 °C, 172.2 mAh/g with 94.0% retention after 100 cycles (188 mA/g). Self-discharge: fully charged WSGPE cell maintains ~4.2 V after 15 days. Li|WSGPE|Li symmetric cells: stable plating/stripping ≥1500 h at 0.5 mA/cm2 and 0.5 mAh/cm2 with ~60 mV overpotential; ~200 mV at 1 mA/cm2 without soft short. Pouch cells: a Li|WSGPE|NCM811 pouch cell achieves a specific energy of 490.8 Wh/kg; a large-format 5.4 Ah pouch cell shows specific energy 451 Wh/kg (table lists 5.124 Ah, 3.8 V, 43.16 g → 451 Wh/kg). These data demonstrate WSGPE as a battery electrolyte enabling high specific energy and wide-temperature performance.

FEP-free GPE (prepared by replacing FEP in WSGPE with an equal amount of FEC) was tested as an electrolyte. In Li symmetric cells at -20 °C, the exchange current density j0 was 0.16 mA/cm^2 (from Tafel plots). Under galvanostatic cycling at 0.5 mA/cm^2 with an area capacity of 0.5 mAh/cm^2, Li|FEP-free GPE|Li cells short-circuited at 293 h. The control-sample EIS evolution indicated it could not form stable interfacial layers.

PTFMA-free GPE (control electrolyte replacing TFMA with PEGDA) was evaluated in Li metal battery tests. It showed slower interfacial Li+ transfer than WSGPE with an exchange current density j0 = 0.21 mA cm^-2 at -20 °C (Supplementary Fig. 24). In Li|electrolyte|Li symmetric cells at 0.5 mA cm^-2 and 0.5 mAh cm^-2, the PTFMA-free GPE cell short-circuited at 236 h (Supplementary Fig. 31a–c). EIS evolution indicated it was unable to form a stable interfacial layer (Supplementary Fig. 20d). No specific discharge capacity data or regeneration procedures were reported for PTFMA-free GPE; hence capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are not extracted.

Like PEO, PPC is immiscible with PVDF-HFP, resulting in a phase-separated PPC-LPIFD... As a result, the PPC-LPIFD has the lowest CCD (0.3 mA cm^-2) and CE (<90%) (Supplementary Fig. 45) among these polymer blends.

For PEO-LPIFD in Li metal batteries: (1) Critical current density: "For PEO-LPIFD, short-circuiting was quickly observed at 0.6 mA cm^-2" (Supplementary Fig. 35a). (2) Coulombic efficiency: "the CE of PEO-LPIFD at a low current density of 0.3 mA cm^-2 can quickly reach 98% ... however, phase separation leads to a quick short circuit" (Fig. 4e and Supplementary Fig. 42a). Additional related property: Li-ion transference number t_Li+ = 0.38 for PEO-LPIFD (Supplementary Fig. 26a). The text also notes PEO-LPIFD is phase-separated, contributing to non-uniform Li deposition and dendrite growth.

PMMA-LPIFD is a single-phase LPIFD polymer electrolyte used for Li-metal plating/stripping tests: “PMMA-LPIFD and PTFEP-LPIFD exhibit a flat and uniform surface … as expected from the miscibility of the polymers.” The Li-ion transference number is reported: “t_Li+ … 0.53 for PMMA-LPIFD (Supplementary Fig. 26b).” SEI is organic-rich: “the SEI formed in PMMA-LPIFD … exhibits a considerably higher ratio of organic to inorganic components…” Dendrite/short-circuit behavior: “PMMA is fully miscible with PVDF-HFP … However, … PMMA forms an organic-rich SEI … Therefore, short-circuiting is observed when the current density reaches 1.4 mA cm^−2 (Supplementary Fig. 35b).” Coulombic efficiency in Li||Cu: “the single-phase PMMA-LPIFD … the Li CE could reach only 95% at the same current of 0.3 mA cm^−2 after 300 cycles … When the current density was increased to 0.5 mA cm^−2, the cell short-circuited within 100 cycles and the Li CE was further reduced.” Deposition morphology: “For PMMA-LPIFD, the morphology of deposited lithium exhibits a whiskery structure … a thickness of 9.0 μm with a loosely packed structure.”

PTFEP-LPIFD is used as a solid polymer electrolyte enabling high-voltage Li||NMC811 full cells. With a 4.6 V cutoff, Li|PTFEP-LPIFD|NMC811 delivered reversible specific capacities of 186 and 101.2 mAh g^-1 at 1C and 2C, respectively (Fig. 6f). It shows anodic stability to 5.4 V after passivation (Fig. 6a) and sustained cycling (>400 cycles, 80% retention) with average CE 99.95% at 0.5C between 2.8–4.5 V (Fig. 6c). As Li-metal metrics, Li||Cu CE ≈99.1% at 0.5 mA cm^-2 after 300 cycles (Fig. 4e) and CCD = 3.7 mA cm^-2 in Li||Li (Supplementary Fig. 35c). Fields like selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are not applicable to this battery electrolyte context.

PEO-LPIFD showed short-circuiting at 0.6 mA cm^-2 (CCD) in Li||Li cells: “For PEO-LPIFD, short-circuiting was quickly observed at 0.6 mAcm^-2 (Supplementary Fig. 35a).” Its Li|Cu CE reached 98% at 0.3 mA cm^-2 but then quickly short-circuited: “the CE of PEO-LPIFD at a low current density of 0.3 mAcm^-2 can quickly reach 98%… However, phase separation leads to a quick short circuit (Supplementary Fig. 42a).” The Li-ion transference number for PEO-LPIFD is 0.38 (Supplementary Fig. 26a).

PMMA-LPIFD-specific results: "PMMA is fully miscible with PVDF-HFP and forms a single-phase LPIFD... short-circuiting is observed when the current density reaches 1.4 mA cm^-2 (Supplementary Fig. 35b)." and "the single-phase PMMA-LPIFD shows a much longer cycling stability, but the Li CE could reach only 95% at the same current of 0.3 mA cm^-2 after 300 cycles (Fig. 4e, pink). When the current density was increased to 0.5 mA cm^-2, the cell short-circuited within 100 cycles and the Li CE was further reduced (Supplementary Fig. 43)."

PPC-LPIFD is immiscible with PVDF-HFP and forms a phase-separated electrolyte; it shows the lowest critical current density and Coulombic efficiency among tested LPIFDs: "the PPC-LPIFD has the lowest CCD (0.3 mA cm^-2) and CE (<90%)" (Fig. 5j, Supplementary Fig. 45).

“PVSF is miscible with PVDF-HFP. An almost inorganic structure contributes to an organic-less SEI. Therefore, PVSF achieves both a higher CE (99%) and a higher CCD (2.4 mAcm−2) (Supplementary Fig. 46).”

P1a is only mentioned as one of the HFP–iBVE copolymers obtained in the synthetic scope ("As exemplified by HFP-iBVE copolymerization, P1a to P1f ... of predetermined molecular weights could be attained..."). The text provides no application testing or performance metrics (capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration) specific to P1a.

P1a–P1f are HFP–iBVE copolymers prepared under the described photocatalytic conditions: “As exemplified by HFP-iBVE copolymerization, P1a to P1f (entries S1–S6 in Supplementary Table 20) of predetermined molecular weights could be attained by selecting appropriate molar ratios of [HFP]:[iBVE]:[CTA].” Specific numerical performance for P1b is not provided in the main text; only P1d is explicitly reported (D = 1.18). General scope conditions for the library are given in Fig. 6 (PC9, PMDETA, visible light, ambient conditions), but no P1b-specific values are stated.

The provided text mentions a series of HFP–iBVE copolymers "P1a to P1f (entries S1–S6 in Supplementary Table 20)" and specifically reports data for P1d (e.g., "When a PC loading of 5 ppm was examined ... P1d was afforded with D = 1.18") and a condition yielding Mn = 233.8 kDa (D = 1.23), but it does not provide any application-related performance metrics for sample P1c. No catalysis/adsorption/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration data specific to P1c appear in the excerpt.

P1d is only reported as an HFP–iBVE copolymer sample prepared at 5 ppm PC9 loading with D = 1.18 (“When a PC loading of 5 ppm was examined in the HFP-iBVE copolymerization, P1d was afforded with D = 1.18.”). No application testing (e.g., catalysis, adsorption/separation, ion exchange) or related performance metrics were provided for P1d.

The provided text mentions the P1 series (P1a–P1f) generally and gives specific data only for P1d (D = 1.18 at 5 ppm PC9) and an unnamed high-Mn sample; no application/performance metrics specific to P1e are reported in the excerpt, and referenced details are in Supplementary Tables not provided.

As exemplified by HFP–iBVE copolymerization, P1a to P1f (entries S1–S6 in Supplementary Table 20) of predetermined molecular weights could be attained by selecting appropriate molar ratios of [HFP]:[iBVE]:[CTA]. Compared to previous RDRPs, this method yielded fluoropolymers of clearly improved molecular weights (above 40 kDa) without losing control over dispersities (D = 1.08–1.34).

P2a is identified only as the HFP–BVE copolymer: “In a typical synthesis of the HFP-BVE copolymer (P2a) …” under Methods. The provided text and figure captions (e.g., Fig. 6 scope notes) do not report any specific performance metrics (Mn, dispersity, selectivity, capacities, regeneration) for P2a in the main text, and image data are not readable here. Therefore no application/performance values specific to P2a can be extracted.

P3a is only referenced implicitly within the grouped mention of vinyl-ether-based fluoropolymers: “ethers with different alkyls (P1a-P1f, P2a-P6a)” and in Fig. 6a-c, where general conditions are given ([CTA]:[PMDETA] = 1:2, [PC9] = 100 ppm, DEC, 450 nm LED, ambient pressure, 25 °C, 3–36 h; [fluoroalkene]:[comonomer] = 2:1 for vinyl ether sets). No individual conversion, selectivity, Mn, or D values for P3a are reported in the provided text; specific data appear to be in figures/supplementary tables not included here.

The provided text mentions P2a–P6a as part of a library of vinyl ether copolymers (including P4a) but does not report any P4a-specific application or performance metrics (e.g., conversion, selectivity, capacity, breakthrough, regeneration). Only general statements and figure references are given without P4a-specific numerical data.

The main text mentions sample series including P2a–P6a (which includes P5a) only in a generic scope statement: “ethers with different alkyls (P1a-P1f, P2a-P6a)… could be connected to copolymers as side groups,” with detailed results in Supplementary Tables (not provided here). No P5a-specific performance metrics (e.g., conversion, selectivity, Mn, D) are reported in the provided text, and figure panels (e.g., Fig. 6a) are images without extracted numeric values. Therefore, no application/performance data specific to P5a can be reliably extracted from the given content.

PFPE1000-diol is evaluated as a Li-ion battery electrolyte with LiTFSI: conductivity of PFPE-diols was measured at 30 °C vs r (Fig. 4A), but no specific numeric value for PFPE1000-diol is reported. Safety metrics for PFPE1000-diol are provided: Td(5%) = 210 °C, no sustained burning observed, and no flash point detected within 25–200 °C (Fig. 2B). The study addresses Li+ transport and salt solvation in PFPE electrolytes (LiTFSI added; target cation Li+), but detailed electrochemical cell cycling is only shown for PFPE1000-DMC, not the diol.

PFPE1400-diol is explicitly listed and described as an electrolyte (Fig. 1B: “PFPE-diol and PFPE-DMC electrolytes…”; table includes PFPE1400-diol). Ionic conductivities for PFPE1400-diol with LiTFSI at 30°C are shown in Fig. 4A (“Ionic conductivities of PFPE1000-diol (•), PFPE1400-diol (green ■), … with LiTFSI at 30°C as a function of r”), confirming its use as a Li-ion battery electrolyte. The text/figures do not provide specific numeric conductivity values for PFPE1400-diol (only plotted curves), nor any regeneration metrics; therefore capacity/selectivity/breakthrough/regeneration are set to null.
